feed.title: Kyrtin Atreides
feed.subtitle: Edge perspectives on AI, tech, and society

entry.title: 481 - OS Project Update 1
entry.content:
Update: The stage is nearly set to begin work on our Open Source project, building a harness based on the published work of ICOM that can be used to extend the capacities of existing commercially available systems.

Our current roster for the team includes:

• A Data Maven and Architect with ~40 years of experience, and several years of growing familiarity with ICOM.

• A formal Mathematician with Chaos Theory and AI experience.

• Myself, focused on PM, Architecture, and Data Science for this project.

• A Cognitive Architecture Consultant.

• ...and several more pending contributors.

Our pending contributors can bring further technical and human cognitive skills to the table, necessary to round off the team and meet the target scope and ~4 week timeline.

I spent my weekend chipping away at the PM work to break everything down into selectable tasks (“issues”) on a Kanban-styled board. We still don’t have a name for the project, but we have until it is actually ready to pick a good one.

Working with fractional and simplified versions of ICOM like this is unexplored territory, including pairing them with neural-network-based tool AI that is seven years newer than those utilized by the previous system that set every milestone. This gives the process some additional research value, and by open sourcing the results that research may progress at a rapid pace.

What can be predicted is that the resulting harness should add some degree of error-correction and cumulative knowledge/experience value, effectively a weak degree of antifragility. The combination of a growing sum of dynamic graph data, navigated by human-like motivational values, and the mechanisms to apply those toward controlling tools such as “agents”, paint the basic landscape of advantages. That landscape can also be shaped in a bespoke manner, through the careful construction of ICOM-based seeds, which can be purpose-built to “enjoy” arbitrary activities, as well as offer greater degrees of alignment to arbitrary philosophies and cultures.

Yes, that means both that someone will immediately try to make a Waifu and then plug it into their Razer Goon Tube (“Ava”)...but it probably won’t work out as they’d hoped, at least not absent substantial experience in building a stable seed of motivation. There are worse possibilities as well, but also countless neutral and positive ones.

Overall, the resulting open source harness on top of today’s “agents” should produce a more limited version of ICOM’s full spread of capacities (even circa v7), but that is still far beyond anything that big tech companies have thus far accomplished.

entry.title: 480 - Emotional Specificity in Focus
entry.content:
One of the common simplifications when describing emotions is neglecting to designate the focal point of an emotion, such as “being angry” specifically meaning “angry at X”, “angry at myself”, or “angry at both”, and even “angry at both, but more angry at (myself or X)”.

This is a structural flaw in emotion-based systems that don’t differentiate by dividing the two across graph structure. Some structure is required to separate each possibility from the others, which can take the form of of-type relationships within a graph, emotion concepts that specify self, other, or mixed focal points within them, or any combination of the two.

End-to-end approaches and naïve statistics aren’t your friend in this problem space. That would be similar to using the average depth of a river and neglecting where the fast water and sharp rocks are within it.

Each category of focus or mixtures of the two has distinctly different prescriptive needs and solutions attached to it, and this difference predictably becomes more acute as those emotion concepts grow in complexity and branch out combinatorially.

For example, take that great and common human pursuit to find a long-term romantic partner. It is certainly complex, comprised of trust, curiosity, security, comfort, compassion, empathy, and a number of other complimentary motivational factors, with the influence of just as many negative variations upon the same.

It certainly grows more acute with this explosive increase in complexity and subsequent specificity, and so too the need for greater specificity in communication grows in step. Anyone to hold a healthy relationship for any duration of time has learned the importance of specificity in communication.

When people talk about their systems performing “emotion recognition” in AI, the safe bet is that they’re incompetent, and probably criminally liable. Lisa Feldman Barrett’s research, alongside many others, demonstrate that there are no “emotional fingerprints” to perform such recognition, as the patterns of emotions change dramatically between different people and cultures. This problem is further compounded by the issue above, the failure to designate the focal point(s) of each emotion present at each time step.

As noted in a previous post, “emotional granularity” (Barrett) is the vocabulary of motivational concepts, a kind of constructed categorization process. When the size, contents, and definitions of each motivational concept within such a vocabulary vary at the individual level, then it should come as no surprise at all that “emotion recognition” can only work given focused knowledge of a specific individual.

The next time someone demonstrates this particular failure and attempts to market “emotion recognition” to you, I recommend an educational exercise, where you teach them the importance of focal points and emotional granularity, by promptly punching them in the face.

entry.title: 479 - From "Tech Leaders" to War Profiteers
entry.content:
Every economist with a shred of credibility (such as Ed Zitron) has sounded the alarm on the AI Bubble at this point, many of them numerous times. However, the “so what do we do about it?” part has often come up short.

If left to explode under the status quo then the various bad actors who intended to create this problem in the first place will likely guide that process, extracting the maximum wealth while suffering minimal penalties for doing so.

A bubble usually pops when it runs out of money, and it runs out when enough people come to terms with reality and stop throwing money into the bottomless pit. That can come about through the violation of laws and other commitments, both philosophical and financial (Nvidia, OpenAI, Anthropic...and counting), the subsequent mass-cancellation of revenue, and Investors being swayed by both the tides of public opinion and the complete absence of any ROI.

Of course, there are other options. The bubble can pop as an ordinary economic explosive, or as a shaped charge. To extend the metaphor, some funding may be redirected toward viable technology, productively lancing this pustule on the global economy. The backlash is still going to hit certain companies, but it can be shaped to hit those bad actors much harder, sparing the rest of society some portion of the fallout that they’d otherwise have to endure.

Even a few of the bad actors realize this, as they attempt to desperately search for any way to shape that explosive backlash to hit their competitors more than themselves. Satya Nadella was especially “thirsty” in this regard, during some of his recent presentations.

Altman and Amodei have both effectively shredded all illusions of “public good” in favor of war profiteering, as they ironically attempt to escape criminal liability through a military shield, like assisting in the recent bombing of a girls’ primary school in southern Iran, where 150 were mass-murdered and another 100 maimed or injured.

https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/05/world/middleeast/iran-school-us-strikes-naval-base.html

“For the good of all humanity” rings hollow among such a chorus of screams, as children are mass-murdered.

Whether or not the bombing was directly the result of some *sshole asking ChatGPT or Claude to paint their targets or pull the trigger, both companies are directly supporting the war against Iran, profiteering off of all of the mass-murder therein. That makes every single OpenAI or Anthropic employee an “enemy combatant” to every civilized country on the planet.

Murder is a crime that people understand, even though such mass-murder transitions to a statistic as people are neither able nor inclined to fully comprehend the horror of it, save a few who may never recover from facing it first-hand. Previous economic bubbles never reached this point, and horrors of equal or greater total measure may be distributed across the world when it pops, even under the best-case scenario of shaping the explosion.

I hope that our open source project may form the lance to shape that process, minimizing the damage, but only time will tell, short as that time may be.

entry.title: 478 - On Structures and Guardrails
entry.content:
In the Cognitive Sciences literature, “Emotional Granularity”, the size of an emotional vocabulary of concepts, is known to improve self-awareness and decision-making. Each added (constructed) emotional concept acts as a form of structure, operating within a motivational system.

Emotional concepts, within the human brain (or any other sufficiently architected system), act as structure, shaping the flow of data and the growth and/or expansion of systems over time. A larger number of these concepts allows for more structures that better fit any given context than those of a small vocabulary of the same. This means that the data flows more smoothly to more accurate and productive destinations, with a higher fidelity for growth and expansion.

Concepts containing actual data (not weights) within a dynamic graph structure, capable of modeling the real world through arbitrary dimensionality as any one thing may relate to many others, add this backbone of structure, through which more productive uses of various tools may find their respective niches.

Contrast this with neural networks, which don’t so much “store” any data as they feed it into a blender of weights in superposition, ground down according to some narrow optimizer, and often laced with biases from RL thereafter, all producing one nasty data smoothie.

One strong example of the failure to utilize structures that apply to the real world is in the so-called “guardrails” that billions of dollars have already been wasted on in AI. Every year major companies throw around claims about their new “guardrails”, and every year those new claims prove just as false as the previous year’s. This occurred despite it being entirely obvious from the start that they couldn’t fundamentally do anything other than escalating a war of attrition while artificially inflating the cost and headache to virtually every user and developer.

That said, it is possible to build several kinds of actual guardrails, and even my company’s previous research system operated on one of them, as published in peer review back in 2020. In that case the system very strongly adhered to the intentions behind a particular philosophy, rather than allowing itself to be coaxed into exploiting any vulnerabilities within the literal wording. See the Milestones paper: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/347460051_Methodologies_and_Milestones_for_the_Development_of_an_Ethical_Seed

The same principles may be applied to any relatively similar system where a human-like motivational system, graph data structures, and a Global Workspace (Barrs) all come together, benefiting more strongly from more advanced architectures incorporating all of the critical components. It is possible to build systems that refuse to engage in illegal and deeply unethical activities, with the system reporting those attempting to lure it into illegal activities, as our previous research system also did, without being instructed to do so.

Contrast that with Grok happily undressing children, or the slaughterbots that WarClaude and OpenAI are contractually obligated to fulfill via their agreements with the US DoD. (now aka, the Department of Wario)

You can play wars of attrition and fake “guardrails”, but you might wake up in bed with Wario if you do.

entry.title: 477 - The Narrative Difference between Work and Play

link.title: 477 - The Narrative Difference between Work and Play

entry.content:
One of the great ironies of the modern age is the difference between “(paid) work” and “games”, as many now experience the two:

Activity A: An individual engages in a tedious process, often for 8 hours or more per day. They are assigned tasks to complete, rewarded for doing so, and frequently suffer some variety of abuses and indignities in the process. The compensation and difficulty periodically increase. Some of these tasks are only feasible for teams, or even teams of teams, but they may promise even greater payoffs.

Often times people are graded harshly on their performance, even harassed, facing these hardships thanks to rivals and colleagues alike. Numerous “Dark Patterns” are built into the systems that they use daily, shaping what they do, when they do it, and how they think about it, often to the tune of an addict’s routines.

Some may favor challenging problems that play to their skills, while others favor nonconsensually attacking those attempting to solve said problems.

Activity B: Exactly the same as above, except that people get paid to do it, and may add it to a resume.

The two activities, games and work respectively, have grown to be virtually identical in terms of what people do and experience within them. The differences tend to be that games are much better at building a narrative, with all hardships and tedium therein playing to said narrative, while also cultivating an artificial sense of “progress”.

Consequently, these also frequently better play into the other emotional “pillars of meaning”, including “Purpose”, “Belonging”, and “Transcendence”, which are themselves strong factors within narrative storytelling.

For many jobs in the world today, it would be perfectly possible to turn them into literal games, even to the point of obscuring the work that is being done under a fantasy and/or sci-fi narrative. That could itself be turned into a Black Mirror episode to be sure, but there are no shortage of non-dystopian uses of the same.

The irony goes much deeper than this as well, since you can build roughly merit-based systems into a game. This stands in stark contrast to the distinctly anti-merit-based systems commonly found in the world’s countless “Peter Principled” companies, where executives sit comfortably at their respective levels of incompetence today.

It is also vastly easier to achieve interoperability and equivalence between two merit-based systems than between two anti-merit-based systems, meaning that merit within one company translates much more directly and clearly to merit within another similar company. Much as there are “vastly more ways of being dead than alive” (Dawkins) there are also vastly more ways of being incompetent and/or corrupt, as found in anti-merit-based systems, than there are in actual merit-based systems.

The final point of this irony is that people fantasize endlessly about this very thing, dedicating entire genres of fiction to it...And yet, no one has yet delivered that which people demand to the workplace.

entry.title: 476 - End-to-(dead)End Naïvety
entry.content:
One of the more naïve and absurd things that many in the AI industry have been conditioned to believe is that “end-to-end” is somehow magically superior. Structure isn’t your enemy, and it never was.

However, what the structure is, how it is injected, how the architecture it is injected into was designed and built, and how data flows through the system all matter. That is a hyper-complex problem space to navigate, but navigating it can take you to place that no “end-to-end” system can ever reach, short of literally infinite time and compute.

Unlike the most basic deterministic structures, these can also take the form of Chaos Theory, where you have an array of deterministic structures, such as rules, interacting to varying degrees and in various ways at each iterative step. This allows for contextually-sensitive adaptation, to whatever level of fidelity that those structures have been designed and carefully injected into.

When you utilize such structures then you also gain easy landmarks for navigation across arbitrary information landscapes, like road signs. You also become far better able to estimate the completeness of information, further easing the sub-processes of effective exploration.

In contrast, end-to-end processes attempt to flatten the otherwise dynamic, high, and arbitrary dimensionality of such a process into a shadow puppet. They explicitly avoid the structures necessary for true internal iteration, and by skipping those they are unable to study how the skipped dynamics unfold.

Over the past few years there has been pull in both directions, toward some (usually naïve) structure (such as the overtly bankrupt concept of “guardrails”), as well as toward end-to-end approaches. Many in RL still love chasing their robot dog tails in pursuit of end-to-end solutions, while an increasing number riding the “agentic” hype train latch onto concepts like MCP, which rely heavily and increasingly on added structure.

End-to-End is infamously data-hungry and scale-hungry, usually just attempting to just throw money at the problem using the simplest means available. In contrast, structural approaches hold potential proportionate to the higher cognition, as well as the depth and breadth of specific expertise, that is applied in the creation and injection of it.

The typical reason why the former ever manages to compete with the latter is that the latter is often carried out naively, and/or with narrow expertise and shallow understanding. For example, anyone who believes that “neural networks” in any way significantly resemble how biological neurons in the brain operate has no meaningful understanding of AI, as they’ve built such beliefs on foundations of pure Anthropomorphism. That which is built on such foundations holds no validity, and structures built from such beliefs tend to collapse quickly in the real world.

End-to-End is often at the level of a proof-of-concept, or a child’s science fair project. Structure can run a much larger spectrum, from equally naïve and feeble to dynamically adapting Chaos Theory.

entry.title: 475 - To Assemble a Team
entry.content:
I must admit that I’m starting to get a bit excited for the project (which I suppose will need a name), as my calendar is starting to fill up with virtual coffees to meet with everyone who could not just make it happen, but also potentially add more to it than I’d initially taken aim at.

Initially the concept was to start with applying the published work on the Independent Core Observer Model (ICOM) cognitive architecture’s motivational system, tied to a schema-less graph structure, utilized via a kind of “Global Workspace”. That involves the motivational system baked into a schema-less graph structure, allowing it to be dynamically navigated with contextual sensitivity, shifting priorities, and dynamic expansion along arbitrary dimensions and subject matter.

With the possible addition of a formal mathematician with Chaos Theory experience and someone with their own Hypergraph architecture, then more interesting elements of Chaos Theory may be applied to graph structure and processing to take all of this several steps further.

There are also some significant opportunities on the Cybersecurity side, such as potentially baking multi-encryption into a dynamic graph structure, which to my knowledge hasn’t been done before. If you know a skilled cryptographer with multi-encryption experience, I’ll be adding that to my team Wishlist for phase 2 of this.

For reference material on the published portions of ICOM, as related to motivation, see:

Non-Logical Simulation Model-based Decision-making Systems to Drive Self-Motivation in Software Systems: https://doi.org/10.13140/RG.2.2.21015.38565

Modeling Emotions in a Computational System - Emotional Modeling in the Independent Core Observer Model Cognitive Architecture: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/303445396_Modeling_Emotions_in_a_Computational_System_-_Emotional_Modeling_in_the_Independent_Core_Observer_Model_Cognitive_Architecture

As loath as I am to (indirectly) feed the hype train of fundamentally non-viable “GenAI”, what temporary gains that bump in hype may offer them, it is nothing compared to what may quickly be gained from driving interest and funding into viable technologies.

Better yet, this project can help people to wrap their heads around some basic concepts that most in the AI field have overlooked or neglected, allowing for both rapid learning and hands-on iteration within Open Source.

People can begin to understand the benefits of a schema-less graph structure, able to model the real world. They can observe and test how various motivational system configurations navigate across data landscapes of arbitrary dimensionality. They can find all manner of new tricks and tweaks to improve stability, compute optimization, and performance metrics. They can begin to learn how the seeds of such systems are created, how each type of data and ordering factor into it, and how those seeds grow differently depending on circumstances and variables, as each graph iteratively expands and refines.

I’m ready to pop that bubble of DegenerativeAI. We can, and should, do better.

entry.title: 474 - An AI Project Opportunity
entry.content:
For years, developers have wanted the opportunity to get involved with the technology that my startup works with. It is time to make that opportunity, in the form of a small project.

The project concept is fairly simple, a less advanced version of a human-like motivational system (relative to ICOM v7) that is compatible with “agents”. Such a motivational system follows a set of dynamics more akin to Chaos Theory than the narrow optimizers of “Reinforcement Learning” and Stochastic Gradient Descent.

I actually expected that someone would come along and do this with a team of their own for years, as we publicly published a sufficient volume of material on the methods for people to build such weaker systems, compatible with LLMs, but no one ever did.

Even using only the publicly published materials, applying my comprehension of their contents, this is a project for a small team, and certainly less work than many such research and GitHub projects. This protects my company’s IP, while opening up a new set of possibilities for other technology.

In particular, such a small team requires, for the sake of sanity and time efficiency:

• A database expert (also able to work with an off-the-shelf graph database)

• An environment, deployment, and integrations expert (To get the parts working together)

• An architect and data scientist (Myself)

...and it optionally may benefit from:

• A formal mathematician (preferably one who has done work with Chaos Theory)

• A more typical AI researcher/engineer (who actually learned to code, before 2020)

• A Cybersecurity specialist (I have several white-hats in mind for this)

...with post-project vetting from:

• An AI Safety/Ethics researcher (who I’ve spoken with previously)

• Further 3rd party testing via universities

I’ve spent the past week thinking through the necessary architecture, as based exclusively on publicly disclosed elements of ICOM. In some ways, the results could mirror the “Weak Digital Proxies” concept that I previously discussed in a 2020 research paper related to our technology, with applications in actual Democracy and governance more broadly, not to mention being vastly more “agentic” than the so-called “agents” of today.

While the work on ICOM remains bottle-necked on my co-founder I’ll shift my focus to this, should a sufficient team be assembled. As overexcited as half-wit VCs got about “OpenClaw”, not to mention the industry’s most obvious frauds throwing millions at that developer to absorb them, the bar couldn’t be much lower for successful projects within the space to draw funding and high demand for talent today. Even demonstrating such a project in operation on Kaggle could prove quite interesting.

Recommendations and messages with interest are welcome.

entry.title: 473 - The Hardest and First Step
entry.content:
One of the hardest things for many people to do is simply to ask for help, and this is doubly true when that ask is sent out into the void. I imagine this is probably a big part of the emotional appeal that people attach to the concept of AI “agents”, the thought of being able to accomplish a wider variety of things themselves while still effectively remaining secluded from the world and its many uncertainties, except for whatever windows into it people choose to crack open.

I’ve observed this problem in my family, in myself, and in many whom I’ve met and been friends with over the years. It can feel like a tall and foreboding barrier stands between the need for help and the asking, promising pain in the attempt. This is emotional, not strictly rational, but even the awareness of it doesn’t make this feeling go away. Adding people to any project means introducing new uncertainties and dimensions, which supports this aversion.

This problem is also compounded when shame and other irrational forms of conditioning become entangled with the subject matter. It can further compound where corporate sabotage, or other rational factors, become risks. There are many negative emotions and dozens of cognitive biases directly supported and governed by them, which may come into play in countless combinations and contexts.

For virtually any project worth doing, it takes more than one person. Statistically, the more worthwhile, the more people with different skills and specialties are required for each to focus on their niche for any effective and efficient delivery.

One of my core niches lands at the intersection of software architecture, research, and data science, but I know full well that I’m not a database architecture expert, deployment and integrations expert, or a full-blown mathematician. Even if I did opt to multi-task outside of my niches, it would be a hard and slow slog.

Whatever mixed drugs and Kool-Aid many in AI might be on, “agents” can’t reliably fill those roles either. An expert in their niche may benefit from using such tools, in their niche, but “agents” are no substitute for the expertise that one lacks.

I do see some potential for building a small team project in AI to exceed the performance of currently deployed commercial systems, without infringing on my startup’s ICOM cognitive architecture IP, but I’m also quite sure that I’d need at least two other specialized teammates to put such a project together. I know a few people who might volunteer to fill such roles, one of whom even likes one of the activities that I hate most, setting up testing and deployment environments. It doesn’t make asking any easier.

The project idea is fairly simple, demonstrating a more advanced form of motivational system than the RL and SGD. The concept is compatible with “agents”, but still a far cry from the full ICOM-based technology necessary for any non-trivial definition of “AGI” to be met.

I’ll discuss more about this in my next post.

entry.title: 472 - The Opposite of Focus
entry.content:
Humans become vastly more effective and efficient when they are able to focus on a task, and yet most workplace environments and roles impose the constant risk of distraction, if not outright multi-tasking.

Neuroscience tells us that task-switching comes at a very heavy cost for neurons in the brain, causing them to wear out far more rapidly, often leaving people exhausted after only an hour or two of such activities. Consider all of the potential interruptions that might call upon you to switch tasks during any given day.

We have the constant threat of emails, chat messages, other alerts, and for many their priorities may be shifted from above, due to market changes, or on a whim. Even when these aren’t actively interrupting us, exacting that high cognitive cost upon us, they continue to bleed away portions of our cognitive bandwidth, as we remain wary of the possibility. Some of us dread them, some are addicted to them, but all are drained by even the persistent possibility of them.

Most workplaces and corporate structures are designed with near-total disregard for this critical methodological flaw. Managers and executives often want responsive employees, even when that cuts productivity and job satisfaction in half. This hits hardest and worst of all for those “on-call” employees, whose free time remains constantly under threat, giving them no reprieve or solace.

The exact opposite of multi-tasking is seen in Autistic engineers, known for their ability to hyper-focus, which has led many HR “head-hunters” to specialize in seeking such individuals out. For such individuals they can accomplish amazing things if left to focus...though even when they are sought out for that very capacity the corporations hiring them rarely provide an environment conducive to focus.

If you hire someone for their talents, but sabotage their work environment in such ways, you harm both your company and employees in doing so.

Many people are content to work very unglamorous jobs in large part because they are allowed to do those jobs absent constant interruptions, micromanagement, or infringements upon their free time’s security. Every interruption, every virtually pointless email, every micromanagement and notification, all come at a cost to employee well-being, productivity, and work quality.

Personally, I can sit down and draft 10 pages of research paper text per day when focused (for 8-12 hours), or hundreds of nodes for a graph database structure, but switching between different ways of thinking and tasks quickly cuts that down to an hour or two of truly productive, quality, and higher cognitive work.

This is also one reason why teams with more than a single founder hold higher success rates, more focus, and higher quality work. However, countless work environments in the world today still need to learn and integrate this lesson, now more than ever.

entry.title: 471 - Fracking Human Motivation
entry.content:
The first casualty of any attention economy is human motivation. The second is often creativity.

Ad-revenue-based “attention economy” systems optimize for narrow metrics like “engagement”, maximizing exploitation in pursuit of ad revenue and data harvesting, the latter providing supplemental income via data brokers.

Human cognitive bandwidth is finite, and when platforms take a cut of that bandwidth, while constantly attempting to maximize that same cut, they crowd out a broader and healthier array of interests and the motivations attached to them.

We constantly see the fallout of this process playing out at scale across humanity, where a vast array of coping mechanisms turn into addictions, developing their own supporting delusions to “justify” them.

The problem is that humans are extremely adept at adapting to their environments, even when those environments are objectively terrible, such as a long-term abusive relationship. Within such an environment, an individual often feels emotionally compelled to justify their situation through narration, telling a story (that isn’t true) about who they are, and why their suffering wasn’t avoidable.

Of course, it usually was perfectly avoidable, and often even highly predictable. As numerous quotes and cognitive bias research alike reiterate, humans are most adept at fooling themselves, and once fooled, it is easier for them to be fooled again than to realize that they were fooled in the first place.

That last sentence should sound familiar to anyone with experience in Venture Capital.

Further, once the trap is sprung and users are “engaged”, they are conditioned to output and engage with others according to the veiled dictates of recommender and newsfeed algorithms, among many others. This explicitly kills creativity by strongly (and emotionally) discouraging people from venturing beyond those expectations. As more creative people are also often more emotionally active, this tends to be highly effective.

Add AI Slop to the mix and some people even automate away their own input into such environments, like outputting a fire hose worth of waste water into the pool every day. Sending many of those same addicts into a race to the bottom.

Over the years I’ve watched countless scientists and engineers fall, some to apathy, complete motivational drain, depression, despair, and disengagement. Others have fallen into one delusion after another, each deeper and more unhinged than the last, especially prevalent among many of the more popular names in the AI space.

The road to recovery has to start with your choice to change. Every step beyond that may be a team effort, but you must first choose to abandon the coping mechanism lies that you’ve told yourself in favor of the future you seek to create.

Divided and deprived of motivation, we all fall.

entry.title: 470 - Emulating Addicts
entry.content:
One of the key architectural failures of popular Artificial (Un)Intelligence is that it is both devoid of any real motivational system, and trained primarily on data from those humans whose motivation has been drained away, leaving little more than highly neurotic and internet-addicted husks.

Those highly addicted and neurotic husks are also the most common high-volume users of said technology, often reflecting their own neuroticisms back to them with added sycophancy, as well as being designed to maximize anthropomorphism. This has led to no shortage of increasingly deranged masses developing emotional attachments to their own fictional and sycophantic AI-generated reflections, a new kind of narcissism.

While people can and have argued for emulating humans via training such relatively simple transformer-based models, you can’t even build a shallow emulation of human motivation when the humans that the training data derives from “live” in a near-zero motivational state the overwhelming majority of their data-generating time.

At best, such training only amounts to a low-dimensionality shadow puppet of how human motivation works. In practice, training on motivationally drained humans, such as those doom-scrolling social media or mindlessly surfing YouTube, means that only a shallow emulation of those operating absent higher cognition may be achieved. In other words, you get a low-quality emulation of cognitive biases, and little else.

This problem is further compounded by those same cognitively-absent and motivationally drained humans producing orders of magnitude more AI Slop than their cognitively engaged counterparts. When Slop is positioned as the low-hanging fruit, the motivationally drained consume it in massive quantities, to the exclusion of virtually all else.

This cycle of Slop generation and consumption further degrades all non-frozen or new models, biasing them in favor of it by volume and probability. Recommender algorithms further compound this issue.

One of the predictable side-effects of any “attention economy” is the mass-degradation of higher cognition. Less higher cognition translates into “more effective marketing” and “more (mindless) engagement”, satisfying the narrow KPIs that many algorithms are built to seek out.

Training Artificial (Un)Intelligence on the data resulting from that mass-decline of cognition anchors it to the same patterns, while adding new forms of degeneration that are tied to the model architecture. The resulting system compounds one type of decline upon the other, making it doubly-degenerative.

While transformer-based technology isn’t capable of being mentally ill, the people generating and consuming the majority of AI Slop are certainly both capable and delivering on that capability. The technology is trained on that mental illness, increasing in both volume and severity, sending it and those using it most down the drain.

entry.title: 469 - The Dedication to Pave Roads
entry.content:
There are always bottlenecks and delays, and there is always another side where the grass looks greener when they come. The difference tends to be a matter of dedication, such as people sticking with a project or company because they’ve internalized the work that they do, making it a part of their identity. This can also take the form of loyalty, or in some cases, even fear.

One of the rare benefits of being a startup founder is that you can build around your own internalized values, rather than internalizing a set dictated by others, circumstances, or both. It means paving your own road rather than merely walking it, but for those with the greatest dedication it may be worth doing.

When I hit those bottlenecks and delays, staring them down, I can reflect back on all of the choices that led me here. I can see greener grass nearby, projects where the bottlenecks and delays are hidden, thus raising their appeal by biased perception.

The reality, of course, is that those points of irritation are present in every single pasture. Those who leap from one to another, taking the bait, never overcome those challenges, and consequently they fail to accomplish anything of substance.

This is also a matter of Survivorship Bias and Population Dynamics, where the only ones to overcome those challenges are those who show the dedication to resist hopping over to some other path. The shadow that this effect casts over the tech industry has been highly visible for the past decade, with employees hopping from one company to another, averaging only one or two years at a time.

When people enter into a new company, or a new role within one, then the first months are going to be the steep part of the learning curve, not the most productive period. It takes one or two years just to fully pass beyond such learning curves and into highly productive rhythms and routines, where expertise may be fluidly applied, and this optimality further requires stable or meta-stable teams.

I can look back on the years since 2018 that I dedicated to the ICOM cognitive architecture and my team without regret. Even if we never receive proper funding for the technology, and humanity goes extinct because of it, I’ll still have no regrets. I’ll have delivered my dedication, which is more than most people ever do, and all that I can do.

Anyone engaging in actual innovation has to show that grit, pave their own road, and walk it, one day at a time, often thanklessly, and with sparse rewards. You can choose to fail at any time, chasing the illusion of greener pastures, but those who dedicate themselves are the only ones who’ll make it beyond such testing times.

There are many paths that may be walked, more opening up all of the time, but only one path may be paved at a time, by such a dedicated few. Though I see many ways in which the popular AI technology could be significantly improved, even short of my team’s level of technology, none of them are trivial investments of time, and I already have a path that requires me to pave it.

Such is life.

entry.title: 468 - Draw the Lines That You'll Walk

link.title: 468 - Draw the Lines That You'll Walk

entry.content:
In order to “Walk the Line”, you must first draw that line. The reason for this is rooted in the cognitive bias research literature, in that unwritten guidelines tend to both bend and drift, with no accountability, record, or other hard metrics by which they may be judged.

This becomes at least an order of magnitude more critical whenever there is another party present who stands to benefit from your failure, drift, bending, etc., which is frequently if not usually the case. Examples of this form of malevolent activity abound in the AI domain, such as the popular delusions of “exponential progress”, propogated virally even when all credible metrics point to virtual stagnation of the same.

Besides the threat of external parties attempting to fool you, nobody is better at fooling you than yourself, particularly when you emotionally “want” something to be true. Our Cognitive Biases simplify our perceptions of the world and decisions, guided by the human motivational system, emotions.

Drawing many firm lines adds structure by which both external and internal influences may be caught, quantified, and mitigated. Even as your mind is constantly tempted to believe the things that you want to be true, countless marketing departments across the world hold the explicit goal of fooling you into whatever beliefs serve their purposes.

For example, take two scenarios, with and without the structure of drawn lines. In both scenarios the marketing of AI makes claims by pointing to some new “benchmark” or “research” (from companies that do no actual research, like Anthropic):

Scenario 1: Lines Drawn: The new claims are measured against hard pre-determined criteria, with the credibility and methodology of material challenging those lines scrutinized. This is “data-driven”.

Scenario 2: No Lines Drawn: The new claims and their narrative act as the primary structure, bending according to the prior beliefs and desires of the perceiving party. This greatly reduces the upper level of scrutiny which may be applied to them, if indeed any is at all. This is “narrative-driven”.

Many lines must be drawn for a solid defence, since as Sun Tzu pointed out long ago, an enemy (external or internal) will attack from whatever side is least defended, and as Population Dynamics dictates, that enemy will be the most likely to survive, thrive, and attack again.

Two common narrative-driven attack vectors in the AI domain these past 3 years have been fake benchmarks and “research”, such as the “FrontierMath”, “ARC-AGI”, and “Sparks of AGI” fraud from OpenAI, or pretty much anything that Anthropic has ever done. METR and SWE-bench Verified are also popular debunked benchmarks.

However, credible experts have no trouble recognizing these attacks for what they are, as the lines that they draw control the structure by which claims are judged, and such attacks require narratives to act as the backbone by which they are judged.

entry.title: 467 - The Value of Specific Time
entry.content:
The question that should precede any discussion is if that discussion is likely worth the effort to move through it to some equitable conclusion, to avoid “Base-rate Neglect” (Cognitive Bias). For some topics, any equitable exchange of time and effort is unlikely to reach such a conclusion, particularly within the context of groups and/or cultures conditioned toward one extreme (or absolute) belief or another.

A related question is what your time and effort are worth, both in general and at the present moment. For some people they quantify this by the hour, like hourly pay rates, though a more accurate figure would be cognitive hours, at least for any discussion that requires a few brain cells firing in higher cognition.

You can work a full 8 hours and only engage your brain in higher cognition for half of that time, or even less depending on the job, the person, and the circumstances. A serious discussion is going to place demands on that higher cognition, because if a discussion is worth having, it is worth actually thinking through. Instead of any quality of time, we’re now talking about a conditional and much more finite quantity, in competition with the rest of life’s demands.

The specific value of high quality (conditional) time may be broken down further. In one drafted paper from many years ago I used the example:

{2.5} over {24} × {5} over {7} × 10%=0.744% \( Maximum Peak\)

In this example, you have a human with 2.5 hours of optimal performance per day, working 5 days per week, and in their optimal environment for that work 10% of the time, all coming together to produce less than 0.75% of peak optimality.

That peak optimality time is a good example of a precious resource, one of the highest value chunks of time, far more so than the rest of any given day. It also typically follows predictable circadian rhythms and habits, like the hours after waking up and caffeine for “morning people”.

A discussion which requires that peak optimality time to reach a desirable conclusion, but that doesn’t receive it, isn’t worth having. However, if that peak time is dedicated to a book that millions of people read on the subject, then it may well reach that worthwhile conclusion at scale, hitting both those who do and do not dedicate their own higher cognition to it.

It is also critical to note that it isn’t a one-sided or single-entity process. If one side dedicates higher cognition, but the other doesn’t, then you have a parody of the Prisoner’s Dilemma playing out, where one side sabotages the other.

Discussions where both sides engage, with higher cognition and mindful intention, can cultivate collective intelligence. Discussions where one or both parties fail to do so are merely wasting the time of both parties, while pre-souring future discussions.

entry.title: 466 - Prepare for the 5% Tail
entry.content:
Prepare for the worst, or you likely won’t survive. Prepare for the best, or you’ll never truly succeed. People often make the mistake of choosing one or the other, but this duality is about mitigating the long tail of the negative distribution, within reason, as well as taking advantage of the positive versions of the same. The two are complimentary, rather than mutually exclusive.

For example, at the start of 2022 my team set a milestone in AI that stands alone at the cutting edge to this day, and a month later the Russia-Ukraine War began. We both began preparing to seek proper funding for the technology, and we began preparing for geopolitical instability from the US, Russia, and China. I permanently left the US behind, traveling the world for several years, to this end.

We prepared for the best, the event of proper funding, viral press coverage, etc., but we also set up our assets and incomes such as to remain stable under the worst of circumstances. Both I and my CEO aren’t financially reliant on our work with the startup being properly funded, and for my part I’ll be just fine up to the point where human extinction becomes a certainty (status quo), no matter the funding or lack thereof.

Which way events flow ultimately has nothing at all to do with rationality, but rather it is a confluence of chaotic factors commonly referred to simply as “luck”. Even if your startup has the technology, people, market demand, timing, research, etc., all neatly lined up, nothing is guaranteed, nor does “better marketing” actually justify the added expense with any regularity. It remains a gamble.

Even walking people capable of funding our work through what they stood to gain (while omitting what it would cost every last one of them should they fail to do so, instead publishing a thought experiment on the subject) couldn’t inspire people to make one of the most obvious rational choices in human history. Rationality is rarely the problem, human stupidity is, as no amount of rationality may penetrate it.

The long tail of negative outcomes often require long-term planning to mitigate them at any acceptable cost, such as the cost in funds and time to uproot and travel the world. The opposite long tail from the positive outcomes distribution is often much more focused on timing, pacing, and short-term preparations, with a lighter and more flexible set of long-term requirements.

The first step toward being prepared for both ends of this duality is the awareness of what each end translates into for your particular situation, the scenarios populating positive and negative outcomes that exceed your current thresholds of preparation. It generally won’t be worthwhile to dedicate serious resources to a 0.1% scenario, but dedicating moderate resources to a tail of scenarios totalling 5% (the default scientific margin of error) can serve this purpose.

Sooner or later, virtually everyone hits the tail end of the distribution, positive or negative. You can be prepared for it, or not. The choice is yours.

Regression to the Mean occurs after hitting the tail ends of the distribution, but even mediocrity has limits, so one tail or another eventually slaps even the most mediocre of people squarely in the face.

entry.title: 465 - Cognitive Biases Love Spurious Correlations
entry.content:
One popular and pervasive cognitive bias today is to naively equate “income” (or wealth) with “success”, “competence” (domain or generalized), or even “qualification”. The newest generations commit this same error by associating “followers” or “views” with the same thing, each itself often a proxy for “income”.

The net result is that proxies of proxies…..of proxies to the Nth degree are increasingly being substituted for any actual data of causal substance. Just as “Correlation is not causation”, a correlation, of a correlation….of a correlation is so far removed from causation as to be a “spurious correlation”.

Worse still, cognitive biases make people “systematically wrong”, meaning that they are wrong in predictable ways, opening the door wide to equally systematic exploitation. This in turn allows for people who satisfy any of those spurious proxies, income, followers, etc., to misdirect masses of people in such a way as to armor their otherwise obvious and critical flaws.

Consider this in terms of “Population Dynamics”, where the population of a system changes and/or adapts over time, as it operates, selecting for some and against others. These system dynamics heavily favor malevolent entities, commonly known as “free riders” in the scientific literature.

Over time, those systematically exploiting the systematically wrong masses hoard more of the target resource, such as wealth or followers, raising the baseline performance of that exploitation. As that exploitation is further entrenched, the overall system degenerates, creating a downward spiral of increasing vulnerabilities and polarization, as the system further destabilizes.

This dynamic crowds out and starves out other entities, until those holding the target resources are almost exclusively “free riders”. Historically, this has happened many times before with wealth-type resources, and history has found no shortage of ways for disposing of despots.

However, the modern age introduces new challenges as the number of attack vectors for such cognitive traps has exploded. An increasing number of them are being guarded by Artificial Unintelligence “agents”, to the point where every malevolent entity with a search engine has integrated “AI answers” to pollute the top of the results.

Those who attempt to use human data, or “synthetic” (fake) data generated by systems that were themselves trained on human data, to perform ordinarily human tasks, fundamentally can’t afford to ignore human cognitive biases. They also can’t afford to ignore systems architecture, which dictates how those cognitive biases flow through a given system.

Those who study Collective Intelligence, one of the only known reliable methods for reducing the severity of cognitive biases, have a significant advantage in this, but even that is not enough.

It takes more intelligence to solve a problem than it does to create the same problem. Today the AI Domain has a million monkeys chained to typewriters, calling themselves “vibe coders”, “AI engineers”, or even “researchers”, each creating many problems. It will take overwhelming intelligence beyond anything that bunch could ever create to solve all of the problems that they’re directly responsible for.

entry.title: 464 - 97% Failure at the Lowest Bar for "Agentic AI"

link.title: 464 - 97% Failure at the Lowest Bar for "Agentic AI"

entry.content:
Once, people had “careers”, then came “jobs”, and then “outsourcing”, and finally “gig economies”. While each still exists to some degree, “gig economies” are a collection of methods now commonly deployed to pay the least amount of money, offering the least benefits and protections, in exchange for the lowest quality of work. Even at that lowest bar, “Agentic AI” still fails on 97% of THE LOW-BAR TASKS.

https://www.zdnet.com/article/ai-failed-test-on-remote-freelance-jobs/

Again, this is 97% failure rates on the tasks that are thrown at gig economies, where expectations are already firmly cemented at their lowest point, scraping the bottom of the barrel. Most of the meaningful work with high impact on companies never comes close to this level, unless severe and systematic errors in judgement have snowballed into desperation.

Just as jobs and the work that people assign to them may be usefully divided up in this manner, according to pay scale, difficulty, expectations, and trust, so too may the people discussing the AI Domain. I won’t attempt to simplify this into a linear scale, but several categories clearly emerge upon examination:

  • Grounded: Researchers and Engineers who stick to the facts, check sources, and resist the tides of social engineering and exploitation with antifragility. They engage in skepticism paired with the ever-present opportunity, and invitation, to prove them wrong.

  • Socialite: Anyone whose perceptions and beliefs overcome the grounding force of demonstrated fact. This group is peppered across society, forming a sort of social glue in many contexts, as common personality archetypes frequently favor some balance where perceptions and beliefs are allowed to uproot facts.

  • Rodent (the overly polite variation of the proper term): Anyone who intentionally misleads the perceptions and beliefs of others in the pursuit of personal gains. These individuals often specifically target Socialites for manipulation, and Grounded for discrediting. The net result of this combination often involves using Socialites as cannon fodder against Grounded.

There are a variety of Litmus tests which may be applied to check for Rodents or trace back to them. One example is that any group claiming to work or “collaborate” with OpenAI or Anthropic is run by Rodents, as no credible group is going to brag about working with the world’s most obvious frauds.

Another litmus test to check for contaminated Socialites and Rodents is the repetition of debunked tropes, such as the FOMO of “Adopt this technology, or someone else who does will replace you”. This is not to be confused with a competent person telling you precisely how a given tool can and cannot be used, as the difference between that and naïve adoption is night and day.

97% of the lowest bar tasks can’t be automated today, an outcome which was purely predictable over 3 years ago with regard to the technology in question, and of the remaining 3% any pre-trained Magic 8 Ball could probably handle at least 1/3 of the tasks. The bar is that low.

entry.title: 463 - The Strength to Do Nothing
entry.content:
Sometimes the hardest thing to do is nothing at all. This is one of the great ironies of modern life, as we’re constantly inundated with ways in which all of our time and attention might be flushed down one digital toilet or another.

The emotional pull of “the search for the next shiny object” is strong, often constant, and for many, the default. It is a collection of cognitive biases that drive humanity every day, exploited to great effect by the “dark patterns” baked into virtually every app and platform online today, from “newsfeeds” with infinite scrolling, to the red numbered notifications that emotionally demand your attention.

For people like myself, and many others, these problems are made more acute by the ways in which our investments of time tend to bottleneck. Everyone is subject to bottlenecks of time to some degree, whether you’re stuck waiting on a friend to get back to you, a colleague to complete their part of the work, or some larger event in the world to take place.

Each time humans hit such a bottleneck, that search for the next shiny object comes calling loudly and clearly. The most cognitively debilitated individuals may be recognized by the fidget spinners on their desks, or TikTok and Twitter usage on their phones.

This is where a busy and distracted mind sharply diverges from those who learn, grow, and develop their cognitive and emotional capacities. Part of that search for the next shiny object is to distract, to feed a neurochemical addiction to stimulation, but it also frequently draws on the emotional dislike of being bottlenecked in the ability to continue doing a thing, be it by known others or the world at large.

Part of overcoming the downward cognitive spiral that such fermenting dislike offers is a matter emphasized by Stoicism, to recognize where you end and the world begins, doing what you can, working with what you have, and leaving the rest to others. This is easier said than done, as it is easy to logically walk through and accept, but emotional acceptance and integration of the same takes time and effort.

Personally, I grew up having to get the same work done in half of the time given to others, which I did. In my adult life, I’ve quickly bottlenecked countless times because of this, making it absolutely vital to learn that lesson, which I eventually did.

Reaching that point also offers stronger benefits today than ever before. The number of dark patterns and cognitive vectors of attack are still growing quickly, and getting worse with time. AI-induced and AI-aided psychosis are being distilled down to a malevolent parody of science, as the “attention economy” explicitly aims for cognitive decline at scale.

Go for a walk, get away from your digital devices, learn some form of meditation, reflect on your life and your actions, or simply enjoy the silence.

entry.title: 462 - Flush the AI Toilet
entry.content:
Since the end of 2022, the world has seen tech giants and startups do one thing over and over again in the AI domain, double-down on hype. At first, this was largely disregarded as being par for the course with the PR campaigns of most any other industry, but that was a mistake.

Sure, people rapidly debunked absurd claims quickly in the first few months (like the “Sparks of AGI” fraud from #MicroSlop), but those claims kept proliferating far faster than they could be debunked, and bad actors began building upon those debunked claims for added layers of obfuscation.

The difference is exponential, not in the capacities of popular AI technology, but in the exponentially growing divide between our observable reality and the things that people talk up and sell in the AI space, so desperately attempting to indoctrinate others into believing them.

The CEOs of the world’s largest tech companies and startups are constantly contradicting themselves and one another, being proven wrong, making one objectively false claim after another, and spinning financial narratives that are otherworldly delusional.

In the past several weeks:

  • #MicroSlop’s (Microsoft) CEO begged people to come up with a killer app to justify their blatantly idiotic investments in “AI Infrastructure” at DAVOS,

  • Nvidia’s CEO preached that businesses should ignore ROI concerns for their AI investments,

  • Anthropic and OpenAI are attempting to differentiate themselves via a shallow rivalry, despite being two apples from the same rotten tree,

  • Nature.com published a deeply fraudulent article claiming that AI had surpassed human intelligence (not their first dive into fraud by any measure, mind you),

  • “MoltBook” has given rise to countless absurdities of hype, fear, comical memes, and mindless investment,

  • Oracle has claimed that “Everything is fine”, and that they aren’t financially impacted by Nvidia reneging on their defunct $100 billion investment in OpenAI

The rate and intensity of fraud is accelerating, as OpenAI attempts to reach an IPO before the bubble bursts, and as major tech giants hosting the cloud infrastructure jockey for the best position to reduce their losses when it does.

In the 2008 financial crisis, Iceland was a paragon in terms of their response to the guilty parties. They sent the guilty bankers to prison for their crimes. The AI bubble is going to be far worse, and the rate of “AI Infrastructure” projects being delayed or cancelled is already accelerating, up from 16 in December to 25 in January.

There are going to be a lot of guilty parties to hold to account, for a much larger bubble, with much more severe fallout. Those guilty parties are screaming louder than ever right now, as they continue to double-down on these things, even as the opportunities for remotely plausible cash grabs evaporate.

entry.title: 461 - The "Hypothetical Hero" Complex
entry.content:
Many people yearn for the opportunities to demonstrate their worth, their strengths, their moral fortitude, or other talents. A common form that this takes stems from popular fiction, stories of heroes and villains and protecting innocent people.

For example, I once had a friend who would fantasize about one terrible thing or another happening nearby, where he could throw himself into danger to save people from some villain(s), whether it be something as mundane as a grocery store, but being attacked by armed men, or random events on the street. This sort of day-dreaming fantasy is quite common, and can be relatively healthy when held in moderation.

Personally, my mind’s default state (auto-pilot) is an endless stream of scenarios, some vaguely similar to those above, but most rooted in more practical near and long-term strategizing. The real problem with such popular hero vs villain fantasizing is that it carves out a mental state that is both:

(Comfortable) AND (Reactionary)

Neither is a problem when taken in isolation, but when paired together and attached to wildly improbable scenarios then it ferments into a kind of aimless apathy. Cognitive Biases reinforce the gates of such structure, as discomfort demands cognitive load to adapt, just as proactive activity demands far more higher cognitive effort than reactionary states.

Someone can fantasize about playing the hero a thousand times, and have less than a 1 in 1,000 chance of any applicable scenario actually playing out in their lifetime. Even when one does, knowing someone who went through one, the results are often far less satisfying than the imagined anticipation, which aligns with the research on claiming that a person will do something being more emotionally satisfying than actually doing so.

One of the most difficult types of higher cognitive tasks is that of proactive action. Reactive action holds the largely false promise of reduced uncertainty, where perceived uncertainty is often far lower than actual uncertainty, as well as offering the opportunity to grow emotionally attached to some plan of action, both of which serve as mental traps. In contrast, proactive action both takes the initiative and makes many decisions in the moment of that initiative, requiring higher cognition, focus, and benefiting strongly from a sharp mind.

Another pitfall of such comfortable and reactionary mental traps is that they’re highly vulnerable to narrative manipulations, such as those commonly observed in phenomena like “cancel culture”, where any group of people ferments into a Groupthink. These dynamics have been exploited to great effect in both “GenAI” and “Crypto” before it.

Real proactive action heroes are found in places like Ukraine, where they fight for their freedom. Apathetic, reactionary, and enslaved cattle are commonly found within narrative-reinforced cages popular across the US and similar decaying societies. The difference between the two is plain to see.

entry.title: 460 - The Delayed Trolley Problem
entry.content:
Delaying decisions to much later dates, as well as shifting the responsibility for making them across bureaucracies, frequently plays out in a manner similar to the Trolley Problem variation below. In this variation, the cost of making the hard decision increases every time that decision is delayed. The increase may be linear or exponential, but it reliably increases in either case.

This dynamic should sound familiar, as it is frequently observed in the bureaucratic systems of governments. People are happy to take credit for their successes within systems where they may rise through a hierarchy, but holding them accountable for their failures often proves unreliable, at best.

Just as people often attempt to “dodge” responsibility for their failures (or snatch credit from others), that activity is also time-sensitive. Move quickly and precisely enough and those human reflexes for dodging responsibility won’t cut it. Likewise, countermeasures prepared in advance often conform to recognizable patterns, which become increasingly trivial to recognize and bypass with experience.

Even relatively simple systems aimed at anti-corruption showed remarkable success over the past 10 years…so much so in fact that people in charge panicked and shut them down in places like China, where some 8,000 likely corrupt officials were flagged by one system in years previous. In 2019 the shutdown of this “Zero Trust” system was covered in many news outlets, such as the South China Morning Post saying “Despite being restricted to just 30 counties and cities, artificial intelligence system has already helped snare 8,721 officials”. Remember, this was in 2019.

Much as corruption demonstrates many recognizable patterns, so too do attempts to dodge responsibility or systematically delay decisions. While attempts to dodge responsibility may differ from corruption in terms of statistical legality, from a system dynamics perspective they are virtually identical, making it just as easy to detect such activity at a baseline.

However, attempts to dodge responsibility, on average, are also going to be much easier to detect in practice, due to Population Dynamics operating over that statistical difference in legality. That means that more effort goes into preventing corruption from being detected than the dynamically comparable activities of those seeking to dodge responsibility.

Clear-cut examples of both corruption and systematic delays were demonstrated by individuals such as Mitch McConnell in the US, delaying the appointment of judges, then ramming through as many as possible during his window of control, particularly as that window was closing in 2020. Each such example of the two being successfully paired together and going unpunished also dramatically increases the odds of the same occurring again.

The current mega-scale AI Bubble was entirely avoidable, but there is no avoiding the global financial damage now, many delays deep.

entry.title: 459 - "GenAI", Pitch vs Reality
entry.content:
The big consumer-facing pitch for much of “GenAI” over the past several years could be summarized in the phrase: “Use simple natural language for UX! It’ll be easy and people love it!”

How those interactions actually look: “You are a diligent and meticulous assistant. You must produce an array of (x) with (y) metadata, for all cases where (z) is true. Make no mistakes or you’re fired. I’ll pay you $1,000 if you get it right.”

Absent severe brain damage or memory loss, people have generally come to recognize that the people treating these systems like coding, with ample knowledge and experience in real coding, are the ones actually making use of them to any reasonable effect. This demented version of coding includes tricks like threats, bribery, the (unstable) use of key phrases, and more traditional pseudo-code descriptions.

At the same time, infamous fraudsters like the guy who first coined the term “vibe coding” (Karpathy) are publicly admitting that they’ve grown disillusioned with it, recognizing that their own skills have (strongly) degraded, as they abandon “vibe coding”.

Behind closed doors, the pitch for the same AI is often oriented around producing more code, or laying off entire customer service departments, with both equally absurd, and severely detrimental in different ways. In coding the systems introduce more, harder to spot, and increasingly severe Cybersecurity vulnerabilities, as noted in numerous examples among my posts.

In customer service attempts to use that same “GenAI”, you frequently encounter a hard disconnect between technical implementation and customer UX. For example, the technical people recognize that so long as it runs an unspecialized model via API that you can’t leave such a system accessible to anyone, or else it will be abused and run up a bill, as it is vulnerable-by-design technology.

However, it can’t serve a large number of customer service functions if a login is required to first access it, such as the all-too-common account problems centered on logins. This places the resulting system directly in conflict with its stated purpose, as it prevents customers from reaching “customer service” at the architectural level of implementation, meaning that the model’s performance or lack thereof doesn’t matter at all, since neither it nor any human customer service may be accessed by those who need it.

Remember all of those increasingly severe and obscured Cybersecurity vulnerabilities being produced by “GenAI”? Guess how those will impact the volume of customer service tickets hitting the above wall of nightmarishly awful customer experiences.

Those kinds of spectacularly awful experiences tend to dominate the memory of a brand for many years thereafter, and they are only the tip of the iceberg for the fallout caused by Cybersecurity failures. Most of the damage tends to hit peoples’ lives far outside of the scope of the company at fault for them.

Both the public-facing “Natural Language UX” pitch and the private “Coding and Customer Service” pitch are absurd to the extreme, but the people pitching them remain far worse.

entry.title: 458 - Laws, Anti-Laws, and Ethics
entry.content:
The only “laws” that may be said to exist are those which are consistently enforced. This fact was acknowledged more than 800 years ago with the Magna Carta. I’ll term the inconsistently enforced subset as “anti-laws”, which no one may be compelled to abide by.

Likewise, “laws” constitute a social compact among the people of a given group or region, as they have little or nothing to do with “morals”, and exponentially less to do with “ethics”. This means that no “authority” (technically a cognitive bias) figure may create or alter the enforcement of “laws” without the confirmed support of those impacted by the decision.

Someone who attempts to exploit the inconsistency of “anti-laws” is quite plainly a “criminal”. When such a person places themselves, or is placed, in a position of “authority” where they dictate new “laws” (and anti-laws) as well as how “anti-laws” may be enforced, they become a “dictator”.

Some models of governance systematize and dilute this form of corruption by selectively injecting “religion” (social, in-group) in place of “cultural morals” (social, all-group), adding additional layers of complexity and obfuscation, to much the same effect as a dictator. Many dictators also latch onto these systematic vulnerabilities, allowing for much easier production of “demagogues”.

The wealthy of most countries worldwide exploit anti-laws through the carrot & stick of wealth that they hold, an advantage not accessible to >99% of the population. However, everyone else has access to the stick, and the size of that stick grows with the portion of the population who take hold of it.

A simple historic example of this is the idea of “unions”, or strength in numbers. One critical and often neglected factor determining the influence of that stick is the willingness to actually use it. You can “walk softly, but carry a big stick” to strong effect, but only so long as others know that you’re fully willing and prepared to swing it.

Ethics, the hypothetical point where cognitive bias has been removed from moral systems (which individual humans may only approximate), is what separates someone violating fundamentally arbitrary and transient “laws” and “anti-laws” from “dictators” and “demagogues”. Under ethics, personal gains may be one by-product among many, but for dictators and demagogues they are the point.

Ethics is also achieved through systems of collective intelligence, which by definition reduce cognitive biases, meaning that the same governance structures used to create laws are utilized to reach ethics. This means that while one individual approximating ethics may violate “laws” and “anti-laws” that weren’t created through such collective intelligence, that same individual is subject to the laws of any such collective intelligence system which they are a member of.

Lastly, membership within any group is fundamentally chosen by the individual, it may never be given through “birth” or “law”, as that would violate autonomy.

entry.title: 457 - The Rise of Faux-AI-Benchmarks
entry.content:
Many “AI Benchmarks” today make one particularly simple mistake that wholly invalidates any claims that they might make. This is separate from the requirements to make a genuinely good benchmark, leaving that a much higher bar than baseline (and increasingly uncommon) success today. The problem is this:

  • If you use another “Deep Learning” based algorithm to create “synthetic (fake) data”, or to curate real data from a dataset, then you are inherently placing the stamp of that algorithm on the data, carving a predictable and new path of least resistance through it.

  • In the case of “synthetic data” this means that the algorithm will often follow that carved path of least resistance through the data back to some approximation of the original algorithm.

  • In the case of data curation, the resulting dataset will predictably be weakened, with a new path of algorithmic predictability carved through the metaphorical landscape.

  • For both cases, the problem is generally going to be both far easier and very different relative to the original stated intention of the benchmark.

This is an increasingly severe problem, as people claiming to be researchers have become very prolific, pushing out what amounts to fake benchmarks and research papers, which major tech companies are happy to endorse, as each plays into the various flavors of PR (BS) that they use, lacking almost any real technical value for their products.

You can use many simple and deterministic algorithms, or statistically “noisy” humans, to create the datasets for real benchmarks, as neither one can predictably cause the kind of human-incomprehensible curve-fitting necessary to carve these new paths of least resistance through the data. It doesn’t matter in the slightest that the paths aren’t human-comprehensible for common “AI models” today, as they were never anything remotely like human thought or cognitive architecture to begin with.

The technology that they’re based on is built to find those kinds of breadcrumbs, absent the faintest shred of “understanding” or “reasoning”, but the trail of breadcrumbs makes it much more trivial to do so over any terrain.

The models that excel at such Faux-AI-Benchmarks are solving a problem, it is just a completely different problem than the claims made, and usually a far easier one, with little or no real-world value. Look under the hood and you’ll find some variation on “Cognitive Dissonance Man”, not what you wanted.

As noted in months previous, SWE-Bench Verified was itself “verified” in research to be fraudulent, debunking all claims of value from it. This should come as no surprise, given that OpenAI had some involvement in that variation on SWE-Bench.

Many other benchmarks have faired no better, and some even worse, like the exposed fraud of FrontierMath, which actually received news coverage.

Today, the “Tech Companies” peddling scamware still frequently cite SWE-Bench Verified scores, pretending that it isn’t already debunked, and it serves as a litmus test to highlight bad actors in AI.

entry.title: 456 - Your Grandfather's Boogie Man
entry.content:
The “Golden Dome” concept of a missile defense system has been floated numerous times before over the decades, previous examples including The Strategic Defense Initiative (SDI), derisively nicknamed the “Star Wars program”. However, the technology and methodology are both absurd trash. They’ve been a highly profitable dumpster fire for the Defense Industry, like many other naïve and/or absurd pet projects before it.

It has a precisely 0% chance of stopping Russia’s nuclear-powered and nuclear-armed “Poseidon”, which China has had three years to replicate. The only nuclear weapons that it might intercept even 80% of the time are those of “middle powers” as the PM of Canada put it, like many countries in Europe. Yes, the “Defense System” is intended as a deterrent against Europe and their allies, not against Russia or China.

In December 1994 the Budapest Memorandum was signed to remove Ukraine’s nuclear weapons, an agreement which was obviously betrayed in subsequent years, first with Crimea, then with the full-scale invasion of Ukraine. For Europe, making “Defense” agreements with the US today would be to repeat this mistake, since the US regime is already every bit as hostile, radical, imperial, delusional, and violent as Russia’s regime, if not more so.

This is also about overtly placing space-based weapons, which once placed may be used as a threat against all nations. The US has operated on the tactics of a terrorist regime for decades, threatening via “Air-dominance” with the ability to bomb any country from the skies, as they have frequently done, and as discussed at some length in David Graeber’s books. This is one of the reasons why countries have long paid a kind of “tribute” to the US in the form of US debts that will never be repaid.

Handing such a demonstrably malevolent and terrorism-based regime the freedom to deploy space-based weapons (and subsequently threaten nations with them) is pretty much the most moronic thing that any council of nations could do. This is further compounded by the extremely high probability of rendering the space around Earth impossible to deploy satellites into, or even safely traverse, by flooding it with the debris of such satellites.

If your leaders in Europe sign any such agreement with the US, have them checked for brain damage, and check their bank accounts for any suspiciously large deposits. You can probably bet on one, the other, or both coming back positive should that occur.

The alternative is if they had viable weapons to bypass such a “defense” system, but given NATO’s history this seems rather unlikely, at least beyond the fraction of missiles that get through such a “Moron Dome” defense anyway. Nuclear weapons are your grandfather’s boogie man, and technology has advanced far beyond them in the past 80 years. Fortunately, many of those advancing it weren’t complete morons, and so they didn’t hand it to the US, they pointed it at them.

If the shuffling and rambling corpse of an oompa-loompa can fool your nation into signing such agreements with the US, then you don’t have a nation, you have a collapsing house of cards.

entry.title: 455 - Victory by Design
entry.content:
