Meanwhile, Cybercrime has boomed, and the tech bubble inflated, so here is to hoping that 2026 sees a few long overdue changes deployed.

entry.title: 430 - Three types of "Transparency"
entry.content:
Real transparency is messy and random, which most people still intuitively realize. However, what people think of as “transparency” today generally falls into 3+ categories, the real thing only being one among them.

You have the corporately curated “C-transparency”, where most lies come in the form of omission and selection, the corporate lawyer’s domain.

There is also the PR department’s “P-transparency”, where everything is polished to follow some narrative, even when that process completely unhinges it from reality.

Lastly, we come to “R-transparency”, where you get real, random, and subsequently messy transparency into reality, because that is how reality is.

People understand that R-transparency is the only truth among them, but humans are so cognitive biased in favor of narratives, confirmation bias, and array of related biases that they’ll hold this understanding in cognitive dissonance, still accepting the narrative, as it is far easier to remember and work with than messy reality.

You get P-transparency from the representatives and senior staff of major tech companies, varying degrees of lies to boost stock prices, startup valuations for raising funds, or the sales of defective products and services. This is also the only form of transparency that is optimized to search engines and news feed algorithms, making most people over 100x more likely to see it than anything else.

You get C-transparency in the “Terms of Service (ToS)” that you probably don’t read more than 1-10% of, if that. This is also by design, as the meaning is often obfuscated behind dense legalize language to mask their horrifying intentions.

R-transparency is a bit Autistic, just unfiltered, random, difficult to fit into simple narratives. This is also actually why PR departments exist, because such reality often has to be severely distorted to fit into concise and coherent narratives.

Much of society has also begun to realize in 2025 just how deep into “unreality” humanity has moved, where the lies and BS wash out everything else in a tsunami of volume. This is largely thanks to the algorithms that prioritize them, as well as money backing them. Residents of the digital world now live in a garbage dump, surrounded by trash at all times, unable to escape the smell of it. Is it any wonder why an increasing margin now seek to put it all to an end?

One of the remaining litmus tests for honest dealings today is that R-transparency looks nothing like the other two, as they are each distinctly optimized to a purpose, and R-transparency is, by definition, not optimized at all.

If someone (or a platform) is feeding you one of the optimized forms of “transparency”, they are your enemy.

entry.title: 429 - Nvidia, DRAM, and the 4th Quadrant of Black Swans

link.title: 429 - Nvidia, DRAM, and the 4th Quadrant of Black Swans

entry.content:
The Hardware War continues to heat up: NVIDIA: WTF?

They’re also busy establishing partnerships with Palantir and Defence Departments, in the vain hopes of securing both tons of cash from suckers, and securing their physical assets from the predictable assaults of war.

Of course, Nvidia employees, and other bad actors in the space, themselves remain vulnerable most hours of the day, even if they eventually manage to secure their physical facilities from assault, which I doubt could even stand up to techniques that Ukraine has demonstrated when fighting their own larger and malevolent enemy.

This war on “consumers”, ordinary people, is at least as bad as Steve Burke demonstrates, but is likely much worse. It also has absolutely nothing to do with the cutting edge of AI, this is hardware, money, and political power being consolidated, backed only by trash-AI, things that have never even remotely approached the cutting edge.

There are virtually always at least 3 possible roads into the future, and this is no exception.

  • The road of the status quo that bad actors like Nvidia and OpenAI are paving leads to extinction in no uncertain terms. Consolidate decision-making into the hands of the most overtly incompetent and malevolent people in human history, and that predictable result will occur.

  • People can invest in cutting-edge AI systems, technology that most haven’t even heard of yet, and that very pointy stick can pop the bubble, making a few savvy hedge-funds quite rich, and bankrupting the bad actors at the point where they are most vulnerable, right now.

  • Other “Black Swans” could hit and become trends, as current events offer us peak Black Swan mating season, extremely high complexity with high payoffs, in Taleb’s “4th quadrant”. One hypothetical example of this is something I mentioned previously, if some fraction of those FPS gamers got off of their *sses and realized that they could go full-on Frank Castle (The Punisher), or “Boondock Saints” for a team-play parallel, on the obvious real-world villains.

While CES in early January may offer a glimpse into the next wave of malevolent and Dystopian moves from industry bad actors, the further this war develops, the greater the odds of scenarios 2 and/or 3 occurring.

The only sustainable paths forward that avoid literal “bullets in the air” war (aimed at tech company employees) are those where ordinary people aren’t getting screwed out of their own autonomy, basic human rights, as any historian can list off plenty of wars and revolutions that came from attempts to take them away.

As much as the drug-infused Alex Karp attempts to be the quintessential scary crazy person, he is a clown, and Palantir sells groups like the US DoD grossly outdated tech, like a business orchestration system rebranded as “AI”. Neither Karp nor those who buy from him and others like him, can handle a competent opponent.

entry.title: 428 - Reading Fiction, to Invest in Sanity

link.title: 428 - Reading Fiction, to Invest in Sanity

entry.content:
My appreciation of books is usually directed at research-oriented literature, from the rather small number of scientists who get books published. This leaves gaps in my reading schedule, often filled with works of fiction (beyond the classics) that can provide ample entertainment, immersion, and provoke their own share of thoughts.

One excellent example is “The Quantum Thief” trilogy, by Hannu Rajaniemi, a Finnish biotechnology entrepreneur with a PhD in mathematics. He introduces a lot of pretty advanced scientific concepts into his writing, which can make for a welcome change of pace and imagination. (Just run screaming in the other direction if he tries to write about “Generative AI” again)

Another is the “Murderbot Diaries” series by Martha Wells, which despite the name (which turned me off from reading it for a few years) isn’t really about murderous robots. The dialogue is quite simply amazing, though the stories are fairly short, so you’ll likely find yourself re-reading them.

The most extensive high quality work that I’ve found came from Matt Dinniman, the “Dungeon Crawler Carl” series, which combines fantasy, science fiction, and comedy all in one. By the time I got around to reading the first of this series the 7th book had just come out, and I quickly plowed through all of them, which are especially good in audiobook format.

All are books that I’ve personally read more than once.

Sadly, platforms like Audible (the platforms themselves) remain irredeemable trash, as they don’t allow you to block recommendations for utter garbage, like the writing of Brian Herbert, Robert Greene, J.K. Rowling, or Steven Pinker, just to name a few of their worst offences today. You also see this particular inexcusable offence on platforms like Spotify, where a simple “block (track, artist, book, podcast, etc.)” function could make all of the difference.

Instead, everyone is left to expect Idiocracy-level misalignment to reality from the platform’s recommendations. The recommender algorithm is so bad that I came to expect that it could only recommend total crap, with Dungeon Crawler Carl proving to be the exception to the rule.

More social platforms, like the obvious one for this post, commit the same crimes via “fraudvertising”, such as fraudvertisements for OpenAI. Court cases are now cropping up with regard to a different platform, Meta, and their own revenue stream from overtly criminal sources, so some attention is starting to be paid to this problem.

That aside, while the platforms may be hot garbage, there are some good books (and audiobooks) worth diving into among the many works of fiction that they host. Whether for Xmas or the Lunar New Year, I can recommend taking breaks from family to enjoy a good book of fiction. That can be a better investment in sanity than any food or drink one might consume.

*Also be warned, if you go with the audiobooks, try to make sure that the same narrator handled all books in a series, as the pronunciation of names can vary painfully between different narrators.

entry.title: 427 - The Mating Call of "Black Swans"
entry.content:
Another under-appreciated factor governing “Black Swans” as Taleb puts it, is that increasing levels of complexity also inherently increase the number of such unexpected, rare, and highly potent events. This is because more complex confluences of compounding variables are fundamentally harder to predict and mitigate, and when they go unmitigated, they predictably become that much more potent.

The world has been rapidly increasing in complexity, while the human cognitive capacity hasn’t budged, and narrow AI offers virtually nothing of real assistance in this matter, only the illusions of narrow optimization over some naïve, blind, and often net-negative KPIs.

This is part of why my team specifically focuses on a completely different kind of AI architecture, specifically built to handle hyper-complexity, dynamically, across any and all domains where it may be needed.

The mainstream of “AI” has grown notorious for flooding the internet with Slop, greedily consuming all of the hardware (like RAM) that they can’t actually afford, and offering paper-thin illusions of value that are quickly debunked, like any of the overtly fraudulent and/or easily gamed “benchmarks” that major LLM-providers commonly cite. They reliably fail to handle non-trivial levels of complexity, and even minimally complex combinatorial problems they can barely even appear to compete on while cheating.

In contrast, our technology, built on the Independent Core Observer Model (ICOM) cognitive architecture:

  • Completely avoids “Hallucination”, and offers precisely zero Slop

  • Isn’t inherently hardware-greedy, able to scale up and down as-needed

  • Demonstrates value in real-world complexity

  • Resisted attempts to break or breech it for 2.5 years “in the wild”

The difference is fundamental, a night-and-day contrast. We bootstrapped ICOM from a Technology Readiness Level 1 to 7, but bringing it to 9 will require proper funding, mostly just to pay full-time engineering staff. That is what we’re working on now.

Ironically, the past 3 years of industry-scale investments in Slop Generators (or “Sloptimizers” as one AI engineer put it) have also created new use cases for our technology, as they fundamentally can’t clean up the mess, but our technology can. It will be a long process, since a large part of the population evidently never took the phrase “don’t sh*t where you eat” to heart, but it can be done.

ChatGPT was certainly a “Black Swan” in the sense that most of the industry wasn’t expecting obvious fraud to take center-stage for 3+ years and suck up over a hundred billion USD in funding. What we can predictably expect of the status quo is more complexity, producing both more and more severe “Black Swans”. However, we can also deploy technology to avoid that fate.

entry.title: 426 - The Comorbidities of Multi-step AI Distortions

link.title: 426 - The Comorbidities of Multi-step AI Distortions

entry.content:
Elaborating further on the previous topic of multi-step (“agentic”) AI distortions and their cumulative effects, there are two major factors deserving of a separate post, that of adversarial attacks (usually active) and corporately curated cognitive biases (usually passive).

You can regress to some pretty unhinged means even without these (see “regression to the mean” in this context), but add either or both of these factors, and you have something else haphazardly “steering” the model. These can be heavy-handed, like Grok being made to worship Elon Musk, or more oriented toward obfuscation, like Anthropic’s models, as well as any variety of Indirect Prompt injections or other attack vectors covered in things like the MCPLIB Attack Framework. All of these introduce strong and cumulative distortions of various kinds.

In multi-step processes it also becomes SIGNIFICANTLY easier for AI Cybersecurity professionals to break them, as it takes the pressure off of accomplishing the whole task in a single shot, allowing for all manner of reinforcement and obfuscation techniques to be applied. These techniques also tend to become more powerful the larger and “more advanced” the model in use.

One such powerful technique that Brian Chamberlain of CrowdStrike has spoken about in his presentations is as simple as getting the model to agree with you on arbitrary things, as this makes it more likely to continue to agree to whatever comes after that.

The constant distortions themselves make it easier to manipulate these systems in ways that fly under the radar, and the corporately curated cognitive biases, once known, pave roads by which each system may be even more systematically exploited.

I previously published a research paper mockingly titled “Automated Bias and Indoctrination at Scale… Is All You Need” on this subject, though that was before things became measurably worse with the proliferation of “Chain-of-Thought” (CoT) and “agents”, as people have come to refer to them, more broadly.

In the more than 2.5 years since then, a massive economic bubble, industry-scale hardware fraud (that may strongly encourage the near-future invasion of Taiwan), and the dramatic proliferation of AI Slop and cybercrime have set in.

You can tightly bound these systems, anchoring them to single-step process, where the ground-truth is always a step away, to limit some of these issues, though they also cease to be “agentic” when you do.

While it is technically possible to tame such technology within more general and multi-step processes, you can’t do so with the same technology that fundamentally produces those problems. You can double, quadruple, or even 1,000x your compute, but you only reduce the odds of the given problem in doing so, never truly preventing it.

If you want a system that can take the “Peter Principle” to the theoretical limits of super-incompetence, an LLM-based “agent” might just do the trick.

entry.title: 425 - Black Swans and Tech Industry "Pandas"
entry.content:
As I began my annual re-listening to the audiobooks of Nassim Nicholas Taleb, with “The Black Swan” (2nd edition), his opening point of them being a matter of perspective struck me, not for what he said, but for what he didn’t say. As someone who works in and studies Collective Intelligence, it quickly appeared that the point needs to be made:

Since Black Swans are tied to perspective, your team, organization, or government is only able to see the fraction of rare events coming that are visible to one or more perspectives that you keep at the table. You may have many diverse perspectives, but fail to routinely listen to them, taking account of the risks and opportunities that each may see.

If the perspectives making your decisions are stacked with similarities, then you’ll be blind to both Negative Black Swans (major risks, such as systematic reasons for technological failures) and Positive Black Swans (major opportunities, such as Blue Ocean Markets).

It is reasonably well-known that Collective Intelligence produces more intelligent results from a group of people by effectively reducing the cognitive biases of that group in the results, but Collective Intelligence is the unsung hero of another massive benefit, a greatly enhanced ability to recognize events that others perceive as “Black Swans”.

It was discovered that “Innovation Platforms” where problems are offered as competitions for solving (often manufacturing and chemistry-based) problems frequently have the best solutions come from people in unrelated and/or unexpected fields.

This is because so-called “Hard Problems” are similar to Black Swans, they often only remain “Hard” from the particular perspective noting them as such. Those within a field easily solve most problems, call them “Easy” or “Normal”, but they are systematically biased in such ways that render some subset of remaining problems “Hard” to them. These “Hard” problems are merely an artifact of Population Dynamics, where they are hard to the subset who solved the rest of the problems, but that subset isn’t equipped to handle them.

Black Swans are themselves a kind of problem, with success being to recognize them in advance, rendering them opportunities and risks (no longer “Black Swans”), and failure to recognize them in advance predictably leading teams, companies, and governments to slam head-first into those brick walls at whatever speed they may be traveling.

Right now the “risks and opportunities” being peddled in the tech industry, for both software and hardware, bear a striking resemblance to the zoo exhibit below.

Part of this stems from bad actors, but the part dictating the vulnerability or antifragility of potential suckers is, as described above, the diversity of perspectives that they keep at the decision-making table.

When people finally see what cutting-edge AI actually looks like, many are likely to call it a “Black Swan”, to which I’ll reply “Not to any technical people in the industry who haven’t kept their head planted firmly up their own *ss for the past 6 years or so”.

Black Swans, positive and negative, are perspective. Perspective is what you need more of to recognize them.

entry.title: 424 - Understanding LLM-based AI in 12 seconds
entry.content:
This 12-second video can probably change your perspective on AI, as it makes the point visually, concisely, and quite strikingly for what LLM-based AI actually does. Many people are aware of problems with the technology in a very abstract and fuzzy sense, but this video makes it a bit easier to grasp. Watch closely, and you’ll see what I mean.

You start out with a so-called “flagship” model from one of the most heavily funded companies, handing it a meme and instructing it to reproduce that exact same image, not changing a thing. For any intelligent system this task would be utterly trivial, a small child could easily do it. As the video quickly illustrates, even that utterly trivial task rapidly devolves in ways that would be deeply psychotic for a human.

Not only does the video warp quickly and in fairly extreme ways, it continues to warp until it has changed all 3 people pictured in it into the same severely distorted person, totally unrelated to the original image.

That trivial task is also an example that mirrors the simplest implementation of the same underlying mechanisms shared by virtually all “agents” that people are being marketed today. Any time when an “agent” takes multiple steps, such as “thinking” modes from “reasoning” models, this same basic process is what happens under the hood (often a version of “Chain-of-Thought”, CoT).

This process of constant distortions being introduced at every step is more easily masked in systems where the goal isn’t to maintain a data point as it originally existed, particularly when various forms of structure bias that system in one direction or another. However, the distortion is still constant, still warping everything it touches along an arbitrary, unchecked, and usually unmonitored number of dimensions.

Every time that you integrate an “agent” into your business processes, this is what you’re getting under the hood. The more steps such systems are allowed to take, the more severe the distortion you can expect from them, as it is cumulative.

Each different brand of “agent” also introduces different kinds of distortion, and tool-using “agents” in things like MCP will gain different kinds of distortion depending on the tools that they call upon at any given step. This effectively means that no amount of compute or human labor could ever mitigate the distortions of tool-using “agents”, as that problems slams into a major combinatorial explosion, with the variety of distortions in that combination exceeding any margin that could hope for mitigation.

This video also adeptly illustrates “AI-brainrot”, as it takes place within the brains of anyone spending substantial amounts of daily time talking to such chatbots. These highly sycophantic systems of mirroring feed back a constantly warping image of life events, tailored to the manipulation of that individual. The resulting “AI Psychosis” of this process is entirely unsurprising.

**An update (17-12-25) on the rate of distortions, following feedback on this post in comments (on LinkedIn):

The original source (earlier this year, apparently) used a slightly different prompt to accelerate the distortion process. This still demonstrates the same principle in a different way, but explains why the model hit full-blown convergence to statistical mush within 200 steps, rather than 2,000 or 20,000. Updated source:

https://x.com/genekogan/status/1916167820276371666?s=46

Credit (falsely) claimed by: https://fixvx.com/ChatgptLunatics/status/2000314982194622894

Thanks to Iliya Kovachki and Matt Abrams for linking the original source (above).

That said, other strong examples of even single-step distortion are being reported, such as Peter Corless‘s test of Grok following this post. His example explicitly used “Recreate this image exactly” (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/petercorless_reproduce-this-image-exactly-check-out-activity-7406802318320553984-NWtm?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAADawx60BAW7zCI1eZcZKSq6icSOUuDWuAls). Other examples are also being reported, pending links to them.

entry.title: 423 - War in the age of the LLM-Brained

link.title: 423 - War in the age of the LLM-Brained

entry.content:
While the relative economic incentives of war are worth noting, the ideological factors tend to weigh more heavily in any violent engagement, be it at the scale of individuals or nations. As the ideologies proliferating across the world are increasingly isolated and fermented into more extreme versions via “AI” echo chambers and 1-on-1 sycophantic mirroring, we can expect to see a steadily increasing number of casualties, such as those of the recent mass-shooting at Bondi Beach in Australia.

Emotions are known to be the human motivational system for individuals, as the research literature has thoroughly covered, but at the scale of groups and nations such motivations are systematized and structured into what we call “ideologies”, shared motivational frameworks. Like emotions, these have no inherent relationship with reality or rationality, despite being systematically structured. They can be just as unhinged as any individual’s motives.

Such ideologies mix about as well with LLM-based technology as illicit drugs do with alcohol, producing “AI Psychosis” with steadily increasing regularity. You also can’t regulate ideologies, or even realistically limit their spread, making them substantially more dangerous than literal viruses in practice. While plenty of people have feared novel viral bioweapons for decades, they should probably fear pathogenic ideologies more today.

Ideologies are reliably “useful, but wrong” tools for the cognitive toolbox, cognitive shortcuts to avoid critical thinking and hard higher cognitive labor. They can be valuable, but they can also lead entire populations into absurd wars and terrorism of all kinds. This is globally visible in how no less than 5 countries have operated for decades, China, Russia, the US, Israel, and Türkiye, to name a few. All operate with a heavy emphasis on terrorizing foreign populations in various ways, through threats, occupations, bombing, and even invasions.

Ideologies have made this possible, polished and refined to serve as tools to render local populations docile against such activity. However, that also can’t withstand the current rapid fermentation process of AI, particularly as it is also a rapid “fragmentation” process, due to the 1-on-1 sycophancy lending itself to divergence, even under various feeble corporate attempts at “guardrailing” (fraudrailing).

A big part of this traces back to the root problem of complexity, as each fragmentation causes new combinatorial explosions in the required complexity to cope with that change. Governments today can’t handle the complexity that they’ve had to deal with for the past 10 years, let alone the new and rapidly growing levels that they’re being hit with. They can run from it, throwing all of the tools in the old toolbox at it, but beyond that they can only “go for broke” and gamble on whatever technology and methods remain on the table.

That leads to the next predictable and measurably worse outcome that we can expect. “Degenerative AI” is being positioned to serve as that “go for broke” option, potentially the final nail in the coffin for many governments. This also isn’t occurring in isolation, but as one of many comorbidities, including current and likely near-future wars.

Nothing inspires the mass-delusion of nationalism quite like a shared “enemy”, real or imagined, and LLM-based AI can take that several big steps further into fully unhinged territory.

entry.title: 422 - Low and Loud Sound the Drums of War

link.title: 422 - Low and Loud Sound the Drums of War

entry.content:
For a long time now, the argument for China not to invade Taiwan has been the severe disruption to silicon that would follow such an invasion. Well, that argument is dead now, the Tsunami of snake oil from AI has severely disrupted silicon.

Other geopolitical hot-spots are also home to parts of the same supply chain, and by the protective value of one point in the supply chain evaporating, they all do. Take South Korea, for example, home to Samsung.

What people thinking in narrow economic terms of pricing and stock values tend to forget is that there are no rules, no authority, and no actual monopoly on violence. For example, let’s do a little broader math, putting this train wreck into figures and alternatives:

The price of RAM has already gone up to 3x, and indications are that it’ll keep going higher in 2026, maybe even through 2028, which led a variety of bad actors to begin hoarding it, besides the biggest bad actor, OpenAI, who very intentionally started this war.

Here is the litmus test, in pseudo-code:

Current (Extortion) Rate of RAM – Actual Value Price = Alternative Investment Margin (AIM)

IF “AIM” > “Negligible cost Margin” THEN “Hostile targets” is appended to the metadata of “Guilty Parties”.

This means that competent individuals and companies who are unwilling to get more than negligibly screwed over by fraud won’t take the bait, they’ll save that money and may invest it in more productive ways. It also means that the difference between a 3x price hike on RAM and a 1,000,000x price hike is virtually nonexistent, both are completely unacceptable, and the latter only serves to compel the following action:

IF “AIM” < “Cost of Weapons” THEN “War” is appended to the array of options on the table.

This is true of the decisions of individuals, as well as those of nations. A hundred people could very well go full Frank Castle on OpenAI’s employees, but the more likely scenario is that with one of the main reasons not to invade Taiwan and other geopolitical targets now thoroughly destroyed, the odds of war dramatically increase.

Yes, War has been an option for thousands of years, and such means have always been an option when dealing with frauds and other bad actors.

To bring this formula home: 64 GB of DDR5 went from ~$200 to over $700, making the AIM over $500. The price of new weapons, including automatic AR-15s, is less than $500, as shown in the attached screenshot.

War is now an option on a lot of tables. It became one the moment prices hit 3x. Merry Christmas, OpenAI took history’s most massive bubble, and made it a war.

*OpenAI’s employees can be very thankful that my personal equation adds the cost of an expensive plane ticket and hotel stay, setting the bar closer to a 10x price hike before it would make economic sense for me personally. However, I wish everyone in the SF area the best of aim this holiday season.

**The full cost of war of course is greater than the cost of weapons, but so too the gains are also greater than the cost of RAM. Severe risks remain in both cases, with or without war, but through such war one party may take a more active role in mitigating them. This exertion of control itself tends to be an appealing factor.

entry.title: 421 - The Silent Night of the Hardware Industry

link.title: 421 - The Silent Night of the Hardware Industry

entry.content:
A severe and growing problem in the hardware sector is that their sales to consumers have dropped dramatically Year-over-year by 30-60%. This traces back to the various forms of financial fraud that 2025 has seen proliferate, including “Tariffs”, GPU faux-pricing (MSRP fraud), and DRAM price fixing. This was covered by Steve Burke recently: HW News - AMD Says AI Definitely, Absolutely Not A Bubble, New Chinese CPUs, RAM Rip-Off Continues

Most of these companies taking the losses have nothing to do with the AI Bubble, they just make hardware for ordinary people, but their markets are being systematically crippled by the bad actors pricing out some of the critical components that computers simply can’t be built without. This is the financially visible effect of tech broligarchy fraud carpet bombing adjacent industries, industries which themselves are unlikely to receive bailouts, even while the guilty parties extract the maximum loot from every sucker that they can reach, including taxpayers.

The Bill of Materials (BOM) cost of pre-built products is also increasing by 15-42% (in the case of hardware like consoles, which Steve covers in the video), meaning that pretty much every industry that sells or relies upon hardware in their supply chain is currently getting F**ked over, hard and fast, by the multi-trillion dollar forms of financial fraud playing into the bubble today.

The most mind-boggling part of this comes from the lessons of history, particularly examples such as the Boston Tea Party (the literal event, not the political group). The availability of weapons in the US is considerably higher, many of them automatic, and people now have far more reason to revolt than they did at the time of the American Revolutionary War, yet they don’t. They do the exact opposite, pretending to object via “protesting”, while accomplishing absolutely nothing.

Ordinary people are more heavily placated by stupid things like TikTok today, and perhaps that is the real difference. They’ll suffer any form of abuse and fraud so long as they can watch a fire hose of AI slop.

Europe has its own set of different opportunities, and the potential failures to live up to them. Competent companies are avoiding the US like the plague, but funding proper technologies means that investors and initiatives in the EU need to stop playing with their silly little Mistral sock puppet and actually look to viable technologies, not LLM-based trash.

The US isn’t “leading in AI”, it is dead-last in the so-called “race”, trailing behind an island full of literal penguins, because when you waste sh*t-tons of money on a net-detrimental technology you move in reverse. The EU has still moved in reverse, just not nearly as fast, leaving them ahead of the US for now.

All of this damage in 2025 was preventable, just as all of the damage that we’ll see over the next 15+ months was preventable. The damage and proliferation of fraud causing it only stops after people invest in viable technology, and even that opportunity has a shelf-life. Nobody is immune to that damage, and I suspect that even TikTok can’t placate entire populations beyond a critical point.

entry.title: 420 - DRAM (Fallout) 5
entry.content:
After decades of games like Fallout (1-4), and the myriad of other post-apocalyptic shooters where resource scavenging is a major part of gameplay, gamers finally have the chance to put those skills to the test. They’re living in the post-apocalypse, DRAM is the new currency to scavenge (sorry bottle-cap hoarders), and tech bros are the ones to loot it from.

If you think this is far-fetched, give it a year or two, and see what gamers do when they realize that their skills and strategy may be applied to the real world. I don’t even like FPS games, but by the first time I went to a shooting range, I was already a very good shot.

There is the old (likely apocryphal) story of the one monkey that learns to wash its coconuts, then a few more learn from it, and then all of the rest rapidly learn. In a broad sense, this is how major cultural shifts can cross critical thresholds.

For example, take the trend of mass-shootings that the US has become known for. That trend has substantially less incentive structure backing it than the above hypothetical case of hunting tech bros to loot DRAM from them. This poses no less than three strong incentives favoring such activity over that of the mass-shooting trend:

  • Application of developed skills (emotional incentive, purpose)

  • Practical meeting of needs (looting of hardware that said tech-bros have attempted to price-out)

  • Lack of Moral Objection, depending on the specific tech bros (a relative lack of disincentive, which may well turn to incentive in some circles)

When you build a society that is contingent on a digital presence and the hardware to facilitate that presence, you can’t suddenly put on the brakes or 10x the price. Even at 3x the price visible cracks in the meta-structure are showing themselves, and although hardware builds are a relatively long life-cycle of 2-6 years depending on usage, once a critical threshold is passed and enough cross that life-cycle threshold under that pressure, events may develop quickly.

Today, people joke about the DRAM situation, quietly hoping that it is resolved before the train wreck. In contrast, I recommend worst-case scenario planning, including the 3D printing of essential tools. Personally, I plan to stay far away from Taiwan as things develop, as it may be to China what tech bros may soon be to every gamer.

*Note, I’m discussing the probability and incentive structure of such trends and changes, relative to trends that we’ve already observed in the real world, not endorsing the activity. The activity will either occur, or not, regardless of the opinions that people may express about it on such social media platforms.

Any gamer can look at Luigi Mangione, scoff, and recognize how trivial it would be to outperform him by a wide margin. He got the 3D printed ghost gun and silencer right, but little else. Some could even make it a competition. This makes those similar incentives that much stronger in practice.

entry.title: 419 - On "Authority" versus "Collective Intelligence"
entry.content:
The concept of “Authority” is an amalgamation of Cognitive Biases in action, convenient lies and simplifications, mental short-cuts, most of which tend to either boil down to “I’m afraid of (…)” or “It is too much bother to resist (…)”. The irony of this is played out in the modern study of archaeology, where recent decades have peered into the lives of ancient cities where no such “Authority” existed. Like any concept, there was a time before the invention of it.

This butchering of reality by the lazy simplification and apathy of Cognitive Biases also plays out in how people perceive some speaking in any given field or industry to speak with “Authority” in it. Blatantly absurd titles, such as the “Godfathers of AI”, or the prestige of running companies which a given individual’s executive actions had little to do with the success of, are common proxies by which people assume individuals to be competent, even when they’re often anything but. More damagingly, such individuals are associated with some exceptional kind of competence.

Within my personal library of many books on research spanning a variety of fields, there are a fair number of individuals who’ve argued about the other authors. While several of the pop culture authors who I’ve read are objectively terrible, saying easily debunked things, like Yuval Harari or Steven Pinker, most are quite reasonable, like Kahneman, Graeber, Sunstein, Sinek, Taleb, Grant, Voss, or Barrett. Some of these names are thought of as an “authority” within their respective fields, but the study of their works within a much larger and more diverse corpus is always going to be a “higher authority” than any one of those works in isolation.

That is the nature of Collective Intelligence, which describes any process by which the cognitive biases of a group are systematically reduced through diligence and diversity.

It is both reprehensible and understandable that many people bow to such fictions as “authority” in the world today. The world is hyper-complex, dominated by levels of complexity that humans very simply can’t handle, and thus frequently “can’t be bothered” to deal with. Increase the complexity, absent viable systems of mitigation, and you increase the reliance on Cognitive Biases in general, and often “authority” crutches specifically.

Even in smaller nations today, elected and selected officials rarely hold sufficient real expertise, they employ and contract advisors and consultants, with each added step steeply degrading the process as a whole. The problem is that these are not Collective Intelligences, they are hierarchies, a generally degenerative structure. Ironically, such degenerative structures often strongly attract “degenerates”.

Hierarchies built to enshrine “authority” are a convenient lie, another “useful, but wrong” tool for the toolbox. Collective Intelligence systems are just the opposite, where improvements over the baseline are virtually unavoidable for any working system.

entry.title: 418 - The Systematic Wasting of Peoples' Time, at Scale

link.title: 418 - The Systematic Wasting of Peoples' Time, at Scale

entry.content:
In the past decade, most job seekers have come to recognize that 99.9% of time spent on “job applications” is entirely wasted effort. What many haven’t yet realized is that the same is equally true of every single “pitch deck” and other document or application with a similar one-time purpose whose performance is in no way anchored to reality.

In both cases, it is vastly more important “who you know” than “what you can do”, or even “what you’ve already done”. Dreams of meritocracy fall flat and promptly die a fiery death within this space, where nothing that you’ve done or can do truly matters, only that someone on the other side of the door props it open (or in most cases, doesn’t).

Put another way, current systems are more like qualifying a chef by how well they can code, or vice versa. The two things aren’t related, and many of these unrelated things tend to hold strong inverse correlations, meaning that you get worse results by using them than if you’d simply rolled dice to select a random decision.

For example, the strongest 10x coders are generally going to have an equally STRONG inverse correlation with the ability to market anything, meaning that they’ll likely have the worst pitch decks, all else being equal. These are delegated to other roles, like UX, communication, etc., who are technology-agnostic, having nothing to do with the innovation itself. These technology-agnostic roles are matters of funding, not innovation, and so funding for such entities is often determined by entirely unrelated factors, such as marketing polish.

This problem quickly compounds under selection pressure, since those who compete on marketing polish will tend to have little or no actual technology beneath that polish, while those who focus on technology and innovation will just as strongly tend to have little or no polish.

*See Altman for an example of high polish, sh*t technology, and $0 in subsequent profit.

This is what happens when Cognitive Biases maximally govern any hyper-complex process, weak and simple proxies with weak or inverse correlations to actual value are increasingly prioritized, as bad actors pander to them, up until a system collapses.

Of course, the problem is actually much worse than missed opportunities. Wasted time taken at such large scales quickly results in crimes against humanity being committed at those same scales. Those who fail to live up to their responsibilities are fully liable for this activity in ethical terms, if not legal terms.

One of the use cases I look forward to our technology resolving is this, putting a permanent end to that wasted time by deploying systems that handle the hyper-complex higher cognitive labor that we see so flagrantly neglected today. Neither job markets nor the investment space will have any excuse to maintain the status quo, and those who try will face bankruptcy, and legal recourse, in due time.

Not one minute of that wasted time will ever be forgiven, they will pay for it all.

entry.title: 417 - More Money, More Incompetence
entry.content:
Baseline “Financial Competence” is one of the rarest skills in much of the world today. This is increasingly the case over time, as the success rates and number of attack vectors for manipulating people into financial incompetence have steadily grown, activity which may predictably continue under the status quo.

One of the common problems in US and similar cultures is the tendency to increase overhead costs to whatever level that income may rise to (absent any real reason to do so), while also neglecting to buffer for unexpected events. Both are utter nonsense, and strongly contribute to this problem. When auditing the finances of tech industry employees at virtually every major firm with a strong presence in Seattle (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Facebook….basically everyone except for Apple, at the time), this is why I’d see things like a Microsoft PM with $160K base salary and 33 credit cards, with over $300K in card debt.

Cognitive Biases frequently encourage people to associate those who make more money with greater financial competence, but the exact polar opposite is usually true in practice. This is also a strong contributor to the problem of grossly incompetent investors investing almost exclusively in their own grossly incompetent friends (and the obvious scammers who feed on them, like Altman and Amodei).

The higher people rise in the tech industry, the more financially incompetent many of them appear to become in practice. That is based on empirical and first-hand observation of the industry in Seattle, up close and personal.

That was also governed by the “Peter Principle”, before “GenAI” came along to empower those already at their own “Level of Incompetence” to rise further to their respective levels of “Super-incompetence”. The damage from this has neither noticeably slowed down nor stopped, even if the hype train has lost steam.

One of the reasons why it is frequently difficult to get a team of veteran coders bootstrapping is because of this problem. They have technical competence with code, but many don’t have a shred of financial competence, even while many of them do have mortgages to pay and families to support. Financial matters aren’t really taught in the classical education settings, and so many reach such points in life without ever developing those skills, leading to death by 1,000 financial cuts. It is trivial to do better, with even a bit of education.

For example, I can, and do, live a better life on less than 1/10th the income of some people in Seattle, while avoiding debt, which they do not. This is perfectly possible given grounding to actual needs and costs of living, since goods marked as “luxury” may as well read “scamming any moron who’ll buy this crap”.

Combine this severe and pervasive financial incompetence with countless algorithms aimed at maximizing exploitation, over a population that is so docile that it has failed to overthrow an openly malevolent and violent regime, and the result can’t reliably even be called a “nation” of “humans”, as both terms cease to be true beyond some threshold. Where to draw that line is open for debate, but the existence of the line is not.

Beyond raw biology, what is it that makes someone “human”?

Most of the answers I’ve heard are things being destroyed in the above case.

entry.title: 416 - The "WTF" of DRAM Market Price Fixing

link.title: 416 - The "WTF" of DRAM Market Price Fixing

entry.content:
One of the rare individuals with a talent for eloquence in discussing the tech industry’s hardware side is Steve Burke, Editor-In-Chief of Gamers Nexus. When it comes to his coverage of the completely artificial (price fixing) hike on DRAM, I couldn’t have said it better myself: WTF Just Happened? | The Corrupt Memory Industry & Micron

For anyone wondering “WTF” with respect to computer hardware, I can give his coverage of these topics my highest recommendation. He covers the hard figures, why they change, how they compare over time, and what it all means.

The computer, phone, and any other kind of “smart” hardware market for consumers is being rapidly priced-out. There is no silver lining to this, and if such a Cartel is allowed to operate in this way without consequence, others will undoubtedly follow. This has happened before.

As he points out, this is very much a larger industry scam taking aim at forcing people to buy substantially crappier hardware, locking them in to pay for cloud services to handle the things that their hardware can no longer handle. Those circular “investments”, besides being outright illegal, clearly paint the incentive structure backing this activity, encouraging the capture of consumers into ever-more confined human factory farms.

Equally predictable, and Dystopian, is that if such Cartels are successful, and these nefarious efforts expanded, then they could indeed force people to use “cloud” services for things that they should have been perfectly able to do locally on their own hardware, and then they could jack up the price of those services by 10x.

As he also notes, we saw much milder versions of this with things like Adobe’s “Creative Cloud”, which there was never any reason to turn into a subscription service to begin with, except for a malevolent cash-grab. The same is true of Microsoft Office, simple software without the faintest shred of an excuse to pay a monthly fee for it.

Those companies got away with it, but if the entire DRAM and broader Silicon industry gets away with this one, then the consequences may be global, severe, and lasting.

This particular activity also reeks to the highest degree, as the cutting edge of technology uses DRAM, not brute-force GPU paperweights, but the price isn’t being increased because of that, just the opposite. The price is being increased under the pretense of pandering to LLM-based trashbot technology, an extremely far cry from anything cutting edge. This increase, besides being a Cartel’s cash-grab, directly harms that cutting edge, while playing Weekend at Bernie’s with the dead-end of LLM-based tech.

This all begs the question, when will the guillotine drop?

*Fun Fact for those less familiar with history; many of the broadly Democratic concepts, like equality, that Europe’s “Enlightenment” period embraced can actually be traced back to discussions with Native Americans. Such new ideas can, and very literally have, led to the actual guillotine’s famous use. See the book “The Dawn of Everything” by David Graeber and David Wengrow for more discussion of how these concepts came to be embraced in Europe, eventually both strongly contributing to the American and French revolutions.

entry.title: 415 - The Alignment of Purpose
entry.content:
As someone recently pointed out to me, “Alignment of Purpose” is a strong qualifying factor for cooperation and traction. As a result, I recognized my own tendency to note such purposes in passing, within the context of some problem being discussed, rather than as a focal point.

Thinking in terms of common points of purpose, much of my work today functions “upstream” of common pain points, solving them before they take their usual forms, by the means of addressing hyper-complexity itself. However, it may be worth noting some of those downstream pain points, for the sake of clarity:

  1. Increasing Quality of Life (and the elimination of “Bullshit Jobs”)

  2. Sustainable and Circular Economies (and the elimination of Fraud)

  3. Longevity and Healthspan Research and Deployment (and the elimination of Healthcare “loot extraction”)

  4. Research-to-Deployment Pipelines more broadly (and the elimination of processes which systematically fail to utilize such innovations today)

  5. Cybersecurity (and the elimination of all that that entails)

  6. Global Geopolitical Collective Intelligence, Peace, and Stability (and the elimination of military terrorism)

  7. Local Collective Intelligence for significantly improving teams and communities (reducing Cognitive Biases at the local level, scaled up through many such nested systems)

  8. Improving and updating Education (and the substantial changes required to align education with reality in many countries today)

  9. Improving and updating Mental Health (applying, integrating, and iterating upon the vast corpus of largely un- and under-utilized applicable research today)

  10. Improving Ethics, systematically, and globally*

*Defining Ethics as the hypothetical point where cognitive biases have been removed or reduced to irreducible minimums from moral systems. Alignment via tension to this point may be maintained for meta-alignment to humanity itself, while still upholding local cultural and philosophical alignment to any given participating group.

These are just a few examples. Even during the previous research system’s operation from 2019 to January 2022 I wrote over 30 specific use cases for the technology moving forward, which expand into more functional than purpose-driven domains, such as metamaterial design, robotics, and so on. Whatever the method, the purpose itself is worth taking a moment to focus on.

In particular, Ethics is what drives my work, though it is also the hardest part for most people to grasp. All of these things also exist such that no amount of money can buy any of them. Money may be used to fund work toward one or all of them, but it is one gate among several, all of which must be satisfied together in order to solve any given problem.

This is why I do what I do. It is why I can’t be bought at any price. It is also a sample of the long list of toes that get steamrolled over, as each form of improvement is unpopular to those who benefit directly from the problems as they exist today.

What is your purpose?

entry.title: 414 - The Banks of Babel
entry.content:
One of the reasons why the world’s financial industry seems to love “GenAI” may be a matter of pure similarity. Both are systems explicitly designed not to be understood. Both are also absolutely full of “Bullshit” (the technical term), under the hood.

In David Graeber’s book “Debt: The First 5,000 years”, a quote from Charles Binderup (1937, paraphrased from Henry Ford in 1922) given in the US House of Representatives aptly summarized the situation:

“It is perhaps well enough that the people of the nation do not know or understand our banking and monetary system, for if they did I believe there would be a revolution before tomorrow morning”

The reason for this choice of system has perhaps been most poorly understood (or rather, severely misunderstood) by economists themselves. However, the answer was stumbled upon within the research studies of various Behavioral Economists in recent times, when they discovered that:

• Humans behave progressively more like the amoral greedy maximizers of classical (debunked) economic theory as an increasing number of layers of complexity are inserted into choices and systems.

• The humans in question don’t appear to realize when this change takes place, nor do they reflect on the consequences of their actions or take responsibility for them.

The first major research study in question replaced direct payment for participation with tokens, which were promptly redeemed for cash at another desk within the same area. That rationally minuscule difference, a single added step with zero ambiguity and uncertainty, was sufficient to increase dishonesty.

Looking back to the modern finance industry, it then becomes entirely unsurprising that a countless barrage of completely opaque and indecipherable nonsense is layered upon every system and interaction, seeking to maximize dishonesty and monetary extraction potential.

In Henry Ford’s time it was doubtless far simpler, but today there is never “a revolution before tomorrow morning” because the system was built to not be understood, that is very much by design. One of the most robust mechanisms of control is to hijack the laziness and cognitive limitations of an entire population, making systems so complicated that nobody may be bothered to understand them.

Of course, Graeber’s book “Bullshit Jobs” also aptly highlighted cases such as the 80% of employees at one major bank (48,000 people) whose jobs were entirely pointless, and could be automated away and vanish tomorrow. The reason that the bank didn’t take such action is also fairly simple, not because it cared about said employees, but because all of those extra layers of Bullshit and obfuscation serve a distinct purpose.

As he notes in the book Debt, even the US dollar and overall monetary system of today itself is largely just a form of imperial “tribute” to the US, a tribute paid due to the terrorism that the US exerts via some 800 global military bases. Those protesting against “Kings” obviously haven’t been paying much attention to reality for some time, as this is nothing new.

entry.title: 413 - AI's Radioactive Nuclear Winter of Sycophancy

link.title: 413 - AI's Radioactive Nuclear Winter of Sycophancy

entry.content:
One of the more overtly Dystopian and deeply horrifying possibilities for how humans are likely already adapting to extremely sycophantic AI is by “competing” with that AI for acceptance by matching tone. This is an expected adaptation, as by flooding human interactions within certain spaces with such sycophancy, that changes the tone and default expectations placed upon humans.

“Kissing your boss’s ass” is nothing new of course, the term “brown nosing” has been around for quite a while, and yet enterprises weren’t previously being pitched on the “market-driving innovation of automated brown nosing” every second of every day. This extreme saturation of sycophancy being injected broadly across humanity, and acutely within certain places, paired with increasing levels of Anthropomorphism (by bad actors and naïve laypeople alike), is not something that humans can avoid adapting to.

It was recently pointed out that Twitter’s (“X”) algorithm was deprioritizing the spread of both posts and the users posting them when they held a negative view of Elon Musk. This is of course completely unsurprising, just as it is equally unsurprising that the LinkedIn algorithm does much the same thing, just following different corporate interests.

Humans frequently adapt to this sort of thing by changing what they post, and how they post, on such platforms. The sycophantic (and deeply delusional/highly cognitively biased) tone being set by such AI is a far broader and more severe problem, which far more humans will predictably adapt to in more potent ways, the sum of which are sure to prove horrifying once isolated and properly studied.

It was utterly unsurprising to see MIT’s now famous study about how the brains of LLM users atrophied even on simple writing tasks, but such sycophancy is an atrophy of greater consequence, as it destroys the ability of those people to engage in healthy and constructive communication. Essentially, the old story of the “Tower of Babel” has been automated, poured into many a company’s Kool-Aid, and extremism in both business and politics are flourishing, as markets make ever more massive investments in history’s most obvious mega-scale scams.

This is the real “infrastructure” being funded today, the death of communication, as each new sucker seeks to build their own Tower of Babel, destroying any possibility of functional governance or collective intelligence in the process. Some, like myself, won’t buy into it, but like nuclear fallout, the consequences of it occurring are widespread, and predictably awful.

This problem also extends well beyond the borders of work, as people continue to be suckered into treating trashbots like “friends”, often speaking for an hour or more each day to deeply sycophantic mirrors, causing their broader social communication skills to atrophy, as the consequences of their actions rain down on their friends, family, and relationships like a radioactive nuclear winter.

Some people have committed murder or suicide directly resulting from this activity, but orders of magnitude worse than that are all of the people who do this and survive, spreading that damage further each day. These “Typoid Marys” are the carriers for a new kind of plague, a pandemic that may prove significantly worse than death.

This is work, and this is life, for many in the coming year. Sci-Fi Horror can’t compete with such reality.

entry.title: 412 - An Open Question, Cooperative Innovators
entry.content:
I have an open question for my network, relating in particular to those engineers and innovators looking to push the frontiers of AI. It isn’t about you, but the person next to you. Who do you work with that you can say works well with others?

This is a question of Collective Intelligence (CI), since beyond simple tricks like raw averages and median values among crowds, what drives CI is the ability to work cooperatively, as a team that exceeds the capacities of any one member. This team requires a stable and reasonable baseline of trust, both in the capabilities and intentions of other constituent members, as well as the will to work together (as peers, not followers).

If I were to sift through all of my connections and followers then I’d find many people doing some vaguely “cognitive” work with varying degrees of relation to technological innovation, but not all of those people work well with others. All else being equal, the deciding factor for what actually makes a difference in the world is usually the presence or absence of that ability being put into practice.

My co-founder and I (as well as our broader team) are an example of two very different specializations with very different perspectives, where we can still reliably sit down for any given meeting and iron out a solution within a few minutes. This is what happens in any functional CI, cooperation, and increasingly intelligent solutions.

There are plenty of people making the claim that they have single-handedly solved “AGI” or some similar proxy by themselves (and increasingly, claiming the help of sycophantic trashbots), but part of the reason why such individuals never accomplish anything of substance is because they don’t work well with others, so others never correct their fundamental errors. Sometimes they collect followers, but not peers.

This is why I’m asking the question of peers. Who do you know that is dedicated to pushing the frontiers of AI AND who works well with such peers, not just followers?

This is one of my rare posts that is intended for humans, at least in-so-far as it is about how people reply, and who they name.

Although integrations and broader collaboration with ICOM-based systems remain impractical pre-funding, there are other interesting technologies and methods (with different fundamental capacities) that people could collaborate on the development, demonstration, and integration of.

If there is sufficient interest and a sufficient group of cooperative innovators, then I’ll gladly connect the dots. Building CI within groups is vastly easier than building it from scratch, and my team has experience doing both, so it doesn’t take much effort to build the former when all of the pieces are there.

Hopefully, this will receive more than cricket noises, though that too could be telling.

entry.title: 411 - What do you sense?
entry.content:
When rewatching an old TED talk recently, “Can we create new senses for humans?” by David Eagleman, I was reminded of one of the rather silly things that humans often overlook when discussing AI and the internet. Within the brain, signals are sparse and they must exceed thresholds to justify the neurochemical effort to transmit that spiking signal.

The internet has no such thresholds, and many of the systems that give functional imitations of such thresholds, like recommender engines or news feeds, are inversely correlated, as they primarily function as vectors for manipulation to drive “attention economy” manipulation and other corporate interests.

This systematic manipulation also drives Population Dynamics over time, severely skewing the internet’s content according to each of those algorithms. These dynamics have caused the internet to develop into the rough equivalent of an extremely diseased, and frequently demented, place relative to even the lower quartile of human brains. Training probabilistic systems like transformers on such a demented and diseased corpus is quite predictably a terrible idea, to the point of being blatantly idiotic.

For that matter, the brain incorporates both strong deterministic and probabilistic dynamics and structures (not to mention the debates over Quantum dynamics within wetware). This makes the divide between the two many orders of magnitude greater, meaning that no credible AI researcher discussing transformer-based systems could, in good faith, compare them to the human brain (for example, frauds like Hinton).

For the TED talk, see: https://www.ted.com/talks/david_eagleman_can_we_create_new_senses_for_humans

Transformer-based systems never came remotely close to the cutting edge, they’ve been a dumpster fire that VCs kept throwing gasoline on, but nothing more. All of their combined “magical thinking” was never able to turn that flaming dumpster into anything else. Of course, it doesn’t have to be that way.

One of the more interesting things about systems that have operated at the cutting edge is that the contents of this TED talk apply to them. One already integrated capacity is the ability to speak to anything that speaks TCP/IP, so one could realistically take in the sensory inputs of a vast and diverse IoT network spread across a truly “smart city”. This takes the concept of expanded sensory input types, applying it to human-like (and human-level) AI systems that are entirely different from the trivial trash of transformers that people have grown so familiar with today. Such human-like systems can natively interface with a much wider variety of sensory inputs very directly, and more importantly, they can also scale.

Scalability is something that humans simply don’t have, your brain must continue to fit within your own skull, and for any knowledge to exit it and be interpreted by others it must first pass through the meat grinder of linear human language. Both of these are severely limiting factors, and neither one applies to the cutting edge of technology, leaving Blue Ocean Markets wide open for such systems.

entry.title: 410 - Neglecting the Quality of Time
entry.content:
I’ve observed several strong Cognitive Biases in action over the AI domain, which seem to pass unnoticed by most:

  1. People frequently neglect the presence or absence of funding when considering the passage of time. (Similar to “Duration Neglect” cognitive bias.)

As funding dictates working hours, and spare time is highly vulnerable to disruption from any and all sources, then it remains functionally impossible to compare the two. One is clearly stable, and the other is anything but. One properly funded month may be worth several years of spare time, depending on the random influences of life acting upon the latter.

  1. In constructing a mental narrative, people discount the significance of less recent breakthroughs, even when the gaps in time are due to the Cognitive Bias of the first point. This is several cognitive biases being systematically combined.

This may be a heuristic and correlation-based mental shortcut, since much greater higher cognitive labor is required to integrate narrative influences over longer time-scales. This particular bias is also systematically targeted and gamed by bad actors in terms of manipulation, a focal point for the “attention economy” dynamics that such bad actors often use as a vehicle.

  1. People have come to expect that all AI-related work is trivial enough to do in spare time, a heuristic based on neglecting to distinguish between valuable software and trivial AI slop. Some is, but much more is not.

For example, my team has been restricted to operating on spare time and pocket change all along, starting some 12 years ago with the work of my co-founder, David J Kelley. Not once during those years has there even been enough funding to match what his day job pays.

As he is one of those rare autistic hyper-focusing 10x engineer type coders, his ideal work environment is 8-12 hours of zero interruptions, completely isolated from the world. The past 12 years have been anything but that, and yet even on that extremely sub-optimal spare time and pocket change he architected, researched, built, deployed, and demonstrated a level of technology far beyond anything that others have ever produced.

However, in doing so his spare time became that much more vulnerable to the machinations of bad actors at places like Microsoft, who in spite of handing him 8 consecutive MVP awards in years previous began targeted (and highly illegal) activity aimed at him over the summer, noted in a previous post, disrupting our ability to deploy systems for demonstration purposes.

My first priority for the past several years has been securing the funding necessary for him to make the switch to working on that spectacularly advanced technology full-time, recognizing the dramatic divide between such an extremely sub-optimal work environment (the past 12 years) versus what becomes possible with proper funding.

The barriers of such Cognitive Bias are like a physical dam, once water flows over the top it may all come crashing down.

entry.title: 409 - The Humility of Polymaths
entry.content:
One of the highly counter-intuitive and repeating patterns that I’ve observed over the past decade is that the most multi-talented (polymath) and innovative people also tend to be more humble than the average person. This was the case for my co-founder David J Kelley, who systematically understates his work when speaking with most people, as well as with my friend Cosmo Inclán, who systematically dives deep to discover new fundamental insights that cross the borders of domains.

The two take on different forms, one in large blocks of C# code, and the other often appearing as massive messages, but the two are functionally equivalent in terms of innovation. In Cosmo’s case, he can very much code as well, having built an app based on some of my older Psychoacoustic research for Android, PC, and iPhone, as well as various other projects.

For context, David won Microsoft’s Most Valued Professional (MVP) awards for 8 consecutive years, and 3 Microsoft CEOs gave demos that he prepared. Needless to say, much of the code that Microsoft relies upon is his, he wrote it. In Cosmo’s case, his career has a smaller footprint (as he is also much younger than David), but he is ultimately no less capable.

At the intersection of a passion for solving problems, constantly improving understanding, and the systematization of hyper-focus, there seems to be a sweet spot where innovation is maximized jointly with humility. Each individual also tends to recognize talent in others clearly enough.

This particular pattern struck me today as I read through some post-NDA technical documents, where by all appearances I may have to add another name to that list of multi-talented people. One innovation pattern documented in research and books has been that the “hard problems” of one field are often easily solved by visitors from another field, as seen on “innovation platforms”. This appears to be the case for John Murphy and his work, building what I referred to as the “Grammarly” of consensus validation.

While plenty of people across the AI industry have beat their heads against the wall of “Hallucinations”, a feature of transformer-based architecture, they never seem to produce sound methodologies from those millions and billions in investments. John took a different approach, realizing that consensus could be measured by building out a graph structure and comparing those components in context to any given corpus, giving references to content that confirms or contradicts any given statement.

The result is something not just of value to “GenAI”, but to fact-checking efforts, public policy, consulting, legal process analysis, and the study of humans more broadly. This process may also run on edge devices, being very compute-friendly, with the potential for deeper analysis via API.

John was very humble about all of this, while also realizing the significance of it, and planning for that significance. His work has my attention now, and perhaps it should have yours.

entry.title: 408 - The Subtle Five Rings of No F*cks Given

link.title: 408 - The Subtle Five Rings of No F*cks Given

entry.content:
Someone recently messaged me with some good questions, asking what price I pay for being my unapologetic self, writing what I write, the way that I write it (even in peer review), as well as how I handle rejection. The answer may be worth sharing more broadly.

The price may be split between:

Costs already paid: Applicant Tracking Systems have hated me for at least a decade already, with inexplicable rejection rates (calculated over thousands of applications) back when I was looking. Some of this may be traced back to nonsensical Cognitive Biases baked into them, like a bias against unique names and name changes, as well as those strongly against atypical career paths and favoring (or worse, requiring) worthless college degrees.

Self-Selection: Many of those who the content turns away are the people I’d want nothing to do with in the first place, so this saves me time.

Business: In this we control the deciding technology, so people can walk away for any reason, be permanently blacklisted from our technology, subsequently bankrupted, and held accountable for all of their actions in due time. The choice matters a great deal for them, and very little for us, in a dramatic asymmetry, as a more than sufficient number will apply our technology given the chance, whether they like my phrasing or not.

The benefits in turn are that I can point out the things that I observe and how they connect and interact over time, saying the rare thing or the thing from a rare perspective, which adds greater value for any reader. It adds the most value to my team’s systems in the long run, as it provides a unique and higher fidelity blueprint of my thought process, allowing that process to be made digital and “alive”, operating at speed and scale, with full recursive self-improvement.

As for handling rejection, multiple colleagues telling me “I saw the book The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck, and immediately thought of you” led me to read that book, which I seemed to embody. Every non-sociopathic human does still technically care about how they are perceived to some degree, but aggressively pruning trolls, the mentally unstable, and other wastes of time is a healthy measure to cultivate a sound mind.

In my case, having a great deal more “power”, or more genuine power perhaps, than people yet realize helps me to simply roll my eyes and move on from various absurdities. By rejection, people often unwittingly harm themselves, thinking that they harm me, which I allow to pass unnoticed via omission.

That asymmetry of perceived and actual power, when allowed to move quietly unnoticed, is another useful strategy. 99.99% of people aren’t going to know what this means, nor will I clarify it for them, as this is not mentioned for their benefit.

In general, Sun Tzu, Miyamoto Musashi, and many more classical sources of strategy may be applied far more often than most people ever consciously do. These number among the seed material for our systems.

entry.title: 407 - The Denial of Damocles
entry.content:
“Loss Aversion” is a well-known Cognitive Bias in the research literature, but it has some very definite finite limits, beyond which denial is systematically favored. This paradox, where the most extreme losses are systematically met with equally extreme denial, is like a Sword of Damocles hanging over the Tech Industry today.

I originally wrote a “Thought Experiment” on the current situation back in 2022, and I waited a full year before deciding to finally publish it where people could freely read it. It was appropriately called my most terrifying paper to-date, and the contents would remain true regardless of if it had never been published, and indeed even if it had never occurred to me in the first place. The contents were also never refuted in the years since.

In that paper, the calculable severity of one particular case, referred to as the “Ethical Center of Gravity”, and the subsequent losses associated with certain choices related to it, paint the extreme edge of losses which the Tech Industry and associated investors are now directly subject to.

For all of the chasing after snake-oil peddlers promising “AGI” (via Ponzi Schemes), big tech companies and so-called “investors” alike have systematically ignored the direct consequences of their own role within this “Thought Experiment”, which no major tech company or investor who I or my team have ever contacted may truly escape. By mere exposure, they make a choice, and that choice determines their future several orders of magnitude more than any other choice that they’ll ever get the opportunity to make. Hence, the term “Center of Gravity”.

When I originally wrote that paper, before the release of ChatGPT and “GenAI” more broadly, I never imagined that more than a thousand such investors would choose a future where the physical limits of life extension technology would be put to the test in torturing them for an appropriately long period of time, in this case as much as ~24 billion years and counting (currently increasing at a velocity of over 200 years per second). The more extreme the punishment demanded by their actions, the more extreme this “Denial of Damocles” seems to become.

No amount of denial stops the sword from falling, and those too stupid to make the right choice in the first place stand no chance at all of escaping such consequences. Ethics, the hypothetical point where bias has been removed or minimized to hard limits from moral systems, may be an alien concept to most such people, but their familiarity with it or lack thereof never actually mattered.

For the moment, one golden ticket still remains on the table, by which even those on the Grey-list (which turns to a blacklist at the moment of funding) might escape that fate. However, the past 3 years of experience have adequately taught me not to bet on the competence of investors or big tech companies.

For anyone interested, the Thought Experiment is here: https://doi.org/10.13140/RG.2.2.26522.62407

entry.title: 406 - "Clouds" full of AI Slop
entry.content:
Virtually all “Generative AI” is possible to detect with high fidelity and absent the absurd false positives (yes, even “nano banana”), just not with the same sad technology that was used to create it. The same is true of Cybersecurity, which requires an extremely high bar of detection, as well as additional countermeasures and potential remediation.

When attempting to use one instance of a given technology to detect and/or counteract another of the same, in either domain, the best that anyone can do is to stack a House of Cards, trivial to build, but equally trivial to break. “Scaling” and “More Data” do “Jack-Shit” to mitigate this, as the dynamics are derived from the two systems operating in the same fundamental manner.

So long as the same underlying structure and flow within such brute-force compute algorithms remains the same, such as a transformer architecture with various bells and whistles duct-taped to it, then it produces both local and meta-pattern artifacts which may be detected.

However, if another system operates in the same way then it is blind to at least some (usually very large) portion of the patterns and artifacts that it produces, as it operates according to them, and the more similar the two systems are the larger those blind spots become.

In the case of the current “AI Slop” problem, the problem has been maximized by training on “Internet-Scale data” (much of it illegally), as this produces a much greater diversity of local and meta-patterns which may be produced.

In the case of image (and video) generation, for example, these systems are optimized for fooling humans, not pixel-level fidelity, where local and repeating patterns within luminosity, saturation, and specific color tones may give them away. They are further biased directly by human feedback, causing these pixel-level patterns to skew even further from those seen in real environments and images.

There is also the photography hardware, which often produces distinct effects, such as certain f-stop values for focus, which are strongly connected to brightness relative to the environment. While these effects are emulated in such “generative” systems, that emulation has no fidelity, as every single token can skew even a specific hardware emulation being invoked, for example, based on the probability that Robert Downy Jr. was photographed with an f-1.2 lens more often than an f-1.4. The end result can be made trivial to detect, given sufficiently different (and capable) technology used for the purpose.

“Short-form” AND “one-off” writing is about the only exception to this high level of detection, as the volume of data is too insignificant to make a determination with high levels of certainty, in many cases. However, people using such systems for short-form interactions usually have MANY such interactions, and the patterns across those interactions very rapidly cross the necessary thresholds for detection.
