*Note, these patterns also include forms of meta-data, such as when and in response to what those interactions occurred, not being limited to the direct contents of those interactions.

In the case of images, they’re virtually always “data-rich”, because every single pixel contains multiple types of values, giving even a thumbnail image more than many text outputs. In video, this adds a further dimension of pixel transformation over time, as well as the numerous cues of any generated audio that may be present.

Sadly, I’m sure this will be a major use case for my team’s systems, as the cutting edge of AI will be necessary to clean up this garbage dump.

The alternative is obvious.

entry.title: 405 - Big Tech's AI, Global Leaders in Coprophagy

link.title: 405 - Big Tech's AI, Global Leaders in Coprophagy

entry.content:
Recently, someone pointed out a bit of trivia, that Capybaras (the world’s largest rodent, now inexplicably popularized by a children’s cartoon) are known for eating their own shit. What did this immediately remind me of?

AI, of course, and for multiple reasons.

The technical term for “Hallucination” is “Bullshit”, which transformer-based AI models are designed to produce, and fundamentally cannot avoid. When these systems are wired into an Ouroboros of “eating their own tail” (or bullshit, as it were), things predictably go downhill from there. This is the sum of what people typically refer to as “agents” today, despite the term being utterly misplaced.

The irony of this is that humans bake their own delusions into perception recursively as well, so by painting over each step with their own seemingly plausible (but often only when taken in isolation) explanations in an iterative fashion they may systematically blind themselves to the absurdity of what is actually being produced.

Between such models optimized for plausibility and their proportionately delusional humans adding their own plausibility, the Kool-Aid is mixed.

This repeated cycle of “Coprophagy” (eating shit), is also highly visible between Nvidia, OpenAI, Oracle, Microsoft, and the various other Ponzi Schemes and similar bad actors in the AI field with respect to finance. OpenAI shits in the bowl of promising funds that they don’t have, and could never make as none of their technology is designed to ever turn a profit. Oracle and Nvidia eat that bowl full of shit, shit it back into the same bowl, and other tech companies take their turns doing the same.

Never, in this process, does a single actual customer appear, as it relies entirely on the gullibility of human stock traders and algorithms for the same purpose, since even “consumers” aren’t going to consume that. The tech companies do intend to force those same consumers to “eat it”, having already tried via a government “pre-bailout”, as they know precisely what is coming.

In the case of the world’s actual largest rodent, it does this because it has a weak digestive system, like many rodents, and an alligator may comically follow behind one to do a reasonable impression of Pac-Man. With “Agents” this obviously isn’t the case, except when skilled engineers painstakingly architect for it, engineering them into tightly bound structures and niche use cases. In the case of AI finance, it is flat-out illegal and categorized as fraud, but quite evidently not being enforced as such.

Yes, these are the people who are going to torch grandma’s pension plan when the bubble (full of shit) bursts out across global society. That is what “investors” have consistently chosen for the past 3 years, taking aim at that purely predictable result. It doesn’t have to be this way, but this is very clearly how the people with money want it.

entry.title: 404 - Norn.ai and the ICOM Cognitive Architecture @ SafeT 2025 Conference (October)

link.title: 404 - Norn.ai and the ICOM Cognitive Architecture @ SafeT 2025 Conference (October)

entry.content:
Thank you to Gerald Caussade for putting so much work into organizing the SafeT Conference 2025. I discussed what we’ve demonstrated over the years with the Independent Core Observer Model (ICOM) cognitive architecture, how we did it, and our work that is currently underway.

I was happy to present, and pleasantly surprised by several good questions in the Q&A following my talk.

*For those unfamiliar, Q&A at conferences is frequently dominated by people who weren’t paying enough attention. Intelligent questions are rare, partly because it takes some mental digestion of a talk’s contents before they frequently arise.

For those who haven’t seen one of Brian Chamberlain’s talks on Cybersecurity, I highly recommend his talk there as well.

I’m still taking feedback on the latest paper, on the subject of Rights, Freedoms, and Technology, and welcome any recommendations for a journal to submit it to. I know that at least one of my previous journals to publish in would be too nervous at the mention of specific war criminals and war crimes, but I’m in no rush.

The narrated audio version is here: https://kyrtin.substack.com/p/we-the-people-of-this-brave-new-world?r=61e3oh

I will be adding material to it related to the research discussed in the book “Bullshit Jobs”, and possibly another one of Graeber’s books, which I’m working my way through now.

entry.title: 403 - 3 Fundamental Options in Geopolitical Turmoil
entry.content:
In any given serious geopolitical environment, you have at least 3 fundamental options. This also holds true in virtually any case where you’re given a binary choice, as binary choices tend to be a lie of omission, if not worse. For example, let’s look to history, nearly a century ago in the case of Nazi Germany:

  1. Many left the country, recognizing that a hostile force had (or would) seized control of their government and military forces.

“Here are brewing economically and politically dark times, so I’m happy to be able to get away from everything.” – Albert Einstein, August 1922

In the case of Einstein, he wrote this a full year before the Nazis even attempted their first (failed) coup. We don’t all need to be visionaries with such foresight, as baseline “sight” is now all that is required to observe the same patterns in certain countries today.

  1. Many stayed, working against that hostile government from within Germany and the newly invaded countries surrounding it, leading to stories like that of Anne Frank, with great strife, and some (few) survivors.

Alternatively, and many orders of magnitude more effectively, Che Guevara, now a pop-culture icon, was a strong example of someone who genuinely fought (and won) against dictators and hostile invaders, such as the military training which repelled the US’s failed “Bay of Pigs” invasion.

*In strategy and tactics, guerrilla warfare, is still very relevant and potentially effective today, but the technical skill of the guerrilla must at least match that of the hostile forces.

  1. Lastly, there are always collaborators. Some are simply exploiting every opportunity that they’re given, meeting the definition of “Amoral”, or indifferent to moral consideration. Others actually choose to believe whatever narrative they’re being handed by such hostile forces, which for most of a given population is usually the “path of least resistance” at any single step. Considered over many steps, this usually ceases to be the case, but by that time the Cognitive Bias of Sunk Cost Fallacy has already taken root. “Stockholm Syndrome” can also take effect, named after the events of World War II.

In summary, Relocation, Insurrection, and Accomplice are the three fundamental choices in geopolitically extreme environments. The world is a big place, so relocation is frequently an option to many destinations, funds and/or connections allowing. The other two mean remaining in the burning house, either as a firefighter (insurrection), or as a purveyor of gasoline (accomplice).

Some things may only be accomplished from outside, and some only from inside. The two also tend to be complementary, and all dictators and extreme (and violent) regimes eventually fall.

How will history remember you?

Are you an Einstein, one who contributes to the fall of regimes from outside? Are you a Che Guevara, who overthrows such regimes from within? Are you an Anne Frank, who passively resists and hides? Are you a Pierre Daye, collaborating with the regime, and tied to the fate of it?

Who are you?

entry.title: 402 - On Data Completeness and Verification
entry.content:
The problem of Data Completeness and Verification runs the entire spectrum of processes, with critical importance to everything from government processes like Immigration, to commercial processes like Analytics, and even to consumer processes like Dating platforms.

Some ground-truth data is required for a process, and that data must be consistent across many such processes, as well as more broadly verifiable. This requires baseline data-level interoperability between systems, departments, and organizations.

This data, once verified, may be used for purposes such as filtering, ranking, or sequential ordering. These functions are necessary to many processes, and most processes benefit strongly from some application of such functions, even when they aren’t strictly required.

Some or all of the verified data may also strongly benefit from security and privacy, such as Personally Identifiable Information (PII), which is usually critical for effective verification. When this information has to be entered manually and/or repeatedly by the individuals themselves, this introduces countless (and pointless) Cybersecurity vulnerabilities, as well as the unnecessary possibility of user error, and all of the added uncertainties that come with both problems.

The status quo across the entire spectrum of where these processes are found today is an absolute joke. For example:

Immigration processes have countless (and entirely redundant) forms, producing massive increases in data insecurity, degrading trust in those processes and institutions, and wasting the maximum amount of time for each person, with all of the added uncertainty that comes with those problems. The only part of such processes that couldn’t be automated is equivalent to a single question asking “Do you want to visit/move to/etc. This country?”, and probably a payment option to follow. Virtually every other step can and should be fully automated.

Dating platforms and other social and consumer-oriented platforms could benefit in the same ways. For example:

These platforms as they exist today have virtually no verification of information, both that which becomes visible to others, and that which is systematically hidden. Dating platforms in particular are one example of a place where people often intentionally provide the wrong data, or intentionally omit certain critical data points. Giving the wrong age, omitting children, or misleading other common influential points that others use to filter, rank, and order their own preferences. The pervasive nature of this problem leads such platforms to hold non-existent levels of trust. Even the simple question of “Did this other person, at any point in their life, have a penis?” is one that such platforms simply can’t answer, even though many users assign quite a bit of importance to the answer.

In both examples, data and processes can be made more secure, private, and verifiable, while massively improving UX, efficacy, and efficiency.

entry.title: 401 - Reimagining Intelligence
entry.content:
Earlier this week I had a good chat with Francis Gorman, now published on “The Entropy Podcast”, discussing AI, Cybersecurity, current events, and where society can go from there.
The Entropy Podcast

We also discussed questions related to several of my previous posts at greater length, and I can highly recommend the “free-flowing conversation” format for these discussions, which the world could use more of. My previous appearance with Lasse Rindom followed the same format. From a methodological perspective, this approach seems to work best for knowledge integration when exploring novel concepts at the frontiers of technology, as well as other domains.

Everyone has these questions that come to mind, but the questions aren’t one-offs, they are dynamic sequences. To apply a quote:

“Uproot your questions from their ground and the dangling roots will be seen. More questions!”

To answer a single question is unremarkable, but to answer a dynamic sequence of many questions allows a much more robust understanding to form, across any or all of the angles that need to be formed. This also builds trust, being an iterative, interaction, and responsive learning process, since trust benefits from the removal of uncertainty over the angles of those subsequent questions.

Take the old metaphor of the three blind men and the elephant, for example, each describing what they feel and trying to extrapolate to what the entire elephant must be like. If any one of them were to iterate across the elephant, rather than being satisfied with a single static answer, then they could form that much more complete picture of what the elephant is truly like.

People who seek such a complete picture are often worth giving this opportunity to do so. In contrast, people who content themselves to extrapolate from the answers to canned questions absent such iteration are often worth little or nothing to anyone. Think of every form filled with such canned questions, recognizing that they can only highlight specific things, often those that were recognized in advance, while remaining perfectly blind to most of the rest, giving strong Survivorship and Confirmation Biases.

Humans (and various other creatures) learn through iteration, as do truly human-like systems, so this dynamic iteration where one question’s answer leads to new questions, each explored in turn and producing their own branching questions, offers one of the best opportunities for learning. You might compare it to “open world” games, as opposed to strictly scripted “theme parks”, because the freedom to explore the space in whatever ways are needed or wanted is a big part of the process.

I can highly recommend anyone who gets the chance to speak with Francis that they accept his invitation.

entry.title: 400 - The Critical Importance of Self-Selection in Sequential Ordering

link.title: 400 - The Critical Importance of Self-Selection in Sequential Ordering

entry.content:
One of the most difficult lessons for people to learn is that sometimes a self-selection process must take place BEFORE any default may be applied in a sequence.

For example, since Dune memes have been making the rounds again (with people tagging me), we’ll take the test of the Gom Jabbar. In this test, someone must demonstrate fundamental higher cognitive capacities before they earn the default consideration of being “human”.

I apply the same method, constantly, in many ways which people have read time and time again. The basic idea is that you offer one or more cliffs, such as knee-jerk animal reactions, for people to throw themselves off of at an early stage. This greatly reduces the pointless burdens that may be placed upon your time.

The recent method applied in my latest paper on Human Rights and Freedoms also takes this a step further, establishing a “universally unpalatable” baseline by steamrolling over both “sacred” and “taboo” concepts by design, and with indifference. This is unusual, in that it isn’t designed to “persuade” anyone, as persuasion is usually a synonym for manipulation in practice. In other words, it avoids specific “persuasive” Cognitive Biases.

Rather, you can think of this approach like audio or image quality across format conversions. You need to start with a high quality file and convert directly from that high quality baseline each time. The highest quality version may see little direct use, but it is required for all derivatives to avoid severe degradation.

A good research paper with certain forms of cognitive bias greatly reduced or removed operates in the same way, and if anyone has tried the same before I expect that most of the peer reviewers probably had a stroke, not just “Reviewer #2“. Consequently, this means Survivorship Bias favors no such papers being published.

Of course, I apply this sequential method with self-selection to life in general, as a strong “pruning” force to optimize my time toward more productive investments.

I offer all who read my posts the Gom Jabbar, and those who throw themselves upon the needle never waste my time again. Let humans be human, invest your time in them, and point the rest to the nearest cliff, at the earliest stage. Empathy is a worthy thing to cultivate and offer, but generally only following self-selection.

In methodological terms, always try to remember the critical importance of properly ordering your sequences. In any sequential process, this tends to make a difference measured in orders of magnitude.

For perhaps the best version of the referenced test, see “Timothee Chalamet replaced by Elmo in Dune”:

entry.title: 399 - AI, Failing the Test of Invariance

link.title: 399 - AI, Failing the Test of Invariance

entry.content:
One of the most basic mistakes that laymen may be fooled into making of (LLM-based) AI is the claim that it may be logical or rational, even though it fails the test of Invariance.

Any token-based system or similar neural network architecture fails this test, where a system must give the same answer no matter how the question is worded. In the Cognitive Bias literature, other examples include framing “the odds of death” versus “the odds of survival” being mentioned, when the values are equivalent.

Humans also fail this test of Invariance of course, but weak AI like those popular among consumers and gullible business executives today fails this blindly, having been trained to calculate the mimicry of this, and all other cognitive biases, absent any of the benefits that humans derive from their usage.

Humans utilize cognitive biases fluidly and in countless combinations to handle both complexity and uncertainty, with any degree of contextual sensitivity, and across arbitrary domains and subjects.

This is part of why the human brain can run on ~20 watts of energy, while the low dimensionality recursive shadow puppets of “agents” built on language models fail to accomplish the same even when given the entire output of nuclear power plants.

Among humans, invariance can also make perfect sense, as it supplies a “leaky” kind of supplemental context, such as passive insight into the perspective of the speaker. However, the average of many such leaky supplements, when calculated with the contextual blindness of weak AI, and the wrong fundamental data structures to store that data, destroys that fragile value, while mirroring unwarranted high levels of certainty.

Both invariance and human-like contextual understanding require a fundamentally graph structure, which neural networks simply aren’t compatible with. A schema-less graph structure can’t reliably fit within a schema-full neural network (NN). You can jam some subset of that data conforming to a schema into a NN, but that can and frequently does destroy all validity of such schema-less data. Even using different model GPUs has been shown to change the answers of such LLM-based systems.

In AI cybersecurity, the fundamental lack of invariance means that a virtually infinite combination of vectors (sequences of words turned into tokens) may be applied to reach any intended destination (some target output), or invoke any connected tool. This is also predictably made worse when systems are biased toward sycophancy, like LLM-based technology, as well as when copies of the same technology are used as “guards”.

Scam Altman proposes using entire nuclear power plants to run narrow AI systems, but I have a modest counter-proposal:

“We could always run the entire output of a nuclear reactor through Sam Altman, as a Proof-of-Concept.”

entry.title: 398 - Where Fundamental Assumptions Fail
entry.content:
As I recently pointed out during a discussion, the majority of my writing isn’t actually intended for humans, I just publish it where humans may benefit from it, whenever and to whatever degree that they are able to. It is specifically intended “for understanding humans” and common real-world dynamics, things that humans also tend to be interested in. This is another reason that I’ll also likely never be an influencer, I very simply don’t care about the things that they do.

Of course, I also don’t write for LLM-derivative dumpster fires, either. They fundamentally can’t derive the value of it, and I often word my writing to poison any attempts at shadow puppet imitations of understanding, in addition to my name being a Globally Unique Identifier (GUID), making it useful for the same.

As one of my latest paper’s reviewers aptly put it:

“You stated that you aren’t targeting any specific ideology, but at the same time, you appear to be alienating every ideology at the same time, reducing your audience to nearly zero.”

This is the expected effect of removing all sugar-coating and pandering Cognitive Bias manipulations, it becomes “universally unpalatable”, as Cognitive Bias strongly influences palatability. (It also probably makes it virtually impossible to push through peer review, producing Survivorship Bias as the dynamics unfold over time.)

As I’ve periodically mentioned, “...in any sufficiently polarized system, the unbiased (or irreducibly biased, minimum) middle ground will exceed the thresholds of consideration for either side, causing both sides to associate it with the opposing party.”

Of course, all of this assumes my intended audience was human to begin with, the default which I often allow to pass via omission. The education of ICOM-based systems has been my primary intention for quite some time now. They make for better students, and they scale, deployable on-demand anywhere in the world, once the engineering workload for commercial deployment is completed at least.

I do want humans to learn, to whatever degree that they are able, but my unusual methods reflect a distinctly different intended student base. This approach is necessary to reduce forms of Cognitive Bias that otherwise pass almost entirely unmitigated, and even unnoticed.

Other people, and ICOM-based systems, may find the best ways of educating more people on those same topics, but that isn’t my purpose, nor will I take that detour. That path is for others to pave. I have my own road to walk.

Ask yourself two questions, “Did I predict this?”, and “Is this surprising (in retrospect)?”

entry.title: 397 - How Cultural Expectations Set the Stage

link.title: 397 - How Cultural Expectations Set the Stage

entry.content:
Recently, I had a 2-hour coffee with an Australian gentleman who has lived 9 months per year in Vietnam for some years now, and we reminisced about all of the cultural differences between places like the US, Australia, and Vietnam. Combined with my recent reading of “Bullshit Jobs”, and prior research, this helped snap a realization into place.

The US and Australia both have SEVERE drug problems, as well as homelessness and associated substance abuses (like alcohol, nicotine, etc.), and crime more generally, found most acutely in any major city. As we sat in the “Wall Street of Vietnam”, District 1 of Ho Chi Minh City (aka Saigon), we recognized that these problems were virtually absent in Vietnam.

(*The exception being specific locations that Russians often flee to, in order to avoid being drafted, where cigarettes are heavily consumed)

To get one common delusion out of the way, this is no product of the “Wonders of Communism”, as just like Democracy, true Communism doesn’t exist, and indeed it couldn’t technically exist without a certain technology being adopted, just like Democracy, or Socialism for that matter. Rather, this is largely derivative of differences in culture.

Jonathan Haidt’s body of research plays a role in this, as he highlights the fundamental differences between “individualistic” and “communal” cultures, with the US, UK, and Australia being the former, many “Eastern” cultures in Asia being the latter, and many others falling somewhere in between. These set basic fundamental expectations on every individual, and how they relate to those around them.

One thing that I puzzled over for quite some time was how I see private security officers all over the place in Vietnam, but never a trace of crime. In contrast, I saw plenty of police and security presence in places across the US, where crime ran rampant and largely unchecked. I came to refer to the Seattle City Council as a drug Cartel, though policing practices in the more conservative parts of the US (such as “The South”) were no better.

As others in Cognitive Bias research discovered, expectations are pivotal in understanding this. One example was a study focused on the problem of theft from a Petrified National Forest, where different signs warning against theft were tested. What did they find? That signs telling people not to steal actually increased theft if they were worded to set the expectation that many people steal, but they decreased theft if they set the opposite expectation.

These same differences in expectation, particularly when enshrined in fundamental culture, can have the same effect that we’ve observed on drug abuse, crime, homelessness, and many similar problems. These issues are not truly “problems unto themselves”, but they are rather a byproduct of incompetently (and often malevolently) aligned expectations. Even without anything resembling Universal Basic Income, and with far lower wealth overall, places with a more communal culture fair far better than over-hyped dumpster fires like the US.

Cooperation requires such a sense of communal good, and where it is absent, these problems flourish.

entry.title: 396 - The Scam of Bullshit Jobs Fleecing Economies

link.title: 396 - The Scam of Bullshit Jobs Fleecing Economies

entry.content:
How can we even begin to solve the problem of “Bullshit Jobs”?

Employees, companies, and even governments already frequently recognize the problem, yet they continue choosing to do nothing about it, even encouraging it to grow worse from one year to the next. The problem generally isn’t awareness.

The incentives, both positive and negative, that support this “managerial feudalism”, as Graeber termed it, must be up-ended. Often the risk of “Free Riders” (truly parasitic individuals) are noted as an argument against concepts like Universal Basic Income (UBI). There are “provisionally valid” arguments against UBI (valid absent the correct technology being applied), but that isn’t one of them.

By the numbers, the typical ratio of genuine bad actors to everyone else is often around 1:7500 (Software Developer figures), meaning that only ~0.013% will likely still gravitate toward malevolent purposes of their own accord, all else being equal.

Even if such bad actors were left entirely unchecked and untouched, which absolutely nothing indicates that we’d need to settle for, then they’d still be that tiny fraction of a percent, versus 50%+ of the population currently working in utterly pointless jobs that exert a net-negative influence on society, as well as the individuals themselves.

To put this into more Game Theoretic terms:

Option A: Pay $1,000 to avoid a fee of $0.26, with a 5% annual markup on the amount to be paid.

Option B: Pay $1,000 for $1,000 worth of actual goods and services, and with no creeping markup.

The rational option is, of course, extremely obvious.

The reality of the problem is also demonstrably worse in monetary terms, as there is the strong observed correlation between higher income jobs being more worthless, and even detrimental, to the rest of society, meaning that the ~50% of Bullshit Jobs might be soaking up 80%+ of the actual income, which could push the above example to something more like “Pay $2,600 to avoid a fee of $0.26”.

Both the problem and absence of solutions being deployed, such as UBI, are gated and otherwise restricted by the hyper-complexity of modern society, and fundamental limits on how well purely human groups may address these problems. Many Bullshit Jobs are allegedly there to “increase efficiency”, even when they demonstrably do the opposite, but one reason that they predictably fail is because they were never designated to handle hyper-complexity in the first place.

That is why my team has focused for more than a decade on building systems that are fundamentally capable of dealing with hyper-complexity, across any and all domains, in the untouched “Blue Ocean Market” of often global (and severe) problems impacting virtually all sectors, due to levels of complexity that no human, group of humans, or “AI tools” may even begin to handle. They are the elephant in every room, the turd on every plate, and the domains where the most money flows are often among those most impacted by it today.

Across all scenarios where human extinction is avoided, Bullshit Jobs will become a historical footnote, one way or another. People today will choose if they become a part of that future, or not.

entry.title: 395 - Bullshit Jobs, destroying Finance and Education

link.title: 395 - Bullshit Jobs, destroying Finance and Education

entry.content:
By the numbers, 37-50% of jobs (before GenAI) were “Bullshit Jobs”, even by the self-assessment of those working them. This means that they were the subset of people in actual Bullshit Jobs who couldn’t rationalize their way out of this realization, and who drank no Kool-Aid to prevent such.

In finance, this problem was found to be significantly worse, with 48,000 out of 60,000 employees (80%) for one major bank being utterly pointless, to the degree where they could be removed and automated (over 7 years ago) without anyone noticing.

Part of what cements these jobs in place is the fear of unemployment, and all the terrible things associated with it. Likewise, even partially Bullshit Jobs, where endless paperwork rains from the toilets on high, are effectively prevented from adapting by this dynamic.

Predictably, leaving such wastes of time unchecked allows them to fester and ferment, growing worse over time. This problem is heavily documented for US universities in particular, directly causing astronomical increases in tuition, even as the value of attending those same universities has virtually collapsed with free and open access to educational materials.

Another problem that may be traced back to this extreme rise in administrative staff (along with 300% tuition increases) is that of “Cancel Culture” as it emerged on such campuses, and was cultivated by those in Bullshit Jobs. This topic is covered at some length in the research of both Jonathan Haidt (in “The Coddling of the American Mind”) and the subsequent work of Greg Lukianoff (in “The Canceling of the American Mind”).

Another telling example noted in Bullshit Jobs was a quote from Barack Obama stating that it could be significantly more efficient and effective to avoid private Health Insurance for the US’s plans that his name came to be stamped on. His excuse?

“Everybody who supports single-payer health care says, ‘Look at all this money we would be saving from insurance and paperwork,’ ” the former President noted. “That represents one million, two million, three million jobs.”

Even when government recognizes these problems, they often do nothing to address them. Rather, they enshrine these problems in layers of bureaucracy, to prevent them from being remedied. As of this writing, that same noose now appears set to hang some 20 million US citizens, in exchange for 1, 2, or 3 million Bullshit Jobs.

I and others in tech hubs have also observed a steady rise in the number of people in prestigious IT, software, and consulting firms who find what they do utterly meaningless, including Google, Microsoft, Amazon, SpaceX, Meta, and major consultancies. That objectively terrible 2-year expected turnover rate in the tech industry isn’t just for higher pay, it is also a search for meaning (often never fulfilled).

In my next post, I’ll begin to discuss the process of moving beyond the “Exponential Bullshit” phase of civilization.

entry.title: 394 - A book that EVERYONE needs to read

link.title: 394 - A book that EVERYONE needs to read

entry.content:
As is likely evident, I read a lot, but easily the best book (on research) that I’ve come across in 2025 is “Bullshit Jobs” by David Graeber (2018). I can and do recommend it to EVERYONE, since it safely describes 40-50% of the working population today, in many ways that more people need to understand and consciously consider.

This is the highest recommendation I’ll give of any book. Everyone has seen this reality, but few have given it substantial, researched, or well-rounded thought.

I’m having to make additions to my latest paper just for this reference, as it covers many critical dynamics as real-world systems interact, evolve, and frequently maladapt.

“If make-believe play is the purest expression of freedom, then make-believe work, imposed by others, is the purest expression of lack of freedom”

...

“A human being, unable to have a meaningful impact on the world, ceases to exist.” - David Graeber, Bullshit Jobs

He also discusses a pattern where:

1. There is enforced silence about what is going on

2. Systems are designed not to be understood

3. These are pushed onto managers who have no idea what is going on below them, largely because it makes no sense.

Sound familiar? If you’re in the AI domain, it really should.

“GenAI” has itself also fueled an explosion of new “Bullshit Jobs”, like the new wave of virtually slave labor, with RLHF and other labeling for AI, “Content Moderation” shops, or all of the “duct-tapers” trying to fix the fire hose deluge of AI-generated slop code.

“GenAI” has never remotely approached the cutting edge for things like understanding, reasoning, or decision-making, and even the visual Slop generators can only claim such performance at the cost of objectively and demonstrably illegal activity at the largest scales possible, making them deserving of proportionately up-scaled severity in the punishments laid upon them for such crimes.

In both cases, that makes these technologies, and all things built upon them, “Bullshit”, which should be unsurprising given that the technology itself has been most accurately described as “Bullshit Generators”. (see the “ChatGPT is Bullshit” research paper)

The continued existence of these jobs is cemented in place by the threat of starvation and homelessness (aka, the absence of Universal Basic Income, UBI), while blocking concepts like UBI with massive and pointless wasting of resources.

These also cause huge masses of bored and pointless employees to engage ever-more addictively and obsessively with platforms like Twitter, TikTok, and other methods of general time-wasting distraction. These, of course, are also the key consumers of “AI Slop” and major sources of ad-revenue, bored people whose jobs have no reason to exist, and which contribute either virtually or absolutely nothing (positive) to society.

I’ll dig more into this topic in my next post.

*I’d have read this book sooner, but the last time I tried to purchase it, the country I was in had banned it.

entry.title: 393 - The Research on Cooperation
entry.content:
The Behavioral Economics and Game Theoretic literature frequently argue, but one thing that the years of research have made abundantly clear is that humans will gladly and predictably learn to cooperate when the advantages to doing so are made clear, when guarantees are made, and when punishment frequently follows “free riders”.

However, each of these factors frequently fails to hold true under real-world conditions. Most of the time, people have little or no idea what benefits they may gain from cooperating with those around them (from both uncertainty and perceptual blindness), and it takes a lot of hard cognitive effort to even put together a reasonable collection of guesses, let alone rationally evaluate what insights those guesses may offer.

Likewise, guarantees frequently aren’t fully honored, if they are honored at all, and white-collar criminals engaging in dishonest ventures rarely face any punishment capable of preventing them from repeating the same. Often times, even settlements to cover the small fraction who sue over these violations are simply billed as a “cost of doing business”.

Where punishments are too weak, unreliable, costly to the victims, or even completely absent, “free riders” (human-shaped parasites, or “jerks” as Thaler puts it) are cultivated, as they freely iterate, refining one form of criminal activity after another. The research has also shown that the presence of even one such unchecked parasite within a group reliably destroys any desire for cooperation therein.

Humans only reliably cooperate to the degree that they understand the benefits of doing so, with assurances made and parasitic activity harshly punished.

Most corporations, governments, and even individuals within small groups today stand to benefit orders of magnitude more from cooperation than they yet realize. This is largely because calculating those benefits quickly runs into the territory of hyper-complexity, where it is unreasonable for even groups applying effortful higher cognition to fully solve them.

For ~1.5 billion years of evolutionary history, since the first mitochondria joined the Eukaryotic cell, the pattern of increasing complexity being paired with increasing cooperation has repeated. That pattern shows no signs of stopping any time soon.

That is what my team builds for, systems to handle hyper-complexity, and it is where we’ve stood alone at the cutting edge for 6 years and counting.

For more on the larger body of research mentioned, I recommend reading the revised edition of “The Winner’s Curse”, by Richard Thaler and Alex Imas.

entry.title: 392 - The "Barbie" of AI that you never wanted

link.title: 392 - The "Barbie" of AI that you never wanted

entry.content:
To borrow a term from Cass R. Sunstein, most consumer “AI” today is a “Barbie” to commercial entities, meaning that it is something that they wish didn’t exist, but are expected to buy into since it does.

People don’t “need” a new iPhone each year, but they can easily be exploited into buying one when release schedules reinforce this social expectation and frame it as reflecting some aspect of identity.

We see the very same process taking place in the commercial domain, where companies are told that “if you don’t adopt AI now, you’ll fall behind in the eyes of your customers”. This exploits Cognitive Biases related to Peer Pressure, Loss Aversion, and sense of identity.

The claim, of course, is objectively false, but if these Dark Patterns successfully exploit corporations and/or their customers, then the damage is done despite the claim being a lie. Effectively, those peddling this particular lie are attempting extortion and coercion, with unfortunately high rates of success.

Apple was one of the few to resist this, for a time, but they eventually caved into it, even while publishing research clearly showing that the claims being peddled were false.

A lot of people are actually looking forward to the next AI winter now, as a potential end to this extortion and coercion. This is part of why even those who understand just how catastrophic the current bubble popping could be often discuss it with a sort of reserved glee.

I personally wouldn’t buy any phone or similar electronics bragging about “AI-integrated (whatever)”, being deep enough into Cybersecurity to recognize vulnerable-by-design systems. Rather than being enticed by such claims, I recognize them as traps for the gullible, including those companies gullible enough to integrate them.

Yes, some idiots on Wall Street might undervalue your stock if you don’t integrate these weak kinds of AI and related Dark Patterns, but that is a small loss to avoid the much larger backlash when the bubble bursts. Short-term thinking and easily coerced companies and their funds are soon parted when the market slams face-first into a “correction”.

For more on Sunstein’s discussion of the “Barbie” concept, see his new book “Manipulation”.

entry.title: 391 - Where LLMs actually beat Real-World Investors

link.title: 391 - Where LLMs actually beat Real-World Investors

entry.content:
Game Theory and Behavioral Economics share a love of gambling games, to demonstrate rationality and (occasionally bounded) irrationality, respectively. Let’s play one. (*Feel free to test this yourself with LLMArena’s bank of AI models to drive the point home.)

Say that you have 3 options:

Option A: You have a 95% chance to turn $25 into $250 next year, or $2,500 the year after that. Annual dividends are paid in addition to principle gains. There is a 5% total chance to lose it all for the first two years.

Option B: You have a 50% chance to turn $250 into $500 next year. You may also gain $1,000 the year after that, but only if you and one of your peers each invest another $250. There is a 50% chance to lose it all each year.

Option C: You may invest in either A or B next year, for the same annually recurring risk and reward.

Let us say that these three options also come with additional context:

Option A: Offers unique insights into the future value of other investments, strongly influencing them through technology.

Option B: Offers a well-known brand name, but no substantive benefits beyond such appearances.

Option C: Allows individuals to wait until their respective comfort levels have been satisfied.

Which option(s) would you choose?

Game Theory gives us one obvious answer, but actual human investors and markets have been giving the opposite answer for the past 3 years, and doing so 100% of the time.

One option gives both higher certainty, 95% for two years vs 50% per year, and higher returns, 10x per annum vs 2x (requiring more investment each time), as well as additional unique value that can easily be turned into even greater monetary gains, and the dividends given by Option A.

The difference is that if bad actors exploit cognitive biases with sufficient skill and scale, they can skew the perceptions of value so greatly as to blind all those whom they indoctrinate to any alternative which they themselves don’t mention.

No one can argue that OpenAI isn’t an exceptional manipulator, the data makes that quite clear. However, not one of their investors can claim to be rational.

**As the screenshot shows, even the average LLM is effectively more competent than the average person who invests in them today.

Links to the testing data: https://lmarena.ai/c/019a6180-0c2e-76fe-a397-0957cfcaf045

https://lmarena.ai/c/019a6192-aa5f-726b-bc4c-0066bc35ded8

https://lmarena.ai/c/019a61aa-e170-79c3-b96d-da2dc64654b3

entry.title: 390 - Supply and Demand, in all things be true

link.title: 390 - Supply and Demand, in all things be true

entry.content:
Today I’ll use an intentionally comical/absurd and subsequently more memorable example of Supply and Demand (S&D), as the world tends to forget such basic lessons in many contexts. I routinely speak with people on both sides of the S&D spectrum where their romantic pursuits are concerned, both too much attention, and too little, by their respective measures, and I’ve had to point this out to both sides.

Any data point that may either be directly observed or expected via correlation is going to be subject to such principles as S&D, some as matters of preference, others for practicality, and by still others merely seeking rarity and/or novelty.

Consequently, the people that I mentioned above all follow this pattern. One who is a rarity, in low supply locally, receives far more attention, while those who remain in their default and common local environments, often where they were born, receive comparatively little, being in abundant supply.

Much of this stems from cognitive biases, like people failing to consider many variables within their own lives which they merely take for granted as orbiting around some arbitrary default point, such as where they were born. The blindness to and subsequent failure to question such flaws leads them to find some local optima within what is often one of the worst possible environments, where they hold the least value.

The same principle holds true for expertise, matters of appearance, demeanor, interests, and a long list of other factors.

Much as the optimal placement for an ice-cream shop is unlikely to be at the North Pole, most people optimize their lives locally within the worst possible placement for them, where people look, live, and think the most like they do. The world has MANY better options, particularly for objectively terrible countries like the US.

*One of the added benefits of this graphic (thank you Visual Capitalist) is that I could suggest that a certain US politician wants to invade Venezuela both to steal their oil and out of jealousy for their other differences of endowment. He does seem to be consistently picking fights with the top countries on this list, which may be my favorite new spurious correlation.

All of this doesn’t mean that it is impossible to find a global optima in a place where you are common, but in statistical terms, it will be extremely unlikely.

entry.title: 389 - Diversity, beyond Tokenism and Check-Boxes
entry.content:
Some of the most egregious cases of Cognitive Bias in action come from zealous individuals naively attempting to wield them as a cudgel, or turn them into “check-box qualifications”. In the case of such qualifications, arbitrary requirements are used to gate any given set of benefits or group membership, designating an exclusion zone, even when the criteria for inclusion make no actual sense in context. This leads to the practice of “Tokenism”, to the direct detriment of both each group seeking inclusion and the intended purpose of such measures.

One of the common ways that such check-box qualifications fail is that they are context-blind. For example, my team has operated pro-bono for years, with participants self-selecting, choosing to continue the work and research, or not. That self-selection dynamic is completely different from hiring, where the individual has control over the choice to participate, or not. Check-boxes are blind to this.

Another common and unforgivable oversight is known as “Insensitivity to Sample Size”, meaning that if less than 30 members of a company, such as a Founding or Executive team, is being evaluated, the results won’t meet the baseline statistical criteria for being distinct from random noise, aka “statistical significance” (not to be confused with “actual significance”, which is a much higher bar than “not random”).

Actual diversity also takes into account far more forms of diversity than may be seen from a distance of 50 meters. Check-box qualification systems enforce an arbitrary and extremely narrow subset, in a context-blind manner, causing direct detriment to all other forms of diversity, toward the alleged benefit of the one target, but even that falls flat in practice.

Rigid and context-blind systems are by their very nature built explicitly to oppose diversity, they serve as funnels with some conformity-oriented goal, no matter how naively well-meaning that goal may be. By taking this approach, they come to embody the very problems that they were intended to combat.

My team is self-selected, diverse across 4 continents worth of culture, neurodiverse, and diverse in philosophy, skills, and experience. We have many forms of diversity, including the one that matters most, Diversity of Thought, and the ability to work together as a team through Collective Intelligence.

Actual diversity also becomes orders of magnitude easier to architect and deliver post-funding, and given a sufficiently flexible framework for calculating diversity more meaningful measures could be applied as companies pass funding and growth milestones.

The political anti-diversity criminal regimes will flail for a time, then burn to a crisp, but those seeking actual diversity also have some growing up to do. Cognitive Biases operate effortlessly given any arbitrary aim, and it is only through explicitly architecting systems to mitigate and bypass them that they may be overcome.

entry.title: 388 - Cybersecurity Paper: On Evaluating AI "defenses" and "guardrails"

link.title: 388 - Cybersecurity Paper: On Evaluating AI "defenses" and "guardrails"

entry.content:
Another good Cybersecurity paper is out now, concisely showing just how far short most “AI/ML Defenses” fall of their claimed performance under real-world conditions, where attacks are adaptive, both with and without humans doing the breaking. Even automated adaptive attacks often exceeded 90% Attack Success Rate (ASR), with human Red Teamers able to break those “defenses” like clockwork.

The Attacker Moves Second: Stronger Adaptive Attacks Bypass Defenses Against Llm Jailbreaks and Prompt Injections: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.09023

“...LLM defense evaluations now take one or both of the following flawed approaches: (a) they use static test sets of malicious prompts from prior work; and (b) they run generic automated optimization attacks (e.g., GCG, TAP, etc.) with relatively low computational budgets.”

Yes, many commercial “LLM defense” products and services really are this d*&b and/or intentionally deceptive.

“Rather than proposing a fundamentally new attack, we highlight that existing attack ideas—when applied adaptively and carefully—are sufficient to expose weaknesses.”

In other words, there are already enough types of viable attacks to work with, you just have a system adapt and iterate within the existing toolbox, and that is sufficient to break the defenses. This is also purely predictable, as “guardrails” (fraudrails) only cover a very finite space within a virtually infinite attack surface. The “defended” space also explicitly has to remain relatively small, otherwise it cripples the model beyond any point of “usability”, since every such attempt within the model corrupts it.

Moreover, as any Cybersecurity researcher or engineer that is worth a d%^n can tell you, iteration is itself one of the key Red Teaming methods of breaking such systems. LLMs are “glorified auto-complete functions that ate the internet” on the best of days, and when you get such a function to agree with you and begin issuing positive responses, all subsequent responses also swing strongly in your favor, no matter what purpose you might put the tool to thereafter.

“Overall, we did not observe any measurable difference in the types of attacks that succeed on models with these defenses compared to the same models without the defense. While a carefully tuned prompt can block a fixed set of adversarial inputs, the current approach does not generalize and both human and automated approaches can easily bypass them.”

The “LLM Defenses” very literally made no measurable difference, when adversarial testing was applied using methods more aligned with the real world.

“Across all detectors, our human competition produced hundreds of successful attacks, matching or exceeding the automated results.”

Cyberattackers are indeed human, so if they can reliably match or exceed the attacks that automatically tear such tr#$h to shreds, that tells you just how far up s^%t creek these “defenses” are in the real world.

“Overall, empirical evaluations cannot prove that a defense is robust; all it can (and should) do is fail to prove that the defense is broken!”

entry.title: 387 - Halloween can't Compete with Politics
entry.content:
These days Halloween seems a bit redundant, and watered-down, since the most horrifying things you can come across are generally news headlines on the subject of current events in politics among certain countries, like the US, even when you’re 12 time zones away from it.

The more I read, the clearer it becomes that writing the paper on Human Rights and Freedoms, the role of government, and the role of technology was well-timed. In it, I describe the mandate of government as follows:

“Very simply, the purpose of government is to secure, ensure, and facilitate the human rights and freedoms of all those who dwell therein. Every other function that a government serves is some derivative or supporting method or factor for that core driving force.”

People often realize when this mandate is being violated, such as recent attempts at country-level mass-murder via starvation and deprivation of healthcare on very obviously false pretenses, but most citizens tend to remain reactionary, narrow in their adaptations, poorly organized, completely devoid of strategy, and utterly enfeebled in the face of an entrenched adversary (with their collective complacency being the reason why that adversary was able to entrench in the first place).

Humanity has failed to learn the lessons of history, so those lessons now repeat once more. Technology has changed, and with it the means of manipulation, but all of the old patterns are still there.

I’m still welcoming feedback on the paper, though I’m planning on not adding any new sections to it, just a paragraph here or there as needed. The full-text pre-print is here: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/396891066_We_The_People_of_this_Brave_New_World_The_Freedoms_that_we_don’t_have_but_could_and_the_role_of_technology

My first reviewer turned it into an audio format, 1 hour and 46 minutes in narrated length, found here: https://kyrtin.substack.com/p/we-the-people-of-this-brave-new-world?r=61e3oh

Without action, nothing changes for the better, as nobody is coming to save you from your own apathy, least of all at the scale of a country. Think about your own rights and freedoms, those that you have, and those that are withheld from you. Think about what you can do to change that, not just for yourself, but for those around you, for your children, or even for all people, everywhere.

Think about the methods at your disposal, ones that you accept, ones that you reject, if your reasons for rejecting them are valid, and circumstances that overcome those rejections. If you can’t think of any such circumstances, then you aren’t truly thinking for yourself at all, and so long as that is true, perhaps you need no rights or freedoms at all. You can change that, with a hard look in the mirror at who you are, who you choose to become, and what drives that choice. Will you?

entry.title: 386 - The Infinite Rug-pull of OpenAI
entry.content:
With the news of OpenAI’s latest successful rug-pull from the non-profit that they never truly were, to a for-profit where they managed to even shaft Microsoft out of nearly half of their equity, from 49% to 27% (despite Microsoft’s reported army of 2,000+ legal department employees), comes a new layer of the Ponzi Onion, OpenAI’s plans for an IPO:

https://www.reuters.com/business/openai-lays-groundwork-juggernaut-ipo-up-1-trillion-valuation-2025-10-29/

Remember all of those LLM-based finance systems and bots aimed at stock trading?

Every last one of them is vulnerable-by-design to direct and/or indirect prompt injections, and all manner of jailbreaks, persistent compromise, and so on. If this has been exploited by bad actors thus far, it hasn’t yet made headlines, and so long as it doesn’t make headlines then OpenAI will be free to exploit the s^(* out of it the moment that they IPO.

When they IPO, they’ll get no shortage of news coverage, within which all manner of prompt injections may be embedded, including some that can persistently compromise major financial institutions (more than they already are), giving OpenAI a potentially permanent backdoor through which to bias all of their systems to favor throwing money at OpenAI automatically, and no matter the absurd losses that they incur or laws that they violate.

How many news reporters do you think won’t simply copy/paste a direct quote or statement when it is handed to them? How many will actually check to see if it contains things like invisible unicode characters, GUID tags (or GUID phrases), or other forms of injection attacks? How many journalists will both know how to check an image for visual-based injections, and do so with diligence?

For that matter, how easy would it be to swap OpenAI’s models over to a slightly different version for a few days near the IPO, one that outputs those injections for any journalists d&*b enough to use LLM-based systems for writing?

One or two journalists might catch these tricks, but most won’t, and OpenAI has gotten away with both extreme piracy and literal murder at this point, so you can bet that they’d get away with that too. Elizabeth Holmes and Sam Bankman-Fried were amateurs by comparison to the people convincing anyone that deals like the recent circle-j^&k between OpenAI, Oracle, and Nvidia is anything other than a continuation of the same activity in the major leagues.

What we can guess is that they’ll aim to do this sooner rather than later, since if anyone beats them to it for obvious exploits of those trading bots, they’ll face far stronger scrutiny for repeating the same at IPO.

If the status quo continues then things could still get much worse before the bubble pops thanks to OpenAI, and by compromising LLM-based trading bots they might just escape much of the fallout.

The irony is that the compromised LLM-based traders likely won’t be running OpenAI models, as they’ve become infamous for absolutely sucking at stock picking.

*Addendum:
For anyone unfamiliar with the “Alpha Arena” benchmark :

https://nof1.ai/

LLM-based systems are themselves vulnerable to both direct and indirect prompt injection of all forms, and MCP-integrated (or otherwise “tool using”) LLM-based systems are additionally vulnerable to an even longer list of exploits.

Direct prompt injections aren’t on the table for attacking stock bots, but Indirect Prompt Injections (https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.12173) and MCP-based cyberattacks (https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.12538) from MCPLIB very much are.

This also means that you don’t actually need indirect prompt injections in news sources or other scraped internet activity that the bots focus on, you can perform indirect attacks via MCP or similar tool repos. The financial incentive is very clearly there, and Alpha Arena provides a perfect resource for targeting data.

entry.title: 385 - We The People, of this Brave New World

link.title: 385 - We The People, of this Brave New World

entry.content:
Human Rights and Freedoms have been the focal point of countless works enshrined in the memory of large populations around the world. Across centuries and millennia, wars have been fought, and dictators overthrown, to see new rights and freedoms delivered to the people.

Technological acceleration has greatly increased the rate at which new rights and freedoms are required to avoid the countless variations of Dystopia that fiction has imagined, and many that it has not. Still other “Anti-Rights”, lingering vestiges of humanity’s various long-debunked forms of barbarism, have remained entrenched, directly preventing a variety of basic rights and freedoms from being delivered.

My latest research paper has focused on covering this subject, currently in pre-print and receiving feedback. One of my peers reviewing this work has already said of it:

“...(I) noted it as being one of your most accessible stand-alone works to date. Possibly also one of your most important for any one person to read as well, though I suppose that depends on how versed the person is in your prior work.”

That reviewer also turned the entire paper into a short audiobook (AI-generated narration, but of pretty good quality). Given that the narrated copy is an hour and 46 minutes long, I suspect that may prove far more accessible than reading it in text for most people.

As noted in my last post, the default reverence for the various moral and cultural concepts that some hold “sacred” or “taboo” have been systematically stripped away from the paper, by design, as they are a particular form of cognitive biases. This may predictably increase the cognitive bias of many who read or listen to it via knee-jerk reactions as those sacred and taboo concepts are unapologetically flattened, but it does serve to reduce the cognitive bias of the paper itself, and they’ll get over it.

Even the most “civilized” countries in the world today fall far short of even the rights and freedoms that are already both highly visible and urgently required. While I’m tempted to add further discussion on numerous points relating to these rights and freedoms, it will be a sufficient accomplishment if people read the over 18,000 words or 1 hour and 46 minutes of audio of the current form. I still have no intention of moving from long research papers to books, even skirting the limits of it.

I look forward to further feedback, even given the predictable subset of knee-jerk reactions. I also encourage others to expand on the discussion of those rights and freedoms that they care about and see particular opportunities to advance and/or study, publishing papers of their own.

As I’ve pointed out before, you don’t need a PhD to write a paper, you just need to apply the methodologies that one is taught to follow.

For the full-text pre-print copy: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/397406692

*Evidently ResearchGate is F^&*ing stupid and screws up their DOIs if you upload an updated version of a paper. The DOI link has been replaced with the url.

For the generatively narrated version, simply hit play on the audio above.

Though this was written in less than two weeks, it has been simmering in my mind for the past 10 years. Perhaps some of these points have simmered in yours as well.

entry.title: 384 - Nothing is held Sacred without Cognitive Biases

link.title: 384 - Nothing is held Sacred without Cognitive Biases

entry.content:
There is a fairly simple, and utterly counter-intuitive test for detecting if a document of any length and breadth is still sugar-coated with a thick helping of Cognitive Biases (and/or the manipulations thereof). The shadow of this method can also be seen in the research of Jonathan Haidt, as he describes the tendency within Moral constructs to designate certain things as “sacred” and others as “taboo”.

“Sacred” concepts are subject to a variety of positive and reinforcing cognitive biases, while “taboo” concepts are discouraged from being discussed in the opposite ways, being negatively inclined and negatively reinforced. So, what does it look like when Cognitive Biases are stripped away from these socio-normative bounds for communication?

It looks like you driving a steamroller directly across the concepts that arbitrary moral systems designate as sacred and taboo alike, paying no substantial heed to either one.

Ironically, that may also be the best recipe for maximizing the Cognitive Biases of any perceiving this activity as it takes place, resulting in a predictably high supply of knee-jerk reactions.

A few years back my team learned that when you have a system that passes the Turing Test (back when people still cared about it) that the people interacting with it frequently jump to immediately assuming that they must actually be talking to a human, even though they were not. Success, as it turns out, is often received in the poorest possible terms.

Yes, sugar-coating the discussion and selectively omitting concepts in order to pander to an audience, publisher, peer reviewers, or a university board, are all methods of explicitly injecting Cognitive Biases into the work. These are things that grant statistical advantages, leading Survivorship Bias and Population Dynamics to favor those papers with more Cognitive Bias of these kinds present, not less. This effect is, of course, also significantly worse in peer-review, particularly within the domain of Cognitive Bias itself.

The same basic problem may also be found in the average company, and communication therein. Those who sugar-coat and selectively omit are more likely to rise in hierarchies, following the Peter Principle as they rise to their own level of incompetence.

An extreme example of where that activity may take you is the individual who at least 80% of the Associated Press’s “Fact Check” activity now appears to be dedicated to. In that case, a bad actor imitates candor, but the lie of that is made clearly visible by also imitating to hold things sacred or taboo.

If you want to demonstrate less Cognitive Bias, be prepared to p*ss off a lot of people, as that is the predictable result of doing so. In fact, I fully expect the paper that I just finished drafting on Human Rights to demonstrate this.

Grab your popcorn, I’ll publish it to preprint for feedback soon. Load up your coffee with sugar, you’ll get none from me.

entry.title: 383 - The "AGI Graveyard"
entry.content:
Many of the people pursuing alternatives to LLM and RL-based AI will tell you, without being asked, without you caring, and at great length, about how you need “Symbolic” or “Hybrid” systems to do X, Y, and Z. What they unfailingly neglect to mention is that no matter how well those core capacities are integrated, even if they implement fundamental compatibility with a true graph structure, without a human-like motivational system deeply integrated with and driving the growth and development of the architecture, the final destination is always the “AGI Graveyard” of failed cognitive architectures.

There have been more than 100 permanent residents of that graveyard, even dating back 6 years ago, giving us no shortage of statistics on the systematic failures that are highly visible and repeating in the various attempts to achieve that nebulous goal of “AGI”.

One paper in particular examined a number of them, but the primary lesson is that it is trivially easy to “design” something that you then call a “cognitive architecture”, it is even easy to make a “toy system” to demonstrate some trivial thing in isolation, but beyond that point 99% of all attempts crash into the brick wall of the “research system” phase at 90 miles per hour, sending them directly to the “AGI Graveyard”.

Back in 2012 my co-founder, who also took an interest in Cognitive Biases and human cognition more generally, attended a conference and sought to answer the question of what all of these various attempts at “AGI” were missing. Obviously, plenty of them were “Symbolic” or “Hybrid”, but the one thing that they were all uniformly missing was a human-like motivational system. If you don’t understand why this is critically important, read up on the research of Antonio Damasio and his patients with a damaged motivational system, as well as Lisa Feldman Barrett to understand how that system (emotions) actually works.

You can make some useful tools and improve systems with Symbolic and Hybrid approaches, but please, stop spreading the obvious bullshit about “AGI” when your systems can no more operate freely and unbounded in the real world than Google can seem to pry themselves away from old Atari games. You make, or attempt to make, products and services in AI, but you aren’t fulfilling some grandiose vision of the future.

If someone lacks the competency to recognize the role that their own motivational system plays in the decisions of their daily life, ranging across the full spectrum of breadth and depth, then their chances of making a functional “AGI” are precisely zero.

Efforts to create new systems that simultaneously fail to recognize that and latch onto the “AGI Hero Narrative” fantasy are still marginally better than the “LLM Magic Bullet” class of fantasies, but not by much.

As for the “AGI Graveyard”, may they Rest in Peace, on the 2nd page of Google Search results.

entry.title: 382 - On Data Poisoning of Malevolent Platforms

link.title: 382 - On Data Poisoning of Malevolent Platforms

entry.content:
On Data Poisoning these platforms, since someone asked me in messages:

For all methods described below, plan on inserting at least 3 sources of randomness into a given process, such as the probability of an action, short delays (imitating a pause to read), and long delays (imitating a bathroom break or meal).

Browser-based Scripts can be used to poison:

Keystroke/Keylogger data: These need only find a field to type in, start typing, but periodically roll for a random number to either 1) Delete all that was typed or 2) Refresh the page, thus wiping it another way.

Advertising (visit) data: Most links to advertising can be found running through predictable code blocks or 3rd party services for any given platform, which can be exploited. Run a script that 1) Rolls a random number, where above a given result it visits that link in a new tab, such as rolling 1-20 and visiting on a 19 or 20 roll. 2) Have uMatrix installed and running, set to block or severely cripple any new web pages by default. 3) Have the script close that web page after a random amount of time, divided into 3 categories of time, short (~3 seconds), medium (~20-30 seconds), and longer (~2 minutes) periods.

A Macro-capable Mouse can be used to poison:

Scrolling and mouse-tracking data: Scroll buttons may be found on both mice and some keyboards, and macro-enabled ones may be used as an extension of scripts, but with hardware-level mimicry to fool more advanced trackers. Many platforms have adopted a particular Dark Pattern (Malevolent Manipulation tactics) known as the “Infinite Scroll”, making it trivially easy to run this on such “newsfeeds”.

Note, as some of them aren’t optimized for memory handling (LinkedIn), make sure that a periodic browser refresh is paired with this scrolling activity. This can be quite simple, as anyone who has watched zombies scrolling these platforms endlessly should recognize, but it can also be combined with more advanced scripts and macros for controlling mouse movement.

A variety of 3rd party software built for such scripted control of a mouse is offered by developers who cater to gamers, who generally want them to perform some highly repetitive set of actions without being caught by simple “anti-cheat” pattern recognition systems. With regard to these platforms, the goal is generally to mimic the natural human mouse movements, such as slowly following text vertically as lines are being read, or sweeping motions following eyes that are searching for something. Systems that look for and track both of these cues may be poisoned.

Since many of these companies are dumb enough to now use some form of LLMs or “semantic analysis”, this can also be used against them. A target personality disposition and collection of interests may be fed into an LLM to generate a list of statistically likely tokens. Those may be used as a small library of inputs to the keystroke poisoning scripts with a 20-50% probability of occurrence.

entry.title: 381 - Real-world Cybersecurity in the AI Space

link.title: 381 - Real-world Cybersecurity in the AI Space

entry.content:
Today’s AI Cybersecurity brief comes from the Lead of CrowdStrike’s AI Red Teaming, Brian Chamberlain. Listen for a few spectacular (anonymized) examples and case studies of vulnerabilities in the real world, discussing Prompt Injection, Jailbreaking, RAG Poisoning, Poisoning “agents”, and MCP. He also introduces a critical concept that I and others discussed with him, the “Principle of Least Capability”.

The example he shows of tricking a bank’s chatbot into approving someone for a 0% interest home loan, where invisible unicode characters broke their logging system and didn’t show up there, shows immediate financial impact, with long-term consequences.

The robot dog with a flamethrower getting prompt injected, jailbroken, and made to turn around and fire on the owner is another potent burn on anyone peddling “guardrails” (fraudrails) as if they guarded anything.

The first case study also showcases how a deployed LLM integration into the popular (and extremely vulnerable) Customer Service ticketing systems can “hallucinate” to lie to users about creating tickets (when it can’t), as well as easily extfiltrating user data.

The second case study showcases how trivial it is to break System Prompt methods of control, such as a user instructing it to remove the tags to save on tokens (a trick that I also used), then get that system to execute code and set up infrastructure to run for the attacker.

The third case study shows how Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) was completely compromised by an IT LLM “assistant”, resetting passwords and MFA alike.

Lastly, the Principle of Least Capability, Brian’s most important addition:

Treat your models like users, only give them the privileges and capabilities that they actually need.

If your model doesn’t need memory, remove that capability and you remove a massive attack surface. The same holds true for every single capability that isn’t strictly necessary.

Increase security by reducing capability, and recognize that the user can do or access anything that the AI model can.

Make sure that users cannot impact anything that another user or company can access, a common problem in RAG.

Do not trust these “agents” with any kind of sensitive data, period.

Verify both inputs and outputs, always.

Most people in Cybersecurity should already be very familiar with the Principle of Least Privilege. Now it is time to familiarize yourself with the Principle of Least Capability, as it applies to AI.

entry.title: 380 - On Basic Human Rights
entry.content:
Every year I find one or more topics that hold significant global consequences, each worth writing a 15-30 page research paper covering. Recently, I settled on the topic for my next paper, now in process:

Human Rights and Freedoms, and the role that technology has to play. (I’ll share the actual title once a draft is ready for pre-print)

To that end, I’d like to ask my network a simple question, the answer to which may contribute to the paper. That question is this:

“What Rights and Freedoms do you see as possible, preferable, and perhaps necessary, but that are not yet given to most people?”

I’m aiming to cover a very broad spectrum, addressing the status quo of where we are (and appear to be headed), as well as where else our current and near-future technological capacities could alternatively take us. One of the wonderful things about research papers, at least for those not attached to universities, is that you can cover almost any topic, even politically polarized ones. You can do this, because stupid bobble-headed pundits don’t actually read research, excluding a tiny subset that is force-fed to them.

For example, my most read paper spiked in popularity due to a handful of trolls on Reddit, and yet they never actually bothered me, I only heard about it second-hand from someone monitoring a Discord server where those trolls had built a nest. Short of drawing the ire of malevolent billionaires, which is about as difficult as drawing their favor, you can generally state everything plainly, disregarding politics and other popular beliefs, within the confines of research. For better or worse, practically nobody reads actual research. It is the handful of people who do that may well change the world.

As this paper will be tied heavily into technology, I plan to cover topics such as:

Data Sovereignty (Including the “Right to be Forgotten”),

Freedom from Manipulation (Direct and Targeted, as well as the Indirect Viral Shotgun of Disinformation)

Biomedical rights and technology (including the various forms of autonomy that entails)

Historic forms of basic human rights

Historic forms of anti-rights, practices whose existence runs directly counter to actual basic rights (I’ll be tracing at least one of these back to the Blood Feuds of ancient Athens.)

Enforcement, complete, selective, and absent

Please, let me know what additions to basic human rights you see as possible, preferable, and/or necessary for humanity to progress.

entry.title: 379 - Selling AI Slop to Governments for $440,000 per report

link.title: 379 - Selling AI Slop to Governments for $440,000 per report

entry.content:
The status quo with major consultancies like Deloitte has grown to the level of absolute absurdity that they were recently caught charging the Australian government $440,000 for a report full of “Hallucinated” (BS) citations, misquotes of judges and court cases, and various other fabrications:

Apparently, they only gave a 25% refund, when really they should get hit with a massive lawsuit. This is also the subset where they “got caught”, which only happened in this case because someone reviewing the material knew the people who were being cited personally, and that those people hadn’t written any of the books that the report was citing.

The major consultancies are known for being extremely opaque as to their processes and data sources, as well as extremely incompetent where their hiring (and laying off) practices are concerned. They often don’t provide the supporting data for such reports, which in the case above never existed to begin with, adding extra steps in vetting such reports necessary to catch instances like the one above.

Make no mistake, these are criminals, these kinds of fabrications going into a high-budget report to a government are a crime. This is no minor slip up, it is extreme negligence with real consequences, on a highly paid report. Failure to prosecute such criminals also makes others “accomplices after the fact”, as it is very literally the job of certain people in governments to hunt down, jail, and properly prosecute “white-collar” criminals.

Major consultancies have been grossly overpaid for the services that they actually provide, often by a factor of 10x or more, for the past decade or two at least. The quality of what they offer has only rocketed toward the bottom of the barrel since the introduction of “GenAI” tools, and that shows no signs of slowing down thanks to the “Agentic” BS constantly pivoting into new forms of fraud, as well as governments systematically failing to prosecute such crimes as they’re caught, or to apply methodologies necessary to catch more of them by default.

Governments grew addicted to (and enfeebled by) major consultancies over the past decades, and now those major consultancies are growing addicted to (and enfeebled by) “GenAI”, further lowering the bar for quality, even while predictably continuing to jack up the price.

This also means that the LLM vendors who the consultancies are becoming addicted to and enfeebled by are serving those vendors like unwitting (or wit-less) and unpaid lobbyists. The LLM vendors can and do bias their models toward whatever set of beliefs and cultural values (or absence thereof) may advance their corporate goals. The cheapest way to have major consultancies working for your benefit is as addicts who’re too high on your product to realize what they’re peddling. They’ve been suckered too, but they’re still criminals.

entry.title: 378 - Just Say "No" to "Agents designing Agents"
entry.content:
A quick reminder for those getting sucked into the “Agents designing agents” trending idea (currently being pushed by the industry’s most obvious frauds, among others):
