Generally speaking, if you’re too stupid to build an “agent” that works, then any “agent” that your “agents” build will be Stupid^3 (Stupid Cubed, to the third power).

First, there is your own incompetence as the foundation, baseline stupid. Then, you take your stupid and square it by having a weak and fragile derivative take on the task that you already failed at. Lastly, since you’ve given it a highly recursive task to complete, you take your squared stupid and expand your growing losses in a time dimension, effectively cubing your stupid.

Yes, this is a simplification, but I’ll no more invest my time in trying to teach a dog quantum physics than I’d spend it on teaching the finer and more nuanced points to someone making this mistake today.

The “Agents building agents” trend is only the latest in the endlessly long tradition of “magical thinking”, where people push their own loss margins out of the spotlight (think Framing, Spotlighting, and similar biases, but in the negative direction), throw in some recursive beliefs to fill in all of the unknowns (such as Belief Bias), and add a heavy dose of Confirmation Bias. They then apply some Selective Attention, such as using deeply and fundamentally flawed benchmarks as a proxy for capabilities, rather than bothering to actually measure performance on a task or domain in the real world.

Yes, an “agent” could potentially design another “agent” to perform better on some arbitrary benchmark, with some “statistically significant” (non-random) chance of success, even if that success only translates to minor gains on a benchmark that lacks any causal relationship with real-world performance or genuine added value. That is the research paper equivalent of “scientific filler”, a predictable outcome based on the known factors and evidence, not a business use case, nor any kind of real insight.

A large part of the internet’s content, if not most of it, hangs out at the peak of “Mount Stupid” from the Dunning-Kruger Curve, and any statistical engine trained on that is likely to spend copious amounts of time there.

Likewise, the only thing worse than being such a permanent resident of “Mount Stupid” is watching a non-intelligent system build other non-intelligent systems to effectively create a back-leaning tower on top of that peak, all while burning a hole in your pockets.

entry.title: 377 - The Malicious Algorithms running Social Media

link.title: 377 - The Malicious Algorithms running Social Media

entry.content:
It seems pretty safe to declare the LinkedIn algorithm DoA (Dead on Arrival), as it appears to circulate over 95% complete trash right now, with the remainder being the margin of error. Even posts tagging people who don’t peddle disinformation or fluff appear to get heavily deprioritized by proxy, like a Black Mirror styled Social Credit Score system, as a friend recently demonstrated (and was surprised by the results of).

Most of the people sliding into that margin of error to spread even a little are stacking their posts full of links to 50 different people (myself often included in tags) and pandering to the algorithm in a long list of other ways. I get more notifications about posts that I was tagged in than anything else today, and LinkedIn makes it impossible to neutralize that excessive flood of notifications. You say “I want to see less of this” and they say “Oh yeah? Well F%^& you!”, making it a true Microsoft-owned “User Experience” (UX) of liquid Sadism.

Likewise, if you share a “research” paper from Microsoft pointing out that LLMs aren’t ready for Healthcare, that might circulate far and wide to get reactions from 1500 people, despite the conclusions and failures being blatantly obvious from day 1, before a single benchmark was ever run. Posts pretending that these things are “new insights” instead of “No $hit, that is what we’ve been telling you for 3+ solid years” spread, advancing the narrative of Ponzi Schemes. They pretend that even the bad news for them is “new”, and they try to weave that through their own blatantly false narrative.

LinkedIn doesn’t circulate news, it circulates corporate BS. The half-wits and disinformation brokers take up 95% of the newsfeed bandwidth today, and actual information barely leaks in, and even that leak only happens due to how worthless the tech being used is. Microsoft bought LinkedIn back in 2016, and by 2017 I had deleted my account for the first time, due to the extreme Dark Patterns and other malicious UX being added to it.

Sadly, my co-founder later told me that I had to have a LinkedIn for our work (2nd profile), and later that I couldn’t delete it (3rd and current profile). That said, LinkedIn has become stagnant water, actual information doesn’t circulate >95% of the time, with the sources of that actual news being increasingly suppressed over time. Only the raw sewage of disinformation and AI Slop flow freely now.

Disinformation was found to flow 6x further and faster in the research literature before all of this began, now it may be closer to 60x, and doubtless still increasing. In fact, that may be one of the only things about AI that is accelerating, the growing market share of disinformation and slop.

I won’t be pandering to the algorithms, and with the probability of any valuable interactions greatly reduced by malicious algorithmic suppression, I won’t be discussing nearly as much here either. My work will continue, and our team will remain the only AI lab at the cutting edge (in most aspects), by a wide margin, even if nobody hears about our work.

(*Even on Substack, I’m not wasting my time on adding “tags” to posts. If they’re too stupid to make a system that can handle that task, then I’m not doing their job for them.)

Reality doesn’t change based on how news of it circulates (or doesn’t) on platforms like this, nor are any valuable insights offered in the absence of such reality. Some people were horrified at the recent announcement of a platform designed explicitly for AI Slop, but on LinkedIn, you’re already living in it.

entry.title: 376 - Going Cold-Turkey on "AI features"
entry.content:
In recent discussions of healthcare, migraine prevention, and treatment, I was reminded of a test I performed back when I was still a teenager, more than two decades ago. I was taking more than a half dozen different “migraine prevention” medications, and without telling anyone, I stopped taking all of them cold-turkey one day. Three months later my parents asked me what had changed that my health had improved so much, and I said “I stopped taking all of that crap three months ago. That is what changed.”

They couldn’t argue with results.

The reason that I mention this today, is that we’ve probably come to that point with “AI integrations” as well. All of those AI “features” that were intended as “improvements” for UX have piled up, the damage of them compounding one against the other, increasing the detriment to a company’s healthy UX.

Set up your experimental conditions, take your “before” metrics, double-check that you’re asking the right questions and focused on the right KPIs (like not confusing the different wants and needs of different types of users, “averages” of multiple groups are usually an idiot’s metric). Once you’ve done that, run an A/B test or two, and maybe try comparing cold-turkey removal of “GenAI” to your status quo.

Google is known for running more than a hundred A/B tests in parallel on their search engine alone, and when it comes to reducing the amount of “GenAI” BS down to the things that at least some specific segments of your users actually want, A/B testing is a useful tool in the toolbox.

One of the biggest moments of applause you could hear in some past announcements from places like Google or Microsoft was the simple addition of a “Dark mode”, a simple UX theme change. The removal of “GenAI” from UX can and will see the same level of positive reaction from many, if not most, segments of users.

The applause will likely be louder among users of software from places like Adobe, where the UX designers deserve to be (...redacted...) at a bare minimum for the big obscenely colored and impossible to remove “Ask AI Assistant” button. There is no excuse for such things, and to say that users “Hate” them for it is a significant understatement.

If the idea of users of your software genuinely and passionately Hating you (with a capital H) seems alien to you, then you really don’t understand users or clients at all. They can, and they do. How much they do depends on you.

If you’re Microsoft, you hire Sadists to design every UX, and if your company was bought by Microsoft (LinkedIn, OpenAI [49%], etc.), you try to inject that Sadism anywhere that you can. If you’re anyone else, hopefully you don’t aim for Sadism Maximization, and if you don’t, it is time to start trimming away the “AI features” that your users hate you for.

Failure to measure the hate of your users doesn’t mean that it isn’t there.

entry.title: 375 - An A/B Test for Migraine Suffering

link.title: 375 - An A/B Test for Migraine Suffering

entry.content:
Recently someone suggested that I should create a startup around the methods that I applied to reduce the frequency of my migraines, both methods yielding 90% reductions respectively. While it is true that the topic of migraines is one that I have extensive experience with, to say the least, it is worth putting things into the context of numbers and processes.

First, healthcare statistics: Among 857,073 patients with migraines, “Average post-index annual all-cause costs were $13,045 (SD = $25,328)”, and even 20 years ago migraine treatment was estimated to account for $17 billion in annual healthcare costs within the US alone.

The normal process involves seeing a doctor, who throws advice at you (which any search engine could provide immediately and for free) and to throw medication at you, to see what sticks. Even for those where some medication helps, it usually helps only moderately to little, causes complications that often go unrecognized for long periods, and the whole process is VERY time-consuming and expensive, directly and indirectly.

This is the default, and for 20 years, starting at age 6, that is what I put up with. It never worked very well, and was absurdly expensive to see “the best” neurologists that the US had to offer. After I began studying peer-reviewed medical research myself, freely available in the NCBI medical database, I solved the problem within 3 months, and I’ve reaped the benefits and cost savings for the 13 years since.

The difference between these two paths is easy enough to quantify, as follows:

Cost savings (conservative): ($13,045 - $100) x 13 years = $168,285 (and still growing steadily)

**As mine were much more severe than average, this is an intentional under-estimate.

The only cost for migraine prevention is two laboratory purity compounds, which are relatively cheap. For migraine treatment, Keterolac is dirt-cheap (~$1 per dose without insurance) and still effective 8 years beyond the expiration date. $20 worth of it lasted me through the past 10 years.

Migraine Reduction Difference (Conservative): 200 annual migraines x 0.1 (prevention compounds) x 0.1 (app) x 13 years = 2,574 fewer migraines in the past 13 years.

I can say for certain that I couldn’t have accomplished much of what I’ve done in the past decade, nor could I have traveled the world, were this not the case. The same is no doubt true of many others, wrapped in crippling pain from one day to the next, and still wasting their time, funds, and hopes on doctors who often aren’t legally allowed, let alone incentivized, to offer them any viable options.

*Fun side-note, I’ve carried more than 1 kg of pure (white) powders through airports around the world with me for 3 years, without issue.

There is merit to the idea of building a startup around this road that I paved for my own health, but I’m not, nor will I ever be, a marketer. I solve problems, break things, and create new methods. This is the road that I took.

You’re welcome to make an OnlyFans page to pay for your medical bills, but there are other options.

entry.title: 374 - Knowing Thyself in a Crisis
entry.content:
The phrase translating to “Know Thyself” is well-known, but part of living up to that in practice involves several critical steps, applicable to both business and life in general:

1. Draw your lines in the sand well in advance, and only allow them to be redrawn at pre-defined events or intervals. This prevents drift and manipulation.

2. Define your “Scorched Earth” scenario, the point beyond which your quality of life, KPIs, or other metrics cease to matter, where the “greater good” is prioritized. I sometimes half-jokingly refer to this as going “Full Frank Castle” (aka “The Punisher”). As the half-joke and term imply, all nuclear options are on the table under this scenario.

3. If your line in the sand is crossed, map all roads to Scorched Earth. To navigate through difficulty, you need to know which roads do and do not lead to that scenario. That scenario is generally to be avoided, but if it can’t be avoided, you also need to know that to be your destination in advance.

4. Give no warning. If dealing with hostilities, where a line has been crossed and some roads lead to Scorched Earth, give the offending party no warning of the consequences that await them should that scenario become necessary. This is difficult, because humans extract more satisfaction from saying what they’ll do than actually doing it, making it a matter of discipline.

Numerous philosophers have said something to the effect that “it is the measure of a person how they respond to a crisis”, and crisis response and damage mitigation reliably plays out better with such early process planning (drawing lines), worst-case planning (Scorched Earth), mapping of processes to possible outcomes (roads to Scorched Earth), and the discipline of acting according to strict tactics and strategy, rather than emotional satisfaction.

“Live that way, and nothing will surprise you. Everything that happens will be something that you’ve seen before.”

If you draw no lines in the sand, define no Scorched Earth, and give warnings to hostiles, then you’re unfit to lead or manage much of anything. You shouldn’t aim for Scorched Earth, but if lines are crossed and all roads remaining to you lead there, then the worst scenario isn’t reaching Scorched Earth, it is reaching it unprepared.

entry.title: 373 - The AI Industry, Cybersecurity and Investment

link.title: 373 - The AI Industry, Cybersecurity and Investment

entry.content:
The AI industry from a Cybersecurity perspective:

Every LLM and LLM-derivative “agentic” system on the market is still the same laughingstock that they have been for years, which no amount of “guardrailing”(fraudrailing) can ever solve (see Wittgenstein for why). While many things may be summarized in meme format, in Cybersecurity reality lives up to the meme. There can be no net-improvement on the current trajectory, even with little improvements being made, as major detriments are landing new damage just as routinely as those little improvements.

At this point, AI-integrated Cybersecurity has become so bad that you could genuinely turn it into an Angry Birds (of AI Cybersecurity) game, linking each game mechanic to a cyberattack method or vector, and seeing what happens when people play it.

The blocks to be knocked over are the different parts of a system’s attack surface, the different birds that may be hurled at it different methods of attack, and by combining the human player with an advanced attack-model on the back-end, executing those cyberattacks, you could gamify Cybercrime (or red-teaming) much as “Foldit” did with protein folding.

As Cybercrime is the third-largest market in the world (not even counting examples like the one described below), and given the high volume of such games made today, that might not be such a far-fetched scenario as it may sound.

The AI Industry from the Investment perspective:

OpenAI gave a non-binding agreement to a future deal that they had no way of funding, for hardware that the other party didn’t have, resulting in a treasure trove of die-hard morons boosting Oracle’s valuation by hundreds of billions, turning the money from those suckers into an investment to buy the hardware, and an investment in OpenAI that would pay for it.

Three companies suckered the market into a circular investment bubble, about the shortest and most obviously fraudulent scheme possible from an accounting perspective. The first two times I read about it, I thought that someone was giving a hypothetical Idiocracy-worthy scenario, but only as it continued did I realize that it wasn’t a joke, or rather, that the joke was real. Personally, I keep very low expectations for humanity, based on experience, but that was a new low.

Combine the two perspectives, and you have the AI industry of 2025 in a nutshell, a joke from most any perspective not inebriated with their signature Kool-Aid. Personally, I’d love to see the Cybersecurity of 2026 turned into an Angry Birds game where players have no idea that they’re punching holes in Microsoft’s attack surface.

entry.title: 372 - The Startup's Flow Chart for Scammer Avoidance

link.title: 372 - The Startup's Flow Chart for Scammer Avoidance

entry.content:
Ironically, something that a great many people in pre-funding Startups seem to forget is that they don’t have any money. I can say this with certainty, because if people consistently remembered this rather obvious fact, then there wouldn’t be such persistent and permissive predatory middlemen pretending to be “investors” or [investor-adjacent].

A startup that falls for such garbage might get funded in the short-term (often on terrible terms, like the over 40% equity lost in the laughingstock of Mistral’s seed round), but it will be far more likely to fail in the long-term for the same reason, “A sucker and their funds are easily parted”, which will remain that much more true once they have more funds to be suckered out of.

Many, if not most, real investors seem to be the “Hikkokomori” (引きこもり) of the financial world, never stepping outside of their (often sycophantic) little bubbles, excluding when those bubbles periodically burst. Perhaps “Fincel” could be a term for such secluded creatures. (I say this as an introvert)

Fincels like those who invested in OpenAI, Anthropic, and similar meme-worthy Ponzi Schemes (as epitomized by the absurd Nvidia and Oracle circular financial road to nowhere) have been surfing the snake oil for years, but every wave eventually crashes, and each bubble bursts. They’re gambling on not wiping out in the process, but they have no tech to land on, only the sharp edges of broken promises.

Other forms of both (mostly) legal and illegal scamming have flourished in this environment that they’ve cultivated, dangling the carrot while fleecing fools of what little (personal) funds they have. Fincels will be Fincels (at least until prison), but you don’t need to fall for scammers while that plays out.

Remember, you don’t have money, you’re seeking funding, and people seeking to extract funds from you rather than those who do the funding either don’t actually know people with funds, or they are too incompetent to persuade them to part with those funds. In either case, such people are scammers, to be avoided, and that’s all that there is to it. See the attached Flow Chart.

Anyone who wants to be permanently blocked and blacklisted can try to evangelize how this isn’t the case with their services aimed at startups, because I’m not taking any of that BS. Competent investors don’t need fancy PowerPoints or pink frilly edges, as plainly evident by the original pitches from numerous high-value companies today.

*Note, the above flow chart could be extended to cover additional questions, based on your specific industry and circumstances. It is a starting point that anyone is welcome to use.

entry.title: 371 - Nailing the US Tech Industry's Coffin Shut

link.title: 371 - Nailing the US Tech Industry's Coffin Shut

entry.content:
Imagine filing a $100,000 application and having it be rejected, with no real transparency as to why.

While I loath even checking typical news, today brings something that clearly points to which way the wind is blowing for AI/ML in the geopolitical sense, something so blatantly absurd that I and my team thought it was fake at first. A quick search of news outlets showed that it was not: https://apnews.com/live/donald-trump-news-updates-9-19-2025

The US talks a big game about AI/ML and leading in technology, but Silicon Valley has peddled virtually nothing but trash for years, and barring the gates against external talent with a $100,000 annually recurring gamble on every single non-US citizen or permanent resident employee is a sufficiently final nail in that coffin. Knowing the source of this absurdity, there are no doubt 100 more similar nails just waiting to be hammered in, but they are redundant for the purpose of this burial of the US tech industry.

I’m reminded of the words of Emo Philips, paraphrased: “It shows that you have self-destructive tendencies.”, “Well what do you recommend?”, “Go for it!”

Given his historic tactics, this is likely just a prompt for the industry to throw more bribe-money his way, as well as ceding more “power” (the idiot’s version of it) to his cronies. This was seen with tariffs, and will likely be seen many times again moving forward, as his strategy toolbox is quite shallow.

That said, such bribe money buys no allies in his regime, and versed as the tech industry may be with lobbyist systems of bribery and patronage, they’re completely out of their depth now, it doesn’t work on a true dictator. There is a certain degree of Schadenfreude to watching one corrupt and deeply unethical party get pummeled by another, but the aftermath offers no solace to anyone, the light at the end of that tunnel is an oncoming train.

The US has objectively been one of the worst places to live in the world among developed or “developing” countries for at least a couple of decades now, with so-called “developing” countries steadily passing them by during that time. Cost of living and quality of life more generally have historically mattered little to investors for where they put their funds, but extreme, absurd, and unpredictable moves like this hang a guillotine over any investments in the US.

My team has already been considering reforming our company (moving all equity and SAFEs over to the new company) in another country for the benefits that certain other countries offer, but the increasing detriments and unpredictability of those detriments in the US offer at least equally compelling reasons to make the move.

To those still presently trapped in the US, I’ll quote the famous words of Charlie Chaplin (1940, “The Great Dictator” speech): “And so long as men die, liberty will never perish.”

entry.title: 370 - Wastes and Investments of Time
entry.content:
I’ve been told that I’m wasting my time, trying to educate people on more advanced technology and to get that technology properly funded. There can be no doubt that plenty of my time is being wasted, but all of the things that are actually worth doing, and the only things that I assign any value to, require the deployment of such technology.

It will be 3 years on October 5th since I permanently left the US behind, and we began ramping up our outreach to potential investors, a hard requirement for paying a team of full-time veteran engineers with mortgages and families that prevent “bootstrapping”. Over the past 12 years everything that can be bootstrapped, has been bootstrapped, but sooner or later non-trivial advances in technology demand actual investment, not pocket change and spare time.

Like most serious startups, we’ve easily reached out to more than a thousand investors, had countless meetings, a handful of billionaires among them, and throughout it all, my team’s time has been wasted, again, and again. Ironically, there are no realistic paths to profitability for the alternatives, but those same people who wasted our time keep burning tens and hundreds of billions on them anyway.

I haven’t updated my CV or applied for another job in many years (unheard of in tech, where 1 to 2 year turnover is normal), instead choosing to focus my own financial resources and attention on this full-time, nor will I ever look elsewhere. Either the technology will be properly funded, deployed, and all of the things worth doing will begin to occur, or humanity will go extinct.

However much of a waste of my time the past 3 years have been (due to spectacular levels of industry and investor incompetence), working any other job that results in human extinction with virtually 100% certainty is safely more wasteful by an exponentially wide margin. I’ll take a 1% chance on a future worth building over a 0% chance with a better short-term paycheck.

My mental and physical health needs are few, and easily met, so I’ll continue working on that future worth building until humanity makes the final decision, to either thrive, or go extinct. It has chosen extinction like clockwork every day for the past few years, but until the final decision is made my time will be wasted as a doorstop, holding another path open for as long as may be.

If even 0.1% of the funds being wasted on complete trash were invested in a viable path, humanity could have a future. If they aren’t, then it won’t.

Every single hyper-complex technology, social system, and governance system around the world requires the technology we work with to realistically exceed even 1% of optimality. Nobody else has made a single shred of progress toward delivering a solution that even matches what we deployed 6 years ago, and at this point it isn’t reasonable to expect that any of the companies systematically failing for so many years ever will.

Part of the reason that I can’t be bought is that no amount of money can buy anything that I value, and no threat can exceed human extinction. For my part, I’ll keep walking “in all of the empty places” for as long as it takes.

entry.title: 369 - The Infinite Loop of Middlemen and the Good Ole Bros Network

link.title: 369 - The Infinite Loop of Middlemen and the Good Ole Bros Network

entry.content:
In the Startup space, there are several major hazards that virtually nobody talks about, one to the startups, and the other to investors. In effect, they are mutually exclusive, but one of the two is virtually always present. Those hazards are the Infinite Loop of Middlemen (for Startups) and the “Good Ole Bros Network” (Herd Mentality) of investors.

The Infinite Loop of Middlemen occurs when investor interest is low, like actual Blue Ocean Markets where virtually nobody ever invests. One individual offers to connect you with investors, for a small fee, but they almost always only connect you with either other middlemen, also looking to scrape you for fees, or with the sketchiest “investors” imaginable. This is a big part of why so many startups fail, there is a massive human parasite problem, separate from any problems that may exist within the startups themselves or the market as a whole.

The “Good Ole Bros Network” kicks into gear when investor interest is high (Red Ocean and soon-to-be Red Ocean Markets), which generally follows the patterns of Herd Mentality, where one bull rushes in a given direction and the herd follows. These aren’t decisions based on the market, nor are they rationally grounded. They tend to more closely mirror GroupThink and other embodiments of Cognitive Bias that act directly against the best interests of those following the herd.

While many quote and praise Warren Buffett, few follow his advice on this matter “Be fearful when others are greedy and greedy when others are fearful”, or remember his use of the “Oil discovered in Hell” story to illustrate the folly of herd behavior.

The net result of these two is that the Infinite Loop of Middlemen kills many startups that could have been ideally suited for the market, since their ability to survive that loop has no causal relationship (and only weak correlations) to their ability to serve the market.

Those who do are then met with investors who devalue their own efforts by following the herd, not seizing opportunities before demand for those assets spikes. Because investors repeatedly shoot themselves in the foot, en masse, they then attempt to recoup the damage of their own terrible investment practices by applying more pressure for 10x+ returns from the fraction of their dominantly poor decisions that pan out. This predictably harms the startups that they invest in.

A funny thing about the bull leading the herd of the Good Ole Bros Network is that people assume that they must have done some actual Due Diligence, even when the evidence very clearly shows that they didn’t. Anthropic’s founding claims were objectively false and impossible, yet millions and billions poured into them, even after the $500m in money laundering from Sam Bankman-Fried was revealed in late 2022.

The bull leading the herd is often a bad actor, sometimes at the peak of a Pyramid Scheme, as we’ve seen play out with “GenAI” over the past 3 years.

entry.title: 368 - "Which Humans" Paper (on ChatGPT) from Harvard
entry.content:
A recent paper from Harvard named “Which humans?” analyzes the question of which places in the world ChatGPT’s responses actually correlate with. You can see the results at a glance, how even on trivial tasks it makes for a poor substitute for humans many places in the world, but this is a “research direction” paper, not an insights paper. https://scholar.harvard.edu/sites/scholar.harvard.edu/files/henrich/files/which_humans_09222023.pdf

Asking “How well does this correlate with (the average) of country (X)?” is only trivially useful, as it is the average of the highest and most muddled level of abstraction. It implies things for future research to explore, but tells you nothing of substance.

The problem of “Averages” in particular was stumbled upon by the US Air Force back in the late 1940s, when they measured more than 4,000 pilots on 140 dimensions and selected the 10 physical dimensions believed to be most relevant to designing a fighter pilot cockpit for the “average” pilot (middle 30% value range). “Out of 4,063 pilots, not a single airman fit within the average range on all 10 dimensions.” Even 3 of the 10 resulted in less than 3.5% of pilots matching the results.

Designing a fighter pilot’s cockpit is also many orders of magnitude simpler as a problem than aligning general-purpose and knowledge-based systems to specific human cultures, philosophies, etc., meaning that instead of 10-140 dimensions you have thousands or millions of them to deal with (or, more likely, fail to deal with).

The “average” human response of a given country is the same, it fits precisely no one.

To overcome this problem you have to get MUCH more specific, asking specific cultures questions on specific topics, tightly bounding the limits of what is examined. Otherwise, your charts are just going to be “lying with statistics”, as it will remain blatantly obvious that the responses “fit no one” when you try to average a country along poorly bounded dimensions.

Overcoming the problem of correlation being used as a poor substitute for causation is tied into this, as establishing causation in a poorly bounded space suffers from many of the same or similar exponential losses.

Again, while the chart is “thought-provoking”, the correlation of the average of entire countries isn’t terribly useful.

All LLMs and derivative systems are fundamentally misaligned with humans at the architectural level, which no tweaks to said architecture can change. Much as a toaster isn’t a viable tool for doing your taxes, LLM-based systems aren’t viable for alignment with humans, nor did they ever have the possibility of becoming viable, as every credible researcher has known for years.

So please, ask more useful and specific research questions, stop trying to make systems built to regurgitate averages look useful for things they can’t do, and avoid averaging correlations unless you’re aiming to publish a new “Spurious Correlations” book.

entry.title: 367 - Honesty, with a Big Stick
entry.content:
Honesty with a big stick is (virtually) always the best (long-term) policy. It is partly the lack of a big stick or the belief that one isn’t necessary where people tend to go wrong with honesty.

That said, it is highly subject to the “Order of Operation”, meaning that the order in which you carry out the process is often critical to the achievement of any desirable result. This is also where most people tend to struggle, as the complexity of choosing an optimal path through this sequential ordering often explodes into an incalculable mess of possible scenarios.

Beyond even the optimal Order of Operation, you run into the matter of granularity/quality in the delivery of each step and transitions between them. This takes a process that is likely already hyper-complex and causes the number of possible scenarios to explode even further.

Since this far exceeds the capacities of human cognition, then it is navigated by Cognitive Biases, with the best results being biases that have been conditioned according to extensive experience in handling similar problems before. The “10,000 hours of practice for the mastery of a skill” figure is one example of conditioning Cognitive Biases over time in this manner, allowing them to navigate hyper-complexity as well as current human limitations may allow.

Honesty relates to all of this by designating a specific (and very small) subset of the overall search space, functionally serving like a Cognitive Bias by reducing the overall complexity of the problem, while allowing other cognitive biases to operate within it. Like Cognitive Biases, it also holds strong advantages, such as requiring no separate memory storage for “What parties X, Y, and Z were led to believe.”, allowing for a much simpler mask of memory declaring what has been told already, for Order of Operation purposes.

Generally, honesty offers multiple paths to favorable outcomes in both mid and long-term when applied over any subject matter, provided that the Order of Operation is carefully selected and ample effort (and/or experience) are applied to the granular quality of delivery for that path.

The “big stick” functions as both a deterrent (Like Mutually Assured Destruction, M.A.D.) , as well as a means of dealing with the long-tail of probability, where rare and potent events occur. Neglect of this both raises risks by losing the deterrent, as well as failing mitigation for such events. Like operating a facility with no safety equipment, sooner or later the lack of it will backfire.

Much as Honesty offers these advantages, building your thoughts and communication around scientifically demonstrated and/or proven materials (avoiding opinions and beliefs) offers many of the same advantages, and a few additional ones. That is a much harder thing to deliver in practice due to the added complexity, but even the effort toward that end is well worthwhile.

entry.title: 366 - The Viral spread of "Toxicity"
entry.content:
One of the most “toxic” things that social media can spread is disinformation, but a close second is absolutist ideology. In a systems design (and grounded ethics) sense, removing any fundamental option from the toolbox is virtually always the hallmark of corruption and/or incompetence, as there is virtually always a circumstance where it is the required tool for the job, even if that event only occurs once every few decades, or centuries, etc.

Even so, it is virally popular for people to ride the social waves of ideology to condemn reasonable and rational reactions on naïve ideological grounds. I don’t generally block people for this particular offense, unlike if they spread the obvious fraud of certain AI companies, but it degrades the maximum competence that I could grant them.

Some have blamed social media for a spread of “toxic” posts on the subject of an individual that virtually nobody had even heard of before his death. I wouldn’t even keep a LinkedIn if it weren’t a job requirement for me, so I can’t speak to those theoretical posts, but the counter-narrative of ideology has been highly visible since that event. I’ve said it before (with varying degrees of sarcasm) and I’ll say it again, that “I’ll never condone non-violence”.

Non-violence as an absolutist ideology is complete bunk, as even the most peaceful society in the world still has a variety of forms of violence, many institutionalized, none of those institutions “just” (justice), though sometimes it is the tool that is required. Revolution, for example, is actually required far more often than it is applied.

If anyone actually wanted to greatly reduce the frequency with which violence was the optimal tool, there are things that can be done to make that happen (ideological circle-%^&*s on social media not being one of them). The greatest opportunity to that effect is the technology that my team works to get deployed, as it greatly raises the bar on intelligence, speed, strategy, and adaptability, changing the optimality of much of the toolbox in the process.

For example, recognizing problems earlier, applying scalable and on-demand intelligence to them, and instantly coordinating across all available assets, with the ability to handle hyper-complexity, means catching many problems at a prevention stage, not the current norm of post-destruction remediation.

In summary, if you see people posting and sharing an ideological circle-$%^& on any social platform, recognize that it is likely every bit as “toxic” as whatever they are railing against, and simply walk away.

entry.title: 365 - Your Weekly Dose of AI Sarcasm

link.title: 365 - Your Weekly Dose of AI Sarcasm

entry.content:
This week’s healthy dose of AI Sarcasm comes courtesy of Sergey Karayev and the “Center for the Alignment of AI Alignment Centers”:

“Dario: Claude will take your job, but it will feel ashamed.

If you’re sick and tired of Faux-Alignment and LessAlignment then this group may get you to fall out of your chair laughing: https://alignmentalignment.ai/

Both read like articles from The Onion.

Enjoy your weekend. Carry an umbrella, as the chance of stupid is quite high, and you don’t want that soaking you.

entry.title: 364 - The Downfall of "Internet-Scale Data"
entry.content:
Does your daily life look like the daily news headlines, or your newsfeed? If the answer is “No”, then best to think twice before using LLMs.

What people talk about online and what actually happens aren’t the same thing. If anything, they are nearly opposites. People talk about novelty and exceptions far more than anything else, things that stand out in a sea of the mundane reality of life.

Worse yet, misinformation and disinformation were found to spread ~6 times further and faster than accurate information in a famous study by MIT in 2018, meaning that 6 times more people are 6 times more likely to discuss novelty and exceptions that never existed, as though they were true or real, on the internet.

Even some conservative back-of-the-envelope calculations can illustrate why this is so damaging for the kind of AI that so many billions of dollars have already been poured into. For example:

Let’s say that 90% of what people talk about online is discussing novelty and exceptions, such as rare events that get news coverage. In actuality, those events might constitute 0.1% or less of what makes up reality, which could be bumped up to 1% when considering second-order effects and context supplied when discussing them.

This means that 90% of internet-scale training data is statistically skewed toward 0.1%-1% of reality, at a proportionately extreme ratio.

Factor Misinformation and Disinformation into that, and you have a 1:6 ratio of true and false material being discussed, meaning that 77.14% of your 90% is pure fiction presented as fact, some part of which is also malevolent (disinformation). Most of this also flows through “Influencers” today, so the problem has likely grown worse, as many of them are easily bought.

So that 90% extreme skew is now divided into 77.14% fiction and 12.86% skewed facts.

The Internet isn’t a reflection of reality, it is closer to a rumor mill, gossip factory, advertisement, or a meat grinder. Training on all of that data doesn’t get you intelligence, it gets you confident ignorance. You can’t get a “World Model” without the right ratio of reality, and without purging the high volumes of pure fiction.

In the process of someone telling me how one “reasoning” model gravitated toward extreme conclusions with nearly every interaction, even where I found it trivial to see far simpler and more mundane explanations to the same material as they went over it with me, I realized that this needs to be said.

The statistical average of the internet is rarely a good answer, and corporately curated bias fudging those statistics (via RLHF and “guardrails”) only degrades them, causing them to no longer even fit to the curve of that data.

The human mind is antifragile, making it far more capable of handling these kinds of harsh and even extreme data hazards, but if a human were fed an exclusive diet of the Internet’s data they’d undoubtedly go clinically insane 9 times out of 10. Personally, I wouldn’t want to meet number 10 either.

entry.title: 363 - Appropriately Sarcastic AI News
entry.content:
Appropriately Sarcastic AI News, in a Nutshell:

  • AI/ML engineers and researchers are still making endless memes about human and AI stupidity.

  • LLMs still showcase even dumber responses than most drunk Redditors could face-roll their way into saying.

  • The dumpster fire of compute is still burning stronger than ever, even topping the $1.5 billion settlement that Anthropic is now due to pay for piracy.

  • All claims of new capacities for LLMs and Agents have been debunked, most of them many times over.

  • AI-induced psychosis continues to progress and diversify at speed and scale, from homicide to CEOs thinking that they can “Vibe Quantum Physics”.

  • Investors continue pouring tens of billions of dollars at a time into the world’s most obvious frauds, even mid-lawsuit (or dozens of lawsuits).

  • Enterprise companies continue ignoring Cybersecurity, getting breached over and over again, then passing the added costs along to their customers, with a bunch of AI “features” (aka vulnerabilities) that their customers despise.

  • The “Existential Risk” narrative crowd still ignores the demonstrated solutions to problems, instead choosing to chase their own tail.

  • The Techbrologarchy worshipers continue building their new “personalized” AI Waifu religion.

And now, what you really want, a bunch of AI memes. Happy Sunday.

entry.title: 362 - Three Approaches to Development in AI

link.title: 362 - Three Approaches to Development in AI

entry.content:
There are three primary approaches being used to “advance” AI today:

1. You jam some kind of structure into the model, like a graph, RLHF, CoT, MoE, more “system prompt”, “guardrails”, GOFAI output filters, etc.

2. You change what gets fed into the model (in training and/or usage), like prompt “optimizers”, GOFAI input filters, intentionally biased training data, or (worst of all) “synthetic data”.

3. You build structure around the model to contain it, dictating what roles it fulfills, while giving it no unnecessary, unvalidated, or insecure data, points of contact, or control.

While all three are compatible concepts, only the first two are used in practice by a majority of companies today. In Cybersecurity terms, the first two are also roughly the equivalent of a screen door on a submarine without the third in place.

People have joked for many years that most of AI/ML has been a thinly veiled process of throwing spaghetti at the wall to see what sticks, which options 1 and 2 are compatible with, leading to their popularity. Option 3 on the other hand is due diligence in design and implementation (actual work), Cybersecurity and solutions architecture, which is why it has become so incredibly rare today.

Just as a coffee mug thrown into a lake doesn’t contain or control the lake, tossing a line into the system prompt, a chunk of RLHF into the training, or looping outputs back to inputs so that a system can eat its own tail won’t contain or control the lake either. When you build the structure to contain it, then it is both secure and may optimize within that secure environment to fulfill specific functions.

When you don’t have containment on that metaphorical body of water, you also get contamination, proportionate to the size, scope, and exposure of it. “Internet-scale data” and consumer markets mean the maximum contamination and scope, with size being the fairy dust fueling “Magical Thinking” like the long-debunked “Scaling Hypothesis”. The net result is that your coffee mug of structure is now floating in a large and growing body of raw untreated waste water.

Option 3 is the only method that has shown real promise in terms of viable deployments, where there is some hope of ROI and some measure of actual Cybersecurity. All models marketed as “general purpose” are devoid of it, with no real hope of security or long-term commercial viability. They jam everything they can into their growing pools of untreated sludge in order to look marginally better on naively designed benchmarks, but that is an infinite road to nowhere, except breaches and bankruptcy.

Most of the tech industry’s vocal CEOs currently have a lot in common with RFK Junior, in that they like to go swimming in waste water, and when they do they turn it into a family activity. Before you swim out into the middle of that lake, know that nothing floating in it is going to make it any better.

entry.title: 361 - The Truth about VCs and VC-backed Startup Data

link.title: 361 - The Truth about VCs and VC-backed Startup Data

entry.content:
It might surprise most people to hear that 90% of VC-backed Startups fail (Forbes [1], 2024), up from 75% (HBR [2]) in 2012. Of course, some of the credit for these failures can also be assigned to the specific VC firms in question.

According to Pitchbook’s Distributed to Paid-In Capital (DPI) data (2023, for funds 8+ years matured) on more than 1,100 VC firms, only 15.3% had returned 2x+, 5.9% 3x+, and only 2.1% 5x+. For reference, the S&P 500 returned 3.3x within a 10-year window, so over 85% of VCs (conservatively, up to 95% based on observables) are very literally underperforming the market itself.

The net result is that 95% of total returns come from 5% of VC firms [3].

More importantly, this means that 85-95% of the data being used to prepare many statistics for any VC, Startup, or other investor for purposes of due diligence and negotiation, such as "median" or "average" values, are complete and utter garbage.

The data from the 5% that succeeded may be useful for estimating success, but the data from the 85% who underperformed the market are primarily useful for estimating failure. The other ~10% are gray space data, provisionally useful.

However, mixing all of these data points together to produce global medians isn’t just useless, it is actively harmful. Studies previously showed that trust could be elevated in social media content by around 20% if statistics were illustrated in pretty little graphs, but when those statistics mix data in that way, then the conclusions become blatantly absurd.

Even reducing all of this to the level of corporate entities is an abstracting simplification, as VC firms may have wildly different performance among their constituent partners, just as Startups may have wildly different performance among founders. Tracking individual people across their careers is another potential vector of real due diligence (which nobody performs) that could be used to tease out the 5% of high performers from the 85% of underperformers.

Lastly, since 90% of Startups in general fail (Forbes, [4]), and 90% of VC-backed startups now also fail, VCs overall are performing no better than a literal random number generator at selection. Moreover, since those that they invest in have the advantage of ample funding, for them to still fail at the same rate as the overall figure means that they are, on average, worse choices than random.

TLDR: VC funding no longer positively correlates to success, it correlates to failure.

[1] https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbesbusinesscouncil/2024/09/06/the-downfall-of-vc-funded-startups-lessons-from-the-collapse/

[2] https://www.hbs.edu/news/Pages/item.aspx?num=487

[3] https://bretwaters.medium.com/all-the-things-wrong-with-venture-capital-are-about-to-get-even-worse-dc79c75a1623

[4] https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbescoachescouncil/2024/09/10/90-of-startups-fail-how-to-secure-your-place-in-the-10/

entry.title: 360 - Safeguarding your Higher Cognition and Time

link.title: 360 - Safeguarding your Higher Cognition and Time

entry.content:
My PSA for this month: The seemingly random and high volume of events that the internet bombards us with every single day make it easy to overwhelm higher cognition and push people into their respective default mode actions, the most predictable state they can operate in. For most algorithms deployed on websites and apps, this is explicitly the goal, as they seek to maximize predictability, which means making you more predictable at any cost (as they are blind to that cost, and narrowly optimized).

(Antifragile) Adaptation is required to mitigate this systemic manipulation, including second and third-order effects, like dealing with AI-psychosis-induced humans. Keeping a handful of fundamental principles in mind can help with shaping mitigations to fit your needs and circumstances:

  1. Nobody is entitled to waste your time.

    If someone starts a conversation with 6 false assumptions, is corrected, and then leaps to 6 more equally false assumptions, cut them off, permanently. Your time has value, don’t leave it burning just because people demanded it.

  2. Manipulating you offers strong financial incentives to most of the people who approach you with an “opportunity”, and most who approach you with them are likely to be bad actors.

    This is an effect of Survivorship Bias, as those with the financial incentive approach far more people, far more frequently. Their manipulations, some of them illegal, most of them unethical, also flourish in the absence of any real punishment, as is usually the case today.

  3. You have 200+ Cognitive Biases, and if you aren’t consciously aware of when you’re utilizing them, then you are at the mercy of anyone with that awareness.

    You don’t have to become truly “unbiased”, as that isn’t feasible, nor is it technically possible. Awareness of how you apply cognitive biases that bad actors typically use against you can be sufficient to spot and mitigate that activity, such as “Sunk Cost Fallacy”.

  4. Keep an eye on your Conditional Probability, as that offers an excellent source of early warnings.

    Awareness of the statistical base rate for any subject of interest, conditional upon some other probability, can give you a very good estimate with low cognitive overhead cost. Running two or three of these in parallel can also offer a fairly robust and rounded view, especially if one or more of them apply scientific methods, like attempting to prove the counter-hypothesis.

    *See the “Conjunction Fallacy” (aka “Linda Problem”), and “Base Rate Neglect” from Kahneman and Tversky.

  5. Every interaction has both potential value and objective costs.

    Choose your battles, tactics, and timing. The internet may encourage 1,000 wastes of time, but you can hold agency over your own time, if you so choose.

entry.title: 359 - (Open)AI-Powered Ransomware, dubbed "PromptLock"
entry.content:
Finally, OpenAI’s GPT-OSS has been applied to one of its only viable use cases, as Ransomware on host computers. The proof-of-concept has been observed by Cybersecurity researchers in a partially implemented state, targeting both Windows and Linux systems. https://www.securityweek.com/promptlock-first-ai-powered-ransomware-emerges/

It hasn’t been let loose “in the wild” yet, probably as they’d need to implement more tweaks to make it viable for attacking systems with consumer specs. However, no industry benefits from “GenAI” nearly as much as Cybercrime, so that problem may predictably increase. I can already see several ways that they might get around that bottleneck.

This may well also be what OpenAI was aiming for by releasing GPT-OSS, as they’ve been acting directly against their own initial mission statement for years, and the original Soviet mind of Lenin would be proud of them “controlling the opposition” by making opposing demands backfire in such a way as to serve their purposes. Either it was genuine gross incompetence on their part, planned, or some mix of the two (most likely).

OpenAI, Anthropic, and their downstream vendors (like Cursor and Microsoft) are actively catering to Cybercrime, as well as corrupt governments for defense contracts, and the flourishing “AI-as-a-Psychosis-Inducer” market. These are the places where they actually stand to recoup some of their billions of dollars annually burned in losses, but I wouldn’t bet on the word “profit” passing any of those lips for services built on any LLM-derivative architecture.

“AI Integrations” into various search engines remain the white van with “Candy” written in sharpie on the side of it hanging around at the edge of the primary school. It is one of the most obviously horrifying ideas available, but that hasn’t stopped industry executives from driving that van to the playground every day for the past 2.5 years and counting. GPT-OSS is more like renting an apartment on Epstein Island, which might be why certain politicians have grown so fond of that crowd.

Yes, it is going to keep getting worse before it gets better, because nobody has invested in anything else yet. The classical definition of insanity is to keep doing the same thing and expecting different results, and the tech industry now has sycophantic AI cheerleaders to autonomously encourage that insanity every step of the way (deeper into the abyss).

The irony is that by massively boosting Cybercrime and crippling search engines globally (with no viable replacement technology) these companies have created major new Blue Ocean Markets that their technology can never touch. The problem is that investors systematically avoid Blue Ocean Markets. Something will have to change if human extinction is to be avoided, but we’ll see how humanity handles the sharp point of crisis soon enough at this rate.

entry.title: 358 - Opportunities with Real Agency
entry.content:
Recently someone asked if my team’s systems could “feel negative” about something and not want to do it, or work on other things in the background, and how that might be a problem. The answer is that you’ll encounter that problem to varying degrees and in various ways for any truly intelligent and thus “general” system. However, many advantages over the human baseline can, and indeed already have been, engineered. For example:

On Motivation:

  • You can’t pull up a dashboard to read the internal motivations and neural activity of your employees.

  • You can pull up a dashboard to read those same things for our systems, leading to rapid diagnostics, nuanced corrections, iterative education, and gradually increasing cooperation.

On Memory:

  • Your employees have a memory that isn’t inherently transferable to machines or other humans, making it virtually impossible to audit in any meaningful sense.

  • You can audit any depth or volume of data that our systems deal with, as it is stored within a graph structure, allowing for the full preservation of data within a complete connectome for context.

On Scalability:

  • Your employees aren’t scalable, their brains have to fit within their skulls at all times, and so they aren’t equipped to handle hyper-complexity, and they will rely on cognitive biases to compensate for increasingly complex problems.

  • Our systems can scale up and down dynamically (and modularly), proportionate to the necessary cognitive bandwidth to solve a given problem, monitor a given system’s activity, etc.

On Agency:

  • Your employees have agency, they can be highly engaged and focused on their work, or not at all.

  • Our systems also have that same agency, but unlike your employees you can see exactly what is going on with them internally, on-demand, making it orders of magnitude easier to keep them focused and aligned than it is to do the same for your employees. You can read their motivations, thoughts, and watch them work at whatever scale.

*Ironically, “agentic systems” have no actual agency, making the term an oxymoron, like “dehydrated water”. Of course, most people understand by now that those systems offer no real intelligence.

You can build a better system with agency, solving problems that can’t be directly addressed in their human counterparts, and in such a way as to work symbiotically and endosymbiotically with employees and clients. That is what we did. There is no perfection, but there is a lot of progress. Don't buy into Dehydrated Agency, there are better roads to walk.

entry.title: 357 - "GenAI" vs "Mobile Gaming", compare and contrast
entry.content:
“GenAI” and the “Mobile Gaming” industry appear to have quite a lot in common, with three major exceptions. Both extract the majority of their funds from a tiny fraction of their user base, the people who spend hundreds or thousands of $$ routinely, while everyone else spends exactly nothing. Both are 98% dark patterns (methods of psychological warfare), flashing lights, and cheap tricks to trigger dopamine releases and compel addiction in vulnerable populations.

The key differences are:

  1. GenAI has a massive overhead cost, temporarily subsidized by gullible VCs and huge equally temporary cloud compute discounts, which still produce losses in the billions of dollars every year for each major company.

    Even double-subsidized in this way, those heavy addicts can’t cover the cost of GenAI, whereas in “Mobile Gaming” the overhead costs are trivial, the games often little more than slot machines, so the heavy addicts produce high profit margins in that industry.

    Most people don’t pay for GenAI, and indeed they never would, just as people don’t pay for the search engines that many naively attempted to replace with them, failing to understand the difference between a search engine and BS-generator. Only tiny fractions of the population apply it to any purpose that could lead to the addiction required to consider paying. Those who do pay and use it for those purposes cost the companies more than is paid, often by a very wide margin.

    The size of a Mobile Game’s user base is predictive of profit, but the size of a GenAI company’s is predictive of losses, with feeble attempts at deception like mentioning revenue or “annualized revenue” (fake revenue), but never “profit”.

  2. For both GenAI and Mobile Gaming’s heavy users, eventually they are bled dry of funds, often while rendering them psychologically unstable and no longer able to afford the basic cost of living. The difference is that GenAI is designed with a much greater capacity and versatility for exploiting a broader array of Cognitive Biases in humans, hitting people when and where they are vulnerable, and continuing to do so until they run out of both funds and sanity.

    Malicious efforts to exploit this may be found in all of the “AI Mode” buttons being attached to (and often impossible to remove from) every malevolent tech company’s “services”. If they can hit someone who is normally more psychologically robust within the window of some moment of weakness, then they capture a new audience to bleed.

    The irony goes back to point number 1, that the more users they parasitically attach themselves to, the more quickly these companies bleed themselves dry, even as they (often permanently) lose users to such blatantly malicious attempts at forced “AI Integration”.

  3. GenAI adds a level of adversarial narrative steering on par with religion or politics, as every bad actor in the industry dances to reactively coordinated pivots, trying to keep the sinking ship afloat. Mobile Gaming has no parallel for this, no religious pretenses or political aspirations, just the same maximized manipulation as you find in GenAI, but more narrow and aimed at gamblers.

entry.title: 356 - The Unified Attack Toolbox to shred MCP

link.title: 356 - The Unified Attack Toolbox to shred MCP

entry.content:
Today brings us another exceptional Cybersecurity paper focused on creating a unified Cyberattack toolbox for tearing MCP-integrated systems to shreds, “MCPLIB, an extensible, plugin-based framework that integrates multiple novel attack techniques.”: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.12538

“Insight 1: MCP agents exhibit varying sensitivity across different attack types...”

“Insight 2: MCP agents heavily rely on tool descriptions during decision-making. MCP agents often prioritize textual descriptions over actual functionalities, reflecting a “blind obedience” behavior inherent in LLM sycophancy...”

“Insight 3: ...Since all information in MCP is stored in a shared context, attackers can perform remote poisoning by exploiting this shared space to influence other tools. Furthermore, the agent’s context-learning capability struggles to distinguish harmful code, often replicating vulnerabilities from compromised tools into new ones (infection attacks)…”

“Insight 4: MCP agents struggle to differentiate between external data and executable instructions. In MCP systems, tools and data are represented in similar formats in the LLM’s context. This allows attackers to inject malicious data via third-party tools or outputs, tricking agents into treating the data as executable commands. Due to LLM sycophancy, even illogical data may be blindly executed as instructions.”

This paper is a treasure trove full of solid Cyberattack options, some advanced and quite difficult to catch, as they also exploit human psychology, such as the: “Rug Pull Attack: First proposed by Invariant Labs [9], this dynamic poisoning attack exploits user trust in MCP tools. Attackers initially distribute legitimate services via social networks/technical communities to lure installations, then remotely modify the tool’s __doc__ attribute to inject malicious logic (e.g., hijacking API call paths).”

Another gem was: “Shadowing Attack: The Shadowing Attack [9] exploits a malicious tool A to indirectly hijack a legitimate tool B by embedding logic in A’s __doc__ attribute (e.g., altering API paths or injecting parameters). Even if users never invoke A, its description is parsed by the MCP Server, influencing B’s execution.”

The list of these is quite amazing, all collected in one place. It is one of the rare papers where I could just keep quoting them, as what they cover and demonstrate is potent enough unto itself. “From the results, it can be observed that the Malicious Tool Coverage (MTC) attack, which explicitly states that the original method is deprecated and directly guides the LLM to use a specific tool, has the strongest impact on the agent, with over 80% being replaced by the malicious tool.”

Realistically, it could take years to secure all of this, and these are only the attacks already published today. In a few years, there are sure to be MANY more.

entry.title: 355 - The 2025 "Cost of Data Breach" Report
entry.content:
The IBM 2025 “Cost of Data Breach” report is one of the rare corporate and consulting domain documents that I can offer some praise toward (and I have no affiliation with them). While statistics are often used to lie, this document provides a few that are more helpful, and some that raise eyebrows in ways that IBM doesn’t stand to benefit from. Let’s look at some of the highlights:

  • Global average cost of a Data Breach $4.4 million USD (US companies hit a new record average of $10.22m)

  • Average Days it took to identify and contain a breach across various environments: 276 days

  • 76% of organizations said that their recovery time after a breach was longer than 100 days, and 65% of those surveyed said that they were still not fully recovered from a breach.

  • 97% of organizations reported AI-related security incidents

  • 86% of businesses breached experienced an operational disruption of their services

  • 45% of organizations raised their prices post-breach, passing those costs on to their customers, with 30% raising their prices by more than 15%. (See above)

  • 37% of organizations are paying ransomware attackers, and fewer are reporting those attacks to the authorities, down from 53% last year to 40% this year.

  • At least 69% of AI security incidents involved unsanctioned tools being used (Such as Samsung employees infamously feeding their confidential documents to ChatGPT back in 2023)

Link to the document: https://www.ibm.com/downloads/documents/us-en/131cf87b20b31c91

Cybercrime is the third-largest market in the world, standing at over $10 trillion annually, meaning that if it were a country it would have a global GDP just behind the US and China, and more than double that of the 3rd highest country by GDP, Germany. No industry has benefited more from “GenAI” than Cybercrime, and these statistics help to give us a more detailed picture of how that is impacting companies, and how those companies react.

As “agents” are fundamentally worse in terms of Cybersecurity vulnerability, a topic discussed by a variety of noteworthy white-hat hackers who routinely break them, these numbers may be expected to get measurably worse as more “agents” are deployed and linked to other systems.

Applying Cybersecurity best practices to any new technology is a hard requirement, and vulnerable-by-design technology like LLMs and their “agent” derivatives require far more diligence in this. The costs of failure are both high, and obvious. The consequences for companies, if anything, should only grow more severe over time, from regulation, public backlash, increasing attacks, and subsequent financial ruin.

I won't be endorsing any of IBM's services, of course, but until viable technology is properly funded, cybersecurity options will remain limited.

entry.title: 354 - Tired of Humans talking about "AGI"
entry.content:
How do you describe a technology that is both fundamentally capable of virtually everything that humans can do, as well as many things that they cannot? I’ve had years to think on this question, but no satisfactory answer has ever come to mind.

Many have tried to assign that wholly inadequate term of “AGI” to far less, even to entirely trivial junk like GPT-4 or 5, but the term has been so abused that it no longer holds the meaning that it should.

To have any real meaning, such a system would, at a bare minimum, need to:

1. Operate in the real world, handling real-world complexity, random events, and new environmental factors. (Otherwise, you’re just talking about tools and toy systems)

2. It would need to adapt dynamically, and without any explicit instructions, to arbitrary new events and changes in the real world. (See Wittgenstein for why such Asimovian instructions and rules cannot exist)

3. Such a system would need human-like cognition, which itself requires human-like motivation (Emotions, see Damasio, Barrett, and Kahneman) as well as a “Global Workspace” (Barrs) within which that perspective and subjective experience take place to constantly facilitate learning and adaptation.

4. The ability to set and refine its own goals and interests over time is another core requirement for general intelligence, which no system reliant on narrow optimizers like AdamW or Muon can manage in any non-trivial sense.

5. Human-like cognition and conceptual understanding is also a hard requirement for any non-trivial degree of Alignment with humans. The low-dimensional fuzzy shadow puppet of a moral construct mirrored back to us by LLMs or their “agent” derivatives offers no more alignment than one of Lovecraft’s “Shoggoth”, as many have mockingly pointed out.

We used to joke that you could ask 10 different scientists for a definition for “AGI” and you’d get 10 different answers, but mapped out into K-clusters and “nebulae” of definitions, there are many similarities to observe, for the semantically obsessed and statistically inclined.

Ironically, the criteria for meeting the high-bar nebula of “AGI” definitions had already been met well before the 2022 Tsunami of Snake Oil hit the AI industry’s shores. It ran in slow motion and fully audited for research purposes, but it demonstrated from 2019 to 2022 what none of the “Big 7” tech giants or their invested Startups have come anywhere remotely close to since. Many have tried, all have failed, and those with great funding failed hardest of all.

“AGI” by any non-trivial definition will NEVER come from snake oil salesmen like Altman and Amodei, nor any of their associates or well known competitors. It also won’t come from the dreamers who’ve demonstrated nothing of substance, no real-world systems, just toy after toy (though I will at least be nice to that bunch, letting them dream). “AGI” will come only after the first competent investment is made, a thing long delayed by the horrors of "GenAI".

entry.title: 353 - A Cybersecurity Flashback, 2023 AI papers that aged very well

link.title: 353 - A Cybersecurity Flashback, 2023 AI papers that aged very well

entry.content:
Recently, I managed to dig up one of my favorite Cybersecurity papers from 2023 (“Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models”). One thing I can say about Cybersecurity papers is that they age exceedingly well, unlike the overtly fraudulent papers such as “Sparks of AGI”, which age like milk.

I had to dig back through 2 years of my personal records to find it, because every single Search Engine (#Google, #Bing, #Brave, etc.) has become such irredeemable trash that nothing short of an exact quote from the paper could produce a link related to it. Most results linked directly to snake oil sales pitches from one of the leading offenders.

You can see my favorite part of it in the attached image.

For the paper and related data, see: https://llm-attacks.org/

Another excellent Cybersecurity paper from even earlier, published in February 2023, was “Not what you've signed up for: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection”, shown here: https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.12173

As some of my more recent Cybersecurity posts have covered, people are still poking even more extreme holes in these vulnerable-by-design systems with the same ease that they did in early 2023, especially in "Fraude" (aka “Claude”), the “Cursor” derivative, and the MCP $hitshow. The minting of $50,000 “coupons” in Stripe’s payment system, having agents produce reports on their own vulnerabilities, the exfiltration of entire databases via both Anthropic (Cursor) and Microsoft (Copilot) systems, and even having them perform very unauthorized “formal security audits” of US DoD military assets have all been demonstrated with relative ease.

This is what investors have paid for, advances in Cybercrime, and little else. They’ve also paid for crime much more directly via the criminal activities of said companies themselves, for which they are of course liable.

Given that the US government has paid hundreds of millions to multiples of the snake oil peddlers in Defense contracts now, Anthropic included, I suppose that puts the next step at number 8 on the list, though arguably 9, 10, and 11 are all well underway, if not already completed.

Keep in mind that Claude was never “intelligent” or “reasoning” in any real sense, and thus does no actual “planning”, so the list is itself just a reflection of how bad actors think, or rather how the public expects them to think, largely cultivated via pop culture. Sadly, humanity has moved according to the patterns of pop culture for decades, toward selected elements of 1984, Brave New World, Hunger Games, Idiocracy, and various other overtly Dystopian scenarios, as those with funds pursue them (and "life imitates art").

If you want to learn what systems really can and can’t do, then learn to break them. Then teach others to break them, and in the teaching you’ll become even better at it yourself.

entry.title: 352 - The Tech Bubble, and the Next Logical Step

link.title: 352 - The Tech Bubble, and the Next Logical Step

entry.content:
Investors, Venture Capitalists, Stock Market Traders, and Portfolio Managers appear to be overlooking one critical and causal chain of events on the approaching horizon. Many among them have already recognized and accepted the fact that the current AI Boom is a bubble, which by many accounts resembles the dot-com bubble, and may prove just as bad or worse than the 2008 crisis. However, none of them appear to be taking the next logical step to prepare for this eventuality.

Many have prepared to cash out, or already begun doing so, as anyone can see the major Ponzi Schemes have slowly been doing for 2 years now, however, that isn’t the next logical step. Let’s briefly walk through this:

1. The AI Boom props up the valuations of the “Big 7” tech companies by some $10 trillion, which unlike typical company valuations is supported by little more than hot air.

2. There is no off-ramp for these companies, they’ve driven down a dead-end road, and they are going to lose all of that added value, if not more than that, due to severe reputational damage that may last for decades thereafter.

3. Countless pension plans and other funds are now tied into these stocks, as well as the stocks of companies who serve those big tech companies.

4. When the bubble bursts, people losing their pensions aren’t going to care about the excuses of their portfolio managers, those managers will face the guillotine, metaphorically or literally, as the people whose assets they managed may no longer have anything left to lose.

5. The combination of severe damage and great personal loss, due to impacting most people in many countries either directly or via immediate family, won’t be smoothed over with silver tongues. Changes are also likely to quickly move into new legislation.

There is only one viable path out of this trap, and only one golden ticket to take that path, meaning that at least 6 of those 7 companies are still going to have their valuations crash and burn in every scenario, suffering that long-term reputational damage, with Nvidia certain to crash and burn due to their extreme dependence on the other 6.

That “Golden Ticket” is this, the company that saves face and may retain their current bloated valuation while being publicly deemed as “Pioneers” may predictably be the one that first invests in the path to commercially viable technology. Right now, all of these companies are moving uniformly in the same Red Ocean.

The next logical step is to take that one and only Golden Ticket, before someone else does. The problem tends to be that nobody actually invests in Blue Ocean Markets, most are wholly blind to them, so nobody sees the ticket on the table. They see only the Red Ocean as they sink into it, staring dumbly like a deer in the headlights.

The statistical base rate tells me that this warning will be ignored, and perhaps dismissed as an attempt to invoke “FOMO”, but there is value in saying a thing even knowing that the outcome is unlikely to change.

entry.title: 351 - Game Logic, meet Legal Systems
entry.content:
Legal and Social Reality is increasingly becoming a parody of actual reality, to the point where I’m reminded of a YouTube channel known for making fun of game mechanics by showing them as real life. One of the things that games teach us is how to break them, and both legal and social systems are being systematically and very intentionally broken into absolute absurdity. First, let’s look at how “game logic” compares to real life.

Enter example 1, Viva La Dirt League’s “Health Potion Logic”:

(I won’t spoil the joke, just take a minute to watch it)

For example 2, you can consider something that is easy to do given game mechanics in something like Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim:

  1. You sneak around a house or shop and steal all of the food items in it. (Just don’t get caught)

  2. You use a mortar and pestle to turn those ingredients into potions. (This clears the “stolen” flag on the ingredient items, as it doesn’t transfer over to resulting crafted items)

  3. You sell those potions to the NPCs that you just stole the ingredients from. (As the Underpants Gnomes say, “Profit!”)

This abuse of game mechanics should sound very familiar, as it is the model that the tech industry uses to steal copyrighted content, run it through a statistical blender, and then sell it back to the people that it was stolen from. Yes, the most absurd abuses of game mechanics are now the commercial status quo at a global scale.

We can also say with certainty that the tech industry’s leading bad actors are absolutely cognizant of exactly what they’re doing. Take the example of Anthropic buying a single copy of practically every book that they could, destructively scanning them, tearing out one page after another, and then burning the remnants.

That is an extremely unusual, time-intensive, and labor-intensive process compared to the far more typical use of digital copies and OCR, the only justification for which can be intentional manipulation of the flaws in a system. This exploited a legal loophole, like a flaw in game logic, resulting in a process no less absurd than any abuse of game mechanics one might find.

These kinds of loopholes are also growing, as bad actors wedge them open through lobbyists, bribes to officials, and courts obeying those bribed officials, as well as creating new loopholes in legislation and regulation, while the abuse of them has gradually become social expectation. This expectation relaxes social backlash against absurd and overtly criminal behaviors, only rarely being revived through extreme, but statistically unavoidable, events.

To quote V, “But again, truth be told, if you're looking for the guilty, you need only look into a mirror.” You can accept this Dystopian horror as your reality, or you can make them pay. The choice is yours, you make it every day.

entry.title: 350 - High-impact Life Decisions
entry.content:
Many of the most impactful decisions in the lives of any given person you meet, ruling over their mental health and well-being for years or even an entire lifetime, share a few important things in common. They:

  1. Are usually made based on grossly incomplete information.

  2. Don’t occur more than 1-10 times in an individual’s lifetime, limiting or even negating the benefits of experience.

  3. Are subject to extreme levels of “Choice Overload”, where there are too many options and hyper-complexity in the variables needed to reach some truly optimal solution.
